2.play: KING HENRY IV, formerly Henry Bolingbroke

3.play: PRINCE HAL, Prince of Wales and heir to the throne (also called Harry and Harry Monmouth)

4.play: LORD JOHN OF LANCASTER, younger son of King Henry

9.play: EARL OF NORTHUMBERLAND, Henry Percy, Hotspur's father

10.play: EARL OF WORCESTER, Thomas Percy, Hotspur’s uncle

13.play: OWEN GLENDOWER, a Welsh lord, father of Lady Mortimer

16.play: SIR MICHAEL, a priest or knight associated with the archbishop

17.play: SIR RICHARD VERNON, an English knight

19.play: POINS (also called Edward, Yedward, and Ned)

23.play: HOSTESS of the tavern (also called Mistress Quickly)

24.play: VINTNER, or keeper of the tavern

1.play: [Enter KING HENRY, LORD JOHN OF LANCASTER, the EARL of WESTMORELAND, SIR WALTER BLUNT, and others]

2.play: KING HENRY IV: So shaken as we are, so wan with care,
notes: wan] COLLIER (Notes & Emend., 1852–3): An alteration, but a questionable improvement [see Textual Notes], by the Corrector of the Folio, 1632.—SINGER (Text of Sh., 1853): Here, at least, Mr. Collier is candid; and tacitly confesses that the authority of the Corrector is not sufficient to sanction any interference with the text which is not a self-evident improvement.

3.play: Find we a time for frighted peace to pant,

4.play: And breathe short-winded accents of new broils

5.play: To be commenced in strands afar remote.
notes: stronds] Dyce (ed. 1857): Malone, and some other editors retain the old spelling, stronds, though in The Merchant of Venice, I, i, 171, they print Colchos strand. In early books we frequently meet with passages where the word is spelled strond and yet pronounced strand, e.g., The Taming of the Shrew, I, i, 164, 5: “That made great Jove to humble him to her hand, / When with his knees he kist the Cretan strond.”

6.play: No more the thirsty entrance of this soil
notes: I would read: the thirsty entrants, i.e., those that set foot on this kingdom through thirst of power. Whoever is accustomed to the old copies of this author will generally find the words occurents, consequents, ingredients, spelt occurrence, consequence, ingredience. By her lips Shakespeare may have meant the lips of Peace—[STEEVENS also admits the possibility of THEOBALD'S interpretation, which CAPELL (Notes, 1770) adopts—Ed.]—MALONE (Suppl. to Var. ’78, 1780). Mr. Steevens’s conjecture is so likely to be true that I have no doubt about the propriety of introducing it into the text. It should be observed that supposing these copies to have been made out by the ear, ... the transcriber might easily have been deceived, for entrance and entrants have nearly the same sound ... A similar mistake has happened in the first scene of Henry V, where we meet in the first folio: “With such a heady currance scowering faults” instead of “With such a heady current scowring faults.” ...—RITSON (Remarks, 1783): Never, sure, was there so much drumbling, nor (except in this edition of 1778) so many wild and absurd conjectures as this simple passage has given rise to. For so simple it certainly is, as that the little meaning it has may be easily discovered by the most ignorant, however doctors may differ about it. ... Everyone talks familiarly about Mother Earth, and they who live upon her face may be, without impropriety, be called her children. The allusion is to the Barons’ Wars—MASON (Comments, 1785): By Erinny [see Textual Notes] is meant the Fury of Discord. The Erinnyes of the soil may be considered as an uncommon mode of expression ... but it is justified by a passage in the second Aeneid of Vergil, where Aeneas calls Helen—Trojae et patriae communis Erinny. An expression somewhat similar occurs in 1 Henry VI [IV, vii, vii]: “Is Talbot slain? the Frenchmen’s only scourge, / Your kingdom’s terror and black Nemesis?” It is evident that the words “her own children,” “her fields,” etc., must refer to “this soil”; and that Shakespeare in this place, as in others, uses the personal pronoun, her instead of its.—[DAVIES (Dram. Misc., 1785) commends the ingenuity of Johnson and Steevens, but prefers the simpler explanation offered by Theobald.—MALONE (ed. 1790) has lost confidence in entrants, and believes that if there is no corruption in the text, Theobald’s interpretation is correct, though Shakespeare here speaks “licentiously.”—MALONE (note in Steevens’s ed. of 1793) quotes in support of Theobald’s interpretation Genesis iv, 2: “Thou art cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother’s blood.”—ED.]—STEEVENS (ed. 1793): Mr. Mason’s conjecture (which I prefer to any other) may receive support from N. Ling’s Epistle prefixed to Wits Commonwealth, 1598: “I know there is nothing in the world but is subject to the Erinnys of ill-disposed persons.” ... Shakespeare could not have designed to open his play with a speech the fifth line of which is obscure. All that is wanted in this emergency seems to be a just and striking personification, or rather, a proper name. ... The name furnished by Mr. Mason may be safely admitted into our text, as it affords a natural introduction to the train of imagery that succeeds. ... I subjoin that Erinny was inserted ... with the deliberate approbation of Dr. Farmer.—SEYMOUR (Remarks,

7.play: Shall daub her lips with her own children's blood;
notes: dawbe] THEOBALD (ed. 1733): The sense of the passage, the antithesis, requires the reading damp.—WARBURTON (ed. 1747): Trempe, from the French tremper, properly signifies the moistness made by rain. [N. E. D., 1926, does not recognize trempe as English.—ED.]—EDWARDS (Canons of Crit., 1748): Why must Shakespeare thus continually be made to use improper French.

8.play: Nor more shall trenching war channel her fields,
notes: trenching] Elton (ed. 1889): “Trench-digging,” not “encroaching.”

9.play: Nor bruise her flowerets with the armed hoofs

10.play: Of hostile paces: those opposed eyes,
notes: paces] [The second edition of the Cambridge Shakespeare (1891) quotes Vaughan (New Readings, 1878–1886) as suggesting prances for paces. This, and many other of Vaughan’s suggestions which he withdrew in the second edition of his work, I have thought it unnecessary to include in the Textual Notes of this edition.—Ed.]

11.play: Which, like the meteors of a troubled heaven,
notes: meteors] Hudson (ed. 1880): Meteor was used in a much more general sense than we attach to the word. It might include the Aurora Borealis, which

12.play: All of one nature, of one substance bred,

13.play: Did lately meet in the intestine shock

14.play: And furious close of civil butchery
notes: close] SCHMIDT (1874): Hostile meeting, grapple.

15.play: Shall now, in mutual well-beseeming ranks,
notes: mutual] ELTON (ed 1889): “Helping each other”. the primary sense. There is also a touch of the next sense, “united.”

16.play: March all one way and be no more opposed

18.play: The edge of war, like an ill-sheathed knife,
notes: edge] SCHMIDT (1874): Sword; cf. Coriolanus, V, vi, 113: “Stain all your edges on me”

19.play: No more shall cut his master. Therefore, friends,

20.play: As far as to the sepulchre of Christ,

21.play: Whose soldier now, under whose blessed cross

22.play: We are impressed and engaged to fight,

23.play: Forthwith a power of English shall we levy;

24.play: Whose arms were moulded in their mothers' womb

25.play: To chase these pagans in those holy fields

26.play: Over whose acres walk'd those blessed feet

27.play: Which fourteen hundred years ago were nail'd

28.play: For our advantage on the bitter cross.

29.play: But this our purpose now is twelve month old,
notes: now . . . month] DYCE (ed. 1864): The reading of the earliest quartos is objectionable because of the following “meet not now,” line 30 — ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 62): A was frequently inserted before a numeral adjective [cf. Textual Notes], for the purpose of indicating that the objects enumerated are regarded collectively as one. . . . (p. 63): This usage is not common in Shakespeare, however, except after one: “But one seven years”—Coriolanus, IV, i, 55.—COWL (ed. 1914): To supply the loss of now, which dropped out of the text in Qq3-6 [i.e., Qq4-6], Q7 reads as but twelve months, and Ff as a twelve-month.

30.play: And bootless 'tis to tell you we will go:

31.play: Therefore we meet not now. Then let me hear
notes: Therefore . . . nowe] MALONE (Var. ed. 1785): “Not on that account do we now meet; we are not now assembled to acquaint you with our intended expedition.”

32.play: Of you, my gentle cousin Westmoreland,
notes: Cosen] HUDSON (ed. 1880): Ralph Neville, the present Earl of Westmoreland, married for his first wife Joan, daughter to John of Gaunt by Catherine Swynford, and therefore half-sister to Henry IV. Cousin, in old English, bears much the same sense as kinsman in our time.

33.play: What yesternight our council did decree

notes: deere] N. E. D. (1897): Important.

35.play: WESTMORELAND: My liege, this haste was hot in question,
notes: hot . . . question] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Hotly discussed

36.play: And many limits of the charge set down
notes: limits . . . charge] COLLIER (ed. 1842): Bounds of the expense.

37.play: But yesternight: when all athwart there came
notes: athwart] COW. (ed. 1914): Perversely, thwarting our purpose.

38.play: A post from Wales loaden with heavy news;

39.play: Whose worst was, that the noble Mortimer,
notes: Mortimer] See note on I, iii, 80, brother in law.

40.play: Leading the men of Herefordshire to fight
notes: Herdfordshire] WALKER (Sh. Vers, 1854, p 1): Hereford is a dissyllable.

41.play: Against the irregular and wild Glendower,
notes: irregular,] SCHMIDT (1874): Lawless. [The use of the comma after irregular perhaps illustrates a principle of rhetorical punctuation discussed by SIMPSON (Sh. Punc., 1911, p. 26), i. e., the use of the comma to mark emphasis. In this use the comma follows the word to be stressed. Cf. Romeo and Juliet, I, i, 188: “Love, is a smoke made with the fume of sighs”; and Antony and Cleopatra, II, vi, 64: “I have heard that Julius Caesar, Grew fat with feasting there.”—ED.]

42.play: Was by the rude hands of that Welshman taken,
notes: taken] BAYFIELD (Study of Sh Vers, 1920, p. 252), [whose contention is that abbreviated and clipped words in the text of Shakespeare are the work of editors, and that the poet himself preferred the full forms, here discusses the forms taken and tane]: In twelve Quarto plays, the Q texts give tane 32 times, taken 4 times. . . . Three of these examples of taken, and three of tane, end a line. If we consider these six together, we shall see how little tane is likely to have been the form that came from Shakespeare’s pen: “Of your fair flesh to be cut off and tane” (Merch. of Ven., I, iii, 145); “My liege, the Duke of Buckingham is taken” (Rich. III, IV, iv, 533); “Was by the rude hands of that Welchman taken” (I Henry IV, I, i, 41); “According to our threefold order tane” (I Henry IV, III, i, 69); “O, I have tane / Too little care of this” (Lear, III, iv, 32); “King Lear hath lost, he and his daughter taine” (Lear, V, ii, 6).

43.play: A thousand of his people butchered;
notes: HUDSON (ed. 1852) We prefer the reading of Qq, not only as having better authority, but because it makes the connection plainer between a thousand people and whose dead corpse. Of course being is understood before butchered, and corpse is used as a collective noun

44.play: Upon whose dead corpse there was such misuse,
notes: corpses] DYCE (ed. 1857): Corps and corpses might be considered as the plural of corse, if other passages in our author’s writings did not forbid us to suppose so, e. g., “My poor corps” (Twelfth Night, II, iv, 62), “Again possess her corps” (Winter’s Tale, V, i, 58)—COWL (ed. 1914): Corpes, i. e., corpses, as in II Henry IV, I, i, 192. So, Middleton, Chaste Maid, II, ii: “The dead corps of poor calves.”

46.play: By those Welshwomen done as may not be

47.play: Without much shame retold or spoken of.

48.play: KING HENRY IV: It seems then that the tidings of this broil

49.play: Brake off our business for the Holy Land.

50.play: WESTMORELAND: This match'd with other did, my gracious lord;

51.play: For more uneven and unwelcome news

52.play: Came from the north and thus it did import:

53.play: On Holy-rood day, the gallant Hotspur there,
notes: holly rode] Holy-rood, or Holy-cross, day, September 14

54.play: Young Harry Percy and brave Archibald,

notes: approvèd] Schmidt (1874): Tested, tried.
Holmedon] Wright (ed. 1897): Humbleton in Northumberland

57.play: Where they did spend a sad and bloody hour,

58.play: As by discharge of their artillery,
notes: artillery] Ax (Rel. of Sh. to Hol., 1912, p 16): When the poet speaks of artillery and gunpowder (cf. I, iii, 56, 60), we must consider such allusions anachronisms. At that epoch gunpowder was being used for siege-guns (cf. Holinshed, III, 39. Henry IV, besieging Berwick, caused a piece of artillery to be planted against the town), but it was not yet employed for field guns or muskets.... Shakespeare could have read... in Holinshed's History of Scotland, that Henry Hotspur assailed the Scotch with an incessant shot of arrows.—Cowl (ed. 1914): Artillery formerly included bows as well as guns, but that Shakespeare had the latter in mind is clear from the context.

59.play: And shape of likelihood, the news was told;

60.play: For he that brought them, in the very heat

61.play: And pride of their contention did take horse,
notes: pride] N. E. D. (1900). Glory; I Henry VI, IV, vi, 56: "Fight by thy father's side ... let's die in pride."—Cow. (ed. 1914): Height.

62.play: Uncertain of the issue any way.

63.play: KING HENRY IV: Here is a dear, a true industrious friend,

64.play: Sir Walter Blunt, new lighted from his horse.
notes: Blunt] Knight (ed. 1839): There is dramatic propriety in making Sir Walter, the dear friend of the king, bring the "smooth and welcome news" of victory; and in this the poet is neither borne out nor contradicted by the Chronicles. An entry, however, has been found in the Pell Rolls of a grant of forty pounds a year "to Nicholas Merbury . . . the first person who reported for a certainty to our lord, the king, the good, agreeable, and acceptable news of the success of the expedition at Holmedon."

65.play: Stain'd with the variation of each soil
notes: Staind . . . soile] Theobald (ed. 1733): This was looked on, I apprehend, in a ludicrous light by some carpers; at least, I find it parodied in an old comedy, and applied to a person in a scuffle tumbled into the dirt: "Your

66.play: Betwixt that Holmedon and this seat of ours;

67.play: And he hath brought us smooth and welcome news.
notes: smothe and welcom] Cow. (ed. 1914): Epithets antithetical to “uneven” and “unwelcome” in line 50.

68.play: The Earl of Douglas is discomfited:

69.play: Ten thousand bold Scots, two and twenty knights,

70.play: Balk'd in their own blood did Sir Walter see
notes: Balkt] GREY (Crit. Notes, 1754): Balkt should be read Bak’d, the blood being coagulated and congeled upon their cloaths and bodies. The same expression is used in John, III, iii, : “Or if that surly spirit, melancholy,/ Had bak’d thy blood and made it heavy-thick.”—WARTON (Var. ed. 1773, Appendix, vol x): Balk is a ridge of land: here is a metaphor, and perhaps the poet meant, in his bold and careless manner of expression, . . . “a ridge of dead bodies, piled up in blood.” If this be the meaning, we might add to the pointing “Balk’d, in their own blood.” I once conjectured “bak’d” . . . but I prefer the common reading. A balk, in the sense here mentioned, is a common expression in Warwickshire and the northern counties. It is used in the same signification in Chaucer’s Plowman’s Tale.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Either bath’d or bak’d, i. e., encrusted over with blood dried upon them. Passages from Heywood’s Iron Age may countenance the latter conjecture: “Troilus lies embak’d in his cold blood”, again “bak’d in blood and dirt”; and again “as bak’d in blood”; also Hamlet [II, ii, 436] “With blood of fathers . . . Bak’d and impasted.”—TOLLET (Var. ed. 1778): Lay in heaps or hillocks in their own blood. . . . Mr. Pope, in the Iliad, has the same thought: “On heaps the Greeks, on heaps the Trojans bled, And thickening round them rose the hills of dead.”—LETSOM (New Readings in Sh, 1853): Bark’d, i. e., coated with dry and hardened blood, as a tree is coated with bark. . . . It is not un-

71.play: On Holmedon's plains. Of prisoners, Hotspur took

72.play: Mordake the Earl of Fife, and eldest son
notes: Earle] MALONE (ed. 1790): Earl is used as a dissylable. Mr. Pope, not perceiving this, reads the Earl.—RITSON (Cursory Crit., 1792): Mr. Pope could no more have conceived Shakespeare capable of writing such a line than he

73.play: To beaten Douglas; and the Earl of Athol,
notes: Earle of Athol] FRENCH (Sh. Genealog., 1869): At the date of the battle of Holmedon, there was virtually no Earl of Athol, that dignity having been resigned to the crown in 1341. . . . There is, however, in Rymer's Foedera, a safe-conduct granted to Walter Stewart, Earl of Athol and Caithness, dated June 8, 1404
Menteith] COWL (ed. 1914): One of the titles of Murdoch Earl of Fife. In making two persons of one Shakespeare follows Holinshed.

75.play: And is not this an honourable spoil?

76.play: A gallant prize? ha, cousin, is it not?

notes: In . . . is.] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): In the first and best quarto these words stand at some distance from [Ha coosen, is it not?], a circumstance that was but lately attended to, which leads the editor now to imagine that they might be designed for line 77, which should finish, then, at Prince: the king's reflection which follows is rather more apposite to such a conclusion; and the line itself accords better with the generality of those in the play, being what the grammarians call acatalectic, or having nothing redundant.—CAMBRIDGE EDITORS (1864). Dr. Nicholson proposes: "A gallant prize? Ha, cousin, is it not, in faith? West. It is a conquest, etc." For, he says, "in faith" sounds too familiar to be addressed by a subject to a king.

78.play: It is a conquest for a prince to boast of.

79.play: KING HENRY IV: Yea, there thou makest me sad and makest me sin

80.play: In envy that my Lord Northumberland

81.play: Should be the father to so blest a son,

82.play: A son who is the theme of honour's tongue;

83.play: Amongst a grove, the very straightest plant;

84.play: Who is sweet Fortune's minion and her pride:
notes: minion] See note on I, ii, 25.

85.play: Whilst I, by looking on the praise of him,

86.play: See riot and dishonour stain the brow

87.play: Of my young Harry. O that it could be proved

88.play: That some night-tripping fairy had exchanged

89.play: In cradle-clothes our children where they lay,

90.play: And call'd mine Percy, his Plantagenet!

91.play: Then would I have his Harry, and he mine.

92.play: But let him from my thoughts. What think you, coz,

93.play: Of this young Percy's pride? the prisoners,

94.play: Which he in this adventure hath surprised,

95.play: To his own use he keeps; and sends me word,

96.play: I shall have none but Mordake Earl of Fife.

97.play: WESTMORELAND: This is his uncle's teaching; this is Worcester,

98.play: Malevolent to you in all aspects;
notes: Malevolent . . . aspects] Henley (Var. ed 1785): An astrological allusion. Worcester is represented as the malignant star that influenced the conduct of Hotspur.—N. E. D. (1888): Aspect,—the way in which the planets look upon each other, transferred to their joint look upon the earth. The astrological sense is the earliest.

99.play: Which makes him prune himself, and bristle up
notes: plume] Grey (Remarks upon a Late Edition, 1751) accuses Warburton of implying, by his note “To this the Oxford Editor gives his fiat,” that the reading plume originated with him and not with “the Oxford editor,” Hanmer.

100.play: The crest of youth against your dignity.
notes: crest] N. E. D. (1893). Comb, tuft of feathers or the like, on an animal’s head, used figuratively as a symbol of pride

101.play: KING HENRY IV: But I have sent for him to answer this;
notes: Cowl (ed. 1914): This is not according to Holinshed.

102.play: And for this cause awhile we must neglect

104.play: Cousin, on Wednesday next our council we

105.play: Will hold at Windsor; so inform the lords:

106.play: But come yourself with speed to us again;

107.play: For more is to be said and to be done

108.play: Than out of anger can be uttered.

1.play: Enter prince of Wales, and Sir Iohn Falstaffe. Falſt. Now Hal, what time of day is it lad?
notes: what ... lad?] TILLEY (Jour. Eng. and Ger. Phil., 1918 and 1925) suggests that this question takes an additional significance in the light of the Puritans' attitude toward proper employment of time, and asserts that it was also used, in Elizabethan times, to indicate tardiness of apprehension on the part of the person questioned He compares Falstaff's question here with As You Like It, III, ii, 283 and with Love's Labour's Lost, II, i, 127; and he quotes the New English Dictionary: to know what o'clock it is—to know the real state of things.

2.play: Prince. Thou art ſo fat-witted with drinking of olde

3.play: ſacke, and vnbuttoning thee after ſupper, and fleeping
notes: sacke] Cf. note on I, ii, 108, Sacke ... Sugar.

4.play: vpon benches after noone; that thou hast forgotten to de-

5.play: maunde that truelie which thou wouldeſt trulie knowe.

6.play: What a diuell haft thou to do with the time of the daie?

7.play: vnles houres were cups of facke, and minutes capons, and

8.play: clockes the tongues of Baudes, and Dialles the fignes of

9.play: leaping houſes, and the bleſſed ſunne himselfe a faire hot

10.play: wench in flame-couloured taffata; I ſee no reason why
notes: flame-coloured taffata] COWL(ed. 1914): An allusion to red gowns worn

11.play: thou ſhouldſt be ſo ſuperfluous to demaunde the time of the

13.play: Falſt Indeede you come neere me nowe Hal, for wee

14.play: that take purſes go by the moone and the feuen ſtars, and
notes: go by] COWL (ed. 1914): Tell the time by So, Davenport, King John and Matilda, I, ii “When our dials retrograde do run, We leave to look on them and go by the sun”

15.play: not by Phœbus, he, that wandring knight ſo faire and I

16.play: prethe ſweet wag when thou art a king, as God ſaue thy

17.play: grace. maieſtie I ſhould ſay, for grace thou wilt haue none.

19.play: Falſt. No by my troth, not ſo much as will ferue to bee

20.play: prologue to an egge and butter.
notes: egge and butter] Steevens (ed. 1793, note on II, i, 56): It appears from The Household Book of the Fifth Earl of Northumberland that buttered eggs was the usual breakfast of my Lord and Lady during Lent.—Cow. (ed. 1914): Typical Friday or Lenten fare. ... The Puritans, who introduced long graces before and after meat, had no mind to fasting and detested an egg and butter as a dish tainted with popery.

21.play: Prin. Wel, how then? come roundly, roundly.
notes: then] Hunter (ed. 1871) suggests that this may refer back to “when thou art a king” in line 16.

22.play: Falſt. Marry then ſweet wag, when thou art king let

23.play: not vs that are ſquiers of the nights bodie, bee called

24.play: theeues of the daies beauty let vs be Dianaes forreſters,
notes: Dianaes foresters] Henderson (Var ed. 1785): “Exile and slander are justly me awarded, My wife and her lack lands and lawful right, And me their lord made dame Diana’s knight”;—so lamenteth Thos. Mowbray, duke of Norfolk, in The Mirror for Magistrates.—Malone (ed. 1790). We learn from Hall that certain persons who appeared as foresters in a pageant in the reign of Henry VIII were called Diana’s knights.—Root (Classical Mythology in Sh., 1903, p 51): It is as patroness and type of chastity that Shakespeare most often alludes to Diana. ... Next in frequency are the allusions to her as moon-goddess. ... Diana as huntress, frequenter of groves, the center of a band of nymphs, appears but little in the authentic plays. ... The earliest allusion is in I Henry IV.

25.play: gentlemen of the ſhade, minions of the moone, and let men
notes: minions ... moone] Steevens (Var. ed. 1803): As Dr. Farmer observes, Gamaliel Ratsey and his company [of outlaws] “became servants to the moone, for the sunne was too hot for them.”

26.play: ſay wee be men of good gouernement, being gouerned as

27.play: the fea is, by our noble and chaſt miſtreſſe the moone,

notes: countenance] Cowl (ed. 1914): With a play on the double meaning of countenance, viz , face and patronage.

29.play: Prince. Thou ſaieſt well, and it holds wel to, for the
notes: it ... well] Cowl (ed. 1914): “The simile is apt.”

30.play: fortune of vs that are the moones men, doth ebbe and flow

31.play: like the fea, being gouerned as the ſea is by the moone, as

32.play: for proofe. Now a purſe of gold moſt refolutely ſnatcht

33.play: on Munday night and most diſſolutely ſpent on tueſday

34.play: morning, got with ſwearing, lay by, and ſpent with crying,
notes: lay by] Warburton (ed. 1747): Swearing at the passengers they robbed,“ lay by your arms”; or, rather, a phrase that signified “stand still ”—Capell (Notes, 1779): “Stand!” the salute of a robber.—Steevens (ed. 1793): A phrase adopted from navigation signifying by slacking sail to become stationary.—Halliwell (ed. 1859) quotes a correspondent who suggests that both lay by and bring in are sea-terms. [Note the continuance of nautical imagery in lines 35, 36.—Ed.]—Hudson (ed. 1880): Perhaps a term used by highway-

35.play: bring in, now in as low an ebbe as the foot of the ladder,

36.play: and by and by in as high a flow as the ridge of the gal-

38.play: Falſt. By the Lord thou fast true lad, and is not my
notes: my] ABBOTT (Sh. Grammar, 1870, p. 160): Though there are many exceptions, the rule seems to be that mine and thine are used where the possessive is to be unemphatic. Mine is used before words to which it is so frequently prefixed as to become almost a part of them, as mine host, but the less common my hostess occurs. . . . Euphony would dictate this distinction. The pause which we are obliged to make between my and a following vowel serves for a kind of emphasis.

39.play: hoſteſſe of the tauerne a moſt ſweet wench ?

40.play: Prin. As the hony of Hibla my old lad of the caſtle, and
notes: Hiblà . . . castle] ANON (ed. Halliwell, 1859): The words of Hiblà being omitted in the Folio, . . . I restore, point, and explain thus: “As is the honey, my old lad, of the Castle, - t. e., of the Castle Tavern” [cf note on I, 11, 8, 9. Dials . . . leaping houses.—ED].

41.play: is not a buffe Ierkin a moſt ſweet robe of durance ?
notes: buffe...durance] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): The sheriff's officers were formerly clad in buff. When Falstaff asks whether his hostess is not a sweet wench, the prince asks whether it will not be a sweet thing to go to prison by running into debt to the sweet wench.—STEEVENS (Var ed 1773): The following passage from an old play, Ram Alley, may serve to confirm Dr. Johnson's observation: "Look, I have certain goblins in buff jerkins Lye ambuscado...Enter Serjeants." Again, in The Comedy of Errors [IV, ii, 33]: "A devil in an everlasting garment hath him, A fellow all in buff." In Westward Hoe, by Dekker and Webster, 1607, I meet a passage which leads me to believe that a robe, or suit, of durance was of some kind of lasting stuff, such as we call at present everlasting.—STAUNTON (ed. 1858): A sergeant's buff leather garment was called durance partly on account of its everlasting qualities, and partly from a quibble on the occupation of its wearer, which was that of arresting and clapping men "in durance."

notes: O rare] HALLIWELL (ed 1859): Shakelthea, or a Shadow of Truth, 1598: “As Caius walks the streets, if he but heare / A black man grunt his note, he cries, oh rare! / He cries, oh rare! to heare the Irishmen / Cry pippe, fine pippe, whith a shrill accent, when / He comes to Mercer's Chapel; and oh rare! / At Ludgate at the prisoners plane-song there / . . . Briefly so long he hath usde to cry, oh rare! / That now the phrase is grown thin and thred-bare.”

71.play: as a gyb Cat, or a lugd beare.
notes: gyb Cat] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Means, I know not why, an old cat — PERCY (Var. ed. 1773) A common term in Northamptonshire for a he-cat — WARTON (Var. ed. 1773, Appendix, vol x). Gib is the abbreviation of Gilbert. ... Christian names have of old been appropriated to many animals, as Philip to a sparrow, Will to a goat. thus Gilbert was the name of a cat of the male species. Tibert, the Old French for Gilbert, is the name of the cat in Reynard the Fox. In the French Roman de la Rose we have "Thibert le chas," which Chaucer translated "Gibbe our cat." ... In Gammer Gurton's Needle we find. "Hath no man stolen her ducks and hens, or gelded Gyb her cat?" The composure of a cat is proverbial. ... Falstaff means that he is grown as dull and demure as a cat.—STEEVENS (Var ed. 1778): "Melancholy as a gib'd cat" is a proverb in Ray's Collection. ... I believe a gib'd cat is one that has been qualified for the seraglio, for all animals so mutilated become drowsy and melancholy.... In Sidney's Arcadia the cat is mentioned as melancholy without reference to castration.—MATHIAS (Pursuit of Lit., 1798, p. 83) Mr. Warton and Mr. Steevens have left it as a matter of doubt whether their own drowsiness and gravity, and that of their brother commentators on this line, was also in consequence of &c.—BOSWELL (Var. ed. 1821) The melancholy of cats generally is spoke of in Lyly's Midas. Gib was applied to any cat, male or female. So, in Gammer Gurton's Needle. "Gib, a fowle fiend might on her light." N. E. D. (1901). A cat, esp., a male cat, in later dialectal use, one that has been castrated.

72.play: Prin. Or an old lyon, or a louers Lute

73.play: Falſt. Yea, or the drone of a Lincolnshire bagpipe.
notes: drone... bagpipe] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): The dull croak of a frog, one of the native musicians of that watersih country.—IBID. (Var. ed. 1803): I am informed by Sir Joseph Banks that in the neighborhood of Boston in Lincolnshire the noisy frogs are still humorously denominated "the Boston waits." In The Pleasant ... Morall of Three Lords and Ladies of London, 1590, mention is made of "the sweet ballade of the Lincolnshire bagpipes."—BOSWELL (Var. ed. 1821): Robert Armin, A Nest of Ninnies, 1608: "At Christmas time ... amongst all the pleasure provided, a noyse of Minstrells and a Lincolnshire bagpipe were prepared."—HARLOWE (Notes and Queries, 1867): I stumbled over this passage at a Lincolnshire vicarage, and asked the vicar whether he had ever heard of a bagpipe peculiar to the county. "Never," he replied, "but I have often heard a bittern so called." In Bewick I find the following description of a bittern: "might be mistaken for a heron, were it not for the

74.play: Prince. What faieſt thou to a Hare, or the malancholy
notes: Hare] JOHNSON (ed 1765): A hare may be considered melancholy because she is upon her form always solitary, and according to the physic of the times, the flesh of it was supposed to generate melancholy.—STEEVENS (ed. 1793): The Egyptians in their hieroglyphics expressed a melancholy man by a hare sitting in her form.—STAUNTON (ed 1858) quotes Turberville's Book on Hunting and Falconry: "The hare first taught us the use of the hearbe called wyld Succory, which is excellent for those who are disposed to be melancholicke She herself is one of the most melancholicke beasts that is."

malancholy of Moorditch] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): I do not understand, unless it may allude to the croaking of the frogs.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1773): It appears from Stow's Survey that a broad ditch formerly parted Bedlam Hospital from Moor-fields; and what hath a more melancholy appearance than stagnant water? Dekker's Gull's Hornbook, 1609: "It will be a sorer labour than the cleansing of Augeas stables or the scowering of Moorditch."—MALONE (Second Appendix to Supplement, 1783): More appositely, Taylor's Pilgrymage, 1618, "My bodie being tired with travel, and my mind attired in moody, muddy, Moorditch, melancholy."

notes: comparative] EDWARDS (Canons of Crit., 1748, Canon II): [Mr. Warburton and Sir Thomas Hanmer] did not understand the common reading, comparative, which Shakespeare uses in the sense of "dealing in comparisons," or "a simile-monger." In this place he uses it as an adjective, but has given us the same word as a substantive in this very play, III, ii, 67.

85.play: Falſt. O thou hast damnable iteration, and art indeed
notes: damnable iteration] EDWARDS (Canons of Crit., 1748, p. 11): A damnable way of quoting or repeating Scripture.—MALONE (Var. ed. 1778): Iteration is right, for it also signified simply citation or recitation. So, in Marlowe's Faustus: "Here, take this book and peruse it well, The iterating of these lines brings gold." From the context, iterating here means pronouncing, reciting.—KNIGHT (ed. 1839): Repetition, not recitation. Falstaff does not complain only of Hal's quoting a Scriptural text, but that he has been retorting and distorting the meaning of his words throughout the scene.—N. E. D. (1901): Repetition.

notes: baffell] TOLLET (Var. ed. 1778): To treat with the greatest ignominy possible So Holinshed explains it: “Baffulling is a great disgrace among the Scots, and it is used when a man is openlie periured, and then they make of him an image, painted reversed, with his heels upward.” (Chron., III, 87 and 121) —Nares (Glossary, 1822): A punishment of infamy, inflicted on recreant knights, one part of which was hanging them up by the heels —Singer (ed. 1826): Something of the kind is implied in [II, iv, 408–9].—N. E. D. (1893): To disgrace a perjured knight with infamy. Spenser, F. Q., VI, vii, 27: “He by the heels him hung upon a tree And bafful’d so.”

102.play: Enter Poines. Poynes nowe ſhall we knowe if Gadſhill haue fet a
notes: Poynes] THEOBALD (ed. 1733): Mr. Pope has given us one signal observation in his Preface to our Author's works. "Throughout the plays," he says, "had all the speeches been printed without the very names of the persons, I believe one might have supply'd them with certainty for every speaker." But how fallible the most sufficient critick may be, this passage in controversy [see Textual Notes] is a main instance. As signal a blunder has escaped all the editors here as any through the whole set of plays. Will anyone persuade me that Shakespeare could be guilty of such inconsistency as to make Poins at his first entrance want news of Gadshill, and immediately after be able to give a full account of him? No; Falstaff seeing Poins at hand turns the stream of his discourse from the prince, and says, Now shall we know, &c., and then immediately falls into railing and invective against Poins. How admirably is this in character for Falstaff! And Poins, who knew well his abusive manner, seems in part to overhear him: and so soon as he has returned the prince's salutation, cries, by way of answer, What says, &c.—JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Theobald's triumph over the other editors might have been abated by a confession that the first edition [i.e., Q2, 1599, neither Theobald nor Johnson knew Q1.—ED.] gave him at least a glimpse of this emendation.—COLLIER (ed. 1858): We can see how this blunder originated [see Textual Notes]—Falstaff's exclamation, "Poins!" having been mistaken for the prefix to a speech.

notes: omnipotent] N. E. D. (1909): Used humorously, unparalleled, utter, arrant.

105.play: villaine that euer cried, ſtand, to a true man.
notes: true man] STEVENS (Var. ed. 1778; note on II, ii, 85): In the old plays a true man is always set in opposition to a thief. So, in the ancient Morality, Hycke Skorner: “And when me list to hang a true-man, Thieves I can help out of prison.” Again, The Four Prentices of London, 1632: “Now, true-man, try if thou canst rob a thief.”—N. E. D. (1926): An honest man as distinguished from a criminal.

107.play: Poines. Good morrow ſweete Hal. What faies Mon-

108.play: ſieur remorſe what ſaies fir Iohn Sacke, and Sugar
notes: Sacke . . . Sugar] Johnson (Var. ed. 1773): Sack in Shakespeare’s time is probably what we call sherry, which . . . is still drank with sugar.—Percy (Var. ed., 1773, p. 342) Sack and sugar was a favorite liquor in Shakespeare’s time. In a letter describing Queen Elizabeth’s entertainment at Killingworth Castle in 1575, the writer says, “sipt I no more sack and sugar than I do malmsey, I should not blush so much” . . . In a MS. of the chamberlains’ books of the city of Worcester, I find the following, which points out the origin of our word sack (fr. sec): “Anno Eliz xxxiii. Item. For a gallon of claret wine, and seck, and a pound of sugar . . . iii s.”—Steevens (Malone’s Supplement, 1780): The difference between true sack and sherry is distinctly marked in the following passage in Fortune by Land and Sea, Heywood and Rowley, 1555: “Rayns Some sack, boy! Drawer Good sherry sack, sir? Rayns. I meant canary. Hast no brain?”—Ritson (Remarks, 1783): Sack, according to the information of a very old gentleman, was composed of sherry, syder, and sugar. . . . It does not seem to be at present known; the sweet wine, now so called, being of a different nature.—Reed (Var. ed 1785): Moryson, Itinerary, 1617, mentioning the Scots, observes: “They drink pure wine, not with sugar as the English.” And again, p. 152 “Gentlemen garraws onely in wine, with which many mix sugar, which I never observed in any other place. . . . And because the taste of the English is delighted with sweetness, the wine in the taverns is commonly mixed.”—Malone (ed. 1790): Falstaff’s wine was Sherry, a Spanish wine made at Xeres. He frequently calls it sherris-sack. Nor will his mixing it with sugar seem extraordinary when it is known that it was a common practice to put sugar into all wines.—Ibid (Steevens ed., 1793): I have found reason to believe that Falstaff’s sack was the dry Spanish wine which we call Mountain Malaga. A passage from Via Recta ad Vitam Longam, by Thos. Venner, Bathe, 1622, seems to ascertain this: “Sacke is completely hot . . . and of thin parts, and therefore it doth vehemently and quickly heat the body. Some affect to drink it with sugar. . . Sack taken by itself is very hot and penetrative; being taken with sugar, the heat is somewhat allayed.” The author speaks thus of the wine which we

Agrees &c. is put for Agrees u with the devil and thee; and if the words be interpreted thus, the modern reading [see Textual Notes] is unnecessary.—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, sect. 335): The third person plural of the verb, in -s, is sometimes used preceding a plural subject.

120.play: foure a clocke early at Gadſhill, there are pilgrims going
notes: Gadshill] See note on I, ii, 102, Gadshill.—BOSWELL (Var. ed. 1821): Mr. Steevens tells us that as early as 1558 there was a ballad entitled The Robbery at Gadshill. ... Shakespeare, however, probably alluded to the conduct of a particular gang who appear in 1590 to have infested Gadshill, ... particulars are in a narrative preserved in the Lansdowne MSS. in the British Museum in the handwriting of Sir Roger Manwood, at that time Chief Baron of the Exchequer, and indorsed with the date 3 July 1590. “Circumstances vrginge me Sr. Roger Manwood to proceade in the late indytinge of Curtall, Manweringe, and other Malefactors in Kent. . . . In the course of the MychaeImas Terme, I being at London, mange robbcryes were done in the hyve wayes at Gadshill on the west part of Rochester ... by horse theeues, with such fat and lusty horses as weare not lyke hakney horsses . . . and one of them sometyme wearing a vizardc . . and no man durst trauell that waye without greate companye.”—HALLIWELL (ed. 1859) quotes a description of a robbery near Gadshill from The Travels of Frederick, Duke of Wurtemburg, in England, 1592, and cites many references to robberies on Gadshill down to Defoe’s Tour, 1724. [Gadshill was so notorious a place for robberies that it seems futile to speculate on what particular robbery Shakespeare had in mind, particularly as Gadshill is mentioned in his source, The Famous Victories of Henry V.—Ed.]

185.play: Po Farewel my Lord. Exit Poines.

186.play: Prin. I know you all, and wil a while vphold
notes: There is something sophistical in this fine speech. The prince enjoys Falstaff, and rather pays himself off with fine metaphors to excuse his enjoyment... Perhaps, however, these are modern refinements of feeling, Shakespeare wrote for the stage, and his effect is broad, natural, and simple.—RANSOME (Short Stud. of Sh Plots, 1890, p. 198). Shakespeare was careful to differentiate between Richard II and Henry V. Richard could never have made this speech... After this speech the audience would enjoy the Falstaff scenes, which would have been quite impossible had they believed themselves to be looking on the creation of another ruined character like Richard—BOAS (Sh and his Predecessors, 1896, p 270). This Pharisaical declaration need not be taken too literally, as it is probably meant for little more than a dramatic “aside” to the audience, assuring them that Henry is not in reality what he appears—BRANDES (Wm Sh., a Crit Study, 1896, p 200) The son is not so unlike the father as the father believes. Shakespeare has made him, in his way, adopt a scarcely less diplomatic policy... This self-consciousness of Henry’s was to some extent imposed upon Shakespeare. Without it he could scarcely have brought upon the stage in such questionable company a prince who had become a national hero. Yet if the prince had acted with the cut-and-dried deliberation of purpose which he here attributes to himself, we should have had to write him down an unmitigated charlatan... We must allow for Shakespeare’s use of soliloquy. He frequently regards it as an indispensable stage-convention, which does not really reveal the thoughts of the speaker, but only serves... to give the hearer information he requires. Such a soliloquy ought to be spoken with a good deal of sophisticated self-justification, or else in a tone of gay raillery—ARNOLD (Soliloquies of Sh., 1911, p. 58). Excluding the villain’s soliloquy, there is only one flagrant case of the self-characterizing monologue in Shakespeare, that of Prince Hal. This speech exists for the sake of the exposition—AX (Rel. of Sh. to Hol., 1912, pp 20-22): We deem this monologue unnatural, the speaker’s explanations strange, and the motives he states hateful.... How easy would it have been for the author to give Harry’s behavior a natural explanation. Could he not have made him say that his escapades were but tricks of youth, and that if God should ever give him the crown, He would also give him the power to bear it?... If we wish to enjoy this play, we must try to forget these words of the prince. But Shakespeare comes to our aid by forgetting them himself, and whenever we see Harry in Falstaff’s company, he is delighting in it heartily and we have nowhere the impression of seeing before us a player within a play, but an ingenuous youth who is anything but a hypocrite.—TOLMAN (P.M.L.A., 1919): I have sometimes wished that Shakespeare had

187.play: The vnyokt humour of your idlenes,

188.play: Yet herein wil I imitate the funne,

189.play: Who doth permit the baſe contagious clouds

190.play: To fmother vp his beautie from the world,

191.play: That when he pleaſe againe to be himſelfe,

192.play: Being wanted he may be more wondred at

193.play: By breaking through the foule and ougly miſts

194.play: Of vapours that did feeme to ſtrangle him.

195.play: If all the yeere were playing holly-dayes,

196.play: To ſport would be as tedious as to worke;

197.play: But when they ſeldome come, they wiſht for come,

198.play: And nothing pleaſeth but rare accidents
notes: accidents] Cowl (ed. 1914): For accidents in the sense of incidents, cf. Tempest, V, i, 250.

199.play: So when this looſe behauiour I throw off,

200.play: And pay the debt I neuer promiſed,

201.play: By how much better then my word I am,

202.play: By fo much ſhall I falfifie mens hopes,
notes: falsifie … hopes] Johnson (ed. 1765): To falsify hope is to exceed hope.—Farmer (Var. ed. 1773. Appendix ii, vol. x): Hopes is used for expectations, whether good or bad. This is still common in the Midland counties. “Such manner of uncouth speech,” says Puttenham, “did the tanner of Tam-worth use to King Edward IV; which tanner having mistaken him and used very broad talk, at length perceiving that it was the king, was afraid, and said As requested, here is the full Play Text for 1 Henry IV Act 1, Scene 3 and the audited Notes section. I have identified the keywords in the text, verified their locations, and updated the line numbers in the notes to match the provided text precisely. Spacing has been added after every line of the text and every note entry.

203.play: And like bright mettal on a ſullein ground,

204.play: My reformation glittring ore my fault,

205.play: Shal ſhew more goodly, and attract more eyes

206.play: Then that which hath no foile to ſet it off.

207.play: Ile fo offend, to make offence a skill,

208.play: Redeeming time when men thinke leaſt I wil. Exit.

1.play: Enter the King, Northumberland, Worcester, Hotspur. KING: My blood hath been too cold and temperate,

2.play: Unapt to stir at these indignities,

3.play: And you have found me, for accordingly
notes: found] SCHMIDT (1874): Detected, unmasked—ELTON (ed. 1889): Found me out, seen that I am so.—N. E. D. (1901) Found out.

4.play: You tread upon my patience, but be sure

5.play: I will from henceforth rather be my selfe

6.play: Mightie, and to be fearde, then my condition

7.play: Which hath bin smooth as oile, soft as yong downe,

8.play: And therefore lost that title of respect,

9.play: Which the proud foule neare payes but to the proud

10.play: WORCESTER: Our house (my soveraigne liege) little deserves

11.play: The scourge of greatnes to be used on it,

12.play: And that same greatnesse to, which our owne hands 13/14: Have holpe to make so portly. NORTHUMBERLAND: My Lord.

15.play: KING: Worcester get thee gone, for I do see
notes: Worcester] Walker (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 235): Worcester is sometimes, I think, a trisyllable. [But note that the most authoritative text, Q1, of V, v, 14 requires us to pronounce Worcester as a dissylIable. The line-division of III, i, 3-9 is not sufficiently well-established to warrant the deductions drawn from it.—Ed.]—Collier (ed. 1858): For the sake of the measure the Old Corrector inserts Lord before Worcester; but the anger and impetuosity of Henry may naturally have produced this brief informality.—Abbott (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 385):—E Mute Pronounced. This is a trace of early English

16.play: Danger and disobedience in thine eye,

17.play: O sir, your presence is too bold and peremptory,
notes: O sir] ABBOTT (Sh Gram, 1870, p. 423): Some irregularities of metre may be explained by the custom of placing ejaculations, appellations, etc., out of the regular verse. Cf., among others, Othello, I, i, 56 "For, sir, it is as sure as you are Roderigo," and Lear, I, i, 53: "Sir, I love you more than words can wield the matter."

18.play: And majesty might never yet endure

19.play: The moody frontier of a servant brow,

20.play: You have good leave to leave us, when we need

21.play: Your use and counsel, we shall send for you. Exit Worcester.

24.play: Those prisoners in your highness' name demanded,

25.play: Which Harry Percy here at Holmedon took,

26.play: As is delivered to your majestie.
notes: delivered] SCHMIDT (1874): Communicated.
envie] SCHMIDT (1874) cites this passage, with some twenty others, to show that envy was used in the sense of “malice shown by calumny and depreciation”

28.play: Is guiltie of this fault, and not my sonne.

29.play: HOTSPUR: My liege, I did denie no prisoners,

30.play: But I remember when the fight was done,

31.play: When I was drie with rage, and extreame toile,

32.play: Breathles and faint, leaning upon my sword,

33.play: Came there a certaine Lord, neat and trimly drest,
notes: and trimly] COWU (ed. 1914): Hotspur’s impatience, here as often, breaks through the restraints of metre.

34.play: Fresh as a bridegroome, and his chin new rept,

35.play: Shewd like a stubble land at harvest home,

36.play: He was perfumed like a milliner,
notes: Milliner] NARES (Gloss., 1822) Originally a man, we presume from Milan, whence he imported female finery. N. E. D (1908). A vendor of fancy wares and apparel, especially such as were originally of Milan manufacture. [Cf. Winter's Tale, IV, iv, 191: "No millner can so fit his customers with gloves"—Ed ]—COWL (ed 1914). Middleton, Old Law, II, ii: "So passing well perfum'd too! Who's your milliner?" Rowley, Search for Money (ed. Percy Soc., ii, 17): "The milliners threw out perfumes to catch him by the nose, and sweet gloves . . . to fit his hand."

37.play: And 'twixt his finger and his thumb he held

38.play: A pouncet-box, which ever and anon
notes: pouncet-box] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): A small box for musk or other perfumes then in fashion, the lid of which being cut with openwork gave it its name, from pounsoner, to prick, pierce, or engrave.—COWL (ed. 1914). A box for containing pounce, a fine aromatic powder. The word has no connection with pounce, to perforate—N. E. D. supports Warburton.

39.play: He gave his nose, and took't away again,

40.play: Who therewith angry, when it next came there,

41.play: Took it in snuff, and still he smiled and talked:

42.play: And as the soldiers bore dead bodies by,

43.play: He called them untaught knaves, unmannerly,

44.play: To bring a slovenly unhandsome corse

45.play: Betwixt the wind and his nobility.

46.play: With many holiday and lady terms

47.play: He questioned me, amongst the rest demanded
notes: questioned] COWL (ed. 1914): Perhaps “talked to,” as sometimes elsewhere.

48.play: My prisoners in your majesty's behalf.

49.play: I then all smarting with my wounds being cold,
notes: cold] MALONE (ed. 1793): Whatever Percy might say of rage and toil, his wounds would at this time certainly be cold, and when they were cold would smart, and not before.—MALONE (ed Steevens, 1793): So, in Mortimeriados by Drayton, 1596: “As when the blood is cold we feel the wound”

50.play: To be so pestred with a Popingay,
notes: pestred] WALKER (Crit. Exam., 1860, II, 351): For “to pester a place or person,” in the sense of to crowd, be in a person’s way, see Coriolanus, IV, vi, 7: “Dissentious numbers pestering streets”; and Hamlet, I, ii, 22: “He hath not failed to pester us with message.”—ELTON (ed. 1889) quotes L. Digges (Lines, c. 1623, prefixed to Sh’s Poems, 1640). “Let but Falstaff come, Hall, Poins, the rest,—you scarce shall have a room, All is so pester’d.”—N. E. D. (1909) gives examples from as early as 1562 of pester in the modern sense of annoy, plague, especially, of noxious vermin, to infest.

51.play: Out of my griefe and my impacience
notes: griefe] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): Pain. In our ancient translations of medical treatises, dolor ventris is commonly called belly-grief.—SINGER (ed. 1856): So Falstaff talks about the grief of a wound in V, i, 132.

52.play: Answerd neglectingly, I know not what
notes: neglectingly] N E. D. (1908): Negligently.

53.play: He should, or he should not, for he made me mad

54.play: To see him shine so briske, and smell so sweet,
notes: briske] N E D (1888): Spruce, smartly dressed. Marlowe's Edward II (1590), I, iv: “I have not seen a dapper jack so brisk.”

55.play: And talke so like a waiting gentlewoman,

56.play: Of guns, and drums, and wounds, God save the mark.
notes: God save the mark] COWL (ed. 1914): An exclamation used generally by way of deprecation or apology, but here (cf Othello, I, i, 33) as an expression of scorn. Originally perhaps a form of words invoking a blessing upon the sign or mark of the cross made by way of averting an evil omen.

57.play: And telling me the soveraignest thing on earth

58.play: Was Parmacitie, for an inward bruise,
notes: Parmacitie . . . bruise] BOWLE (ed. Malone, 1790): So, in Sir T. Overbury's Characters, 1616: “An Ordinary Fencer. His wounds are seldom skin-deep, for an inward bruise, lambstones and sweetbreads are his only spermaceit.”—REED (Var. ed. 1803): Sir R. Hawkins, Voyage into the South Seas, 1593: “his [whale's] spawne is for divers purposes. This we corruptly call parmacittie, of the Latin words, Sperma ceti.”

59.play: And that it was great pitty, so it was,

60.play: This villanous saltpeeter, should be digd

61.play: Out of the bowels of the harmeles earth,

62.play: Which many a good tall fellow had destroyed
notes: tall] SCHMIDT (1874): Sturdy, lusty.—ONIONS (1911): Doughty, valiant.—N. E. D. (1919): Good at arms, stout or strong in combat, brave.

63.play: So cowardly, and but for these vile guns

64.play: He would himselfe have beene a souldior.

65.play: This bald unjoynted chat of his (my Lord)

notes: indirectly] WARBURTON (1747): Negligently.—N. E. D. (1901): Evasively.

67.play: And I beseech you, let not his report

69.play: Betwixt my love and your high majestie

70.play: BLUNT: The circumstance considered, good my lord,

71.play: What ere Lord Harry Percie then had said
notes: Lord] COLLIER (ed. 1842): Blunt, in Percy's presence, would hardly omit his title [see Textual Notes].—ELTON (ed. 1889) explains the omission of Lord in F1 as a mechanical error made at the time that the name of God was being deleted from the text; see note on I, ii, 38, By the Lord.

72.play: To such a person, and in such a place,

73.play: At such a time, with all the rest retold,

74.play: May reasonably die, and never rise

75.play: To do him wrong, or any way impeach

76.play: What then he said, so he unsay it now.

77.play: KING: Why yet he doth denie his prisoners,

79.play: That we at our owne charge shall ransome straight

80.play: His brother in law, the foolish Mortimer,
notes: brother in law] STEEVENS (Var ed 1778): Shakespeare has fallen into some contradictions with regard to the Lord Mortimer. Before he makes his personal appearance in the play, he is repeatedly spoken of as Hotspur’s brother-in-law. In Act III [i, 193], he calls Lady Percy his aunt, which she in fact was. This inconsistency may be accounted for as follows. It appears from both Dugdale’s and Sandford’s account of the Mortimer family that there were two of them taken prisoner at different times by Glendower, each bearing the name Edmund; one being the Earl of March, nephew to the Lady Percy, and the proper Mortimer of the play, the other Sir Edmund, uncle to the Earl and brother to Lady Percy.

81.play: Who on my soule, hath wilfully betraid

82.play: The lives of those, that he did lead to fight

83.play: Against that great Magitian, damnd Glendower,

84.play: Whose daughter as we heare, that Earle of March

85.play: Hath lately married: shall our coffers then

86.play: Be emptied, to redeeme a traitor home?

87.play: Shall we buy treason? and indent with feares
notes: indent . . . fears] POPE (ed 1723): Indent: bargain, article—WARBURTON (ed 1747) The reason he says bargain with fears is that he supposed Mortimer had betrayed his forces out of fear, as appears from the next speech. No need therefore to change fears to foes—JOHNSON (ed 1765): The king is not desired to article, or contract, with Mortimer, but with another for Mortimer. Fears may be used in an active sense for terrors.

88.play: When they have lost and forfeited themselves?

89.play: No, on the barren mountaines let him starve:

90.play: For I shall never hold that man my friend,

91.play: Whose tongue shall aske me for one penny cost

notes: Revolted Mortimer] GENTLEMAN (Introd. to Bell’s ed. 1773): The debate, so far, is maintained with becoming dignity and spirit, on both sides. “Revolted Mortimer!” should not be roared by Hotspur. Upon the king’s turning quickly upon him with a look of anger and resentment, he immediately softens his voice to a low and submissive cadence.

94.play: He never did fall off, my soveraigne liege

95.play: But by the chance of war, to prove that true

96.play: Needs no more but one tongue. for all those wounds,

97.play: Those mouthed wounds which valiantly he tooke,

98.play: When on the gentle Severns siedgie banke,

99.play: In single opposition hand to hand,

100.play: He did confound the best part of an houre,
notes: confound] N. E. D. (1893): Consume.—COWL (ed. 1914), Cf. Coriolanus, I, vi, 17: “How could’st thou in a mile confound an hour?”

101.play: In changing hardiment with great Glendower,
notes: hardiment] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): Hardiness, bravery.

102.play: Three times they breathd, & three times did they drinke

103.play: Upon agreement of swift Severns floud,

104.play: Who then affrighted with their bloudie lookes,

105.play: Ran fearefully among the trembling reedes,

106.play: And hid his crispe-head in the hollow banke,
notes: crispe-head] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Curled. . . . In one of Jonson’s Masques: “The rivers run as smoothed by his hand, Only their heads are crisped by his stroke” (Whalley’s ed., vi, 26).—SINGER (ed. 1826): Kyd’s Cornelia, 1596: “O beauteous Tyber . . . Turn not thy crispy tides like silver curls.”

108.play: Never did bare and rotten pollicy
notes: bare] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): I believe bare is right. “Never did policy lying open to detection so colour its workings.”—MASON (Comments on the Last Ed., 1785). Policy lying open to detection is no policy at all; the epithet base agrees best with rotten.

109.play: Colour her working with such deadly wounds,
notes: Colour] SCHMIDT (1874): Give a specious appearance.

110.play: Nor never could the noble Mortimer

111.play: Receive so many, and all willingly,

112.play: Then let not him be slandered with revolt

113.play: KING: Thou dost bely him Percy, thou dost bely him,
notes: bely] SCHMIDT (1874): Misrepresent.

114.play: He never did encounter with Glendower. 115/116: I tel thee, he durst as well have met the divell alone,

117.play: As Owen Glendower for an enemy.

118.play: Art thou not asham'd? but sirrha, henceforth
notes: sirrha] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): In the impetuous language of passion it is unreasonable to look for regularity of verse [cf. Textual Notes].—COWI. (ed. 1914): “Henceforth,” pronounced “hennesforth,” is a trisyllable, as in Marlowe, Edward II, I, i (Q1).

119.play: Let me not heare you speake of Mortimer:

120.play: Send me your prisoners with the speediest meanes,

121.play: Or you shal heare in such a kind from me

122.play: As will displease you. My Lord Northumberland:

123.play: We licence your departure with your sonne,

124.play: Send us your prisoners, or you wil heare of it. Exit King

125.play: HOTSPUR: And if the divel come and rore for them
notes: And if] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 74): And if represents both even if and if indeed.

126.play: I wil not send them: I will after straight

127.play: And tel him so, for I will ease my hart,

128.play: Albeit I make a hazard of my head.
notes: COLLIER (ed. 1858): Mr. Singer here introduces [see Textual Notes] a most cacophonous conjunction.

129.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: What? dronk with choler, stay, & pause a while, 130a: Here comes your uncle. Enter Worcester. 130b: HOTSPUR: Speake of Mortimer?

131.play: Zounds I will speake of him, and let my soule

132.play: Want mercy if I do not joine with him.

133.play: Yea on his part, ile empty all these vaines,

134.play: And shed my deere bloud, drop by drop in the dust,

135.play: But I will lift the down-trod Mortimer

136.play: As high in the aire as this unthankefull king,

137.play: As this ingrate and cankred Bullingbrooke.
notes: cankred] SCHMIDT (1874): Venomous, malignant.—HUDSON (ed. 1880): Canker, verb and noun, is used of anything that corrodes and consumes or that has the virulent and malignant qualities of a cancer

138.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Brother, the king hath made your nephew mad.

139.play: WORCESTER: Who strooke this heat up after I was gone?

140.play: HOTSPUR: He wil forsooth have all my prisoners,

141.play: And when I urg'd the ransome once againe

142.play: Of my wives brother, then his cheeke lookt pale,

143.play: And on my face he turn'd an eie of death,
notes: eie of death] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): An eye menacing death. Hotspur seems to describe the king as trembling with rage rather than fear.—MASON (Comments on Last Ed., 1785): Worcester, immediately after, remarks that he cannot blame him for trembling at the name of Mortimer.

144.play: Trembling even at the name of Mortimer.

145.play: WORCESTER: I cannot blame him, was not he proclaim'd

146.play: By Richard that dead is, the next of bloud?

147.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: He was, I heard the proclamation:

148.play: And then it was, when the unhappy king,

149.play: (Whose wrongs in us God pardon) did set forth
notes: in vs] HUDSON (ed. 1880): Which we inflicted.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Caused by us.—COWL (ed. 1914): To us. Cf. Wyclif: "Oure dettouris, that is men that haue synned in us."

151.play: From whence he intercepted, did returne

152.play: To be depos'd, and shortly murdered.

153.play: WORCESTER: And for whose death, we in the worlds wide

154.play: Live scandaliz'd and fouly spoken of.

155.play: HOTSPUR: But soft, I pray you did king Richard then

158.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: He did, my selfe did heare it.

159.play: HOTSPUR: Nay then I cannot blame his coosen king,

160.play: That wisht him on the barren mountaines starve,
notes: wisht] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 248): In the Elizabethan period there was difference of opinion as to which verbs did, and which did not, require to. Cf. All’s Well, II, i, 129: “As one near death to those that wish him live.”

161.play: But shal it be that you that set the crowne

162.play: Upon the head of this forgetful man,

163.play: And for his sake weare the detested blot

164.play: Of murtherous subornation? shal it be
notes: subornation] COWL (ed. 1914): Of being suborned to murder, of aiding and abetting murderers.

165.play: That you a world of curses undergo,

166.play: Being the agents, or base second meanes,

167.play: The cordes, the ladder, or the hangman rather,

168.play: O pardon me, that I descend so low,

169.play: To shew the line and the predicament,
notes: line] SCHMIDT (1874): Row, rank; cf. III, ii, 85.

170.play: Wherein you range under this subtil king!

171.play: Shall it for shame be spoken in these daies,

172.play: Or fil up Chronicles in time to come,

173.play: That men of your nobility and power

174.play: Did gage them both in an unjust behalfe,
notes: gage] SEYMOUR (Remarks, 1805): Does not this refer to chivalry? And does it not mean that these men had laid down their nobility and power as a gage or pledge to support the cause of the usurper?

175.play: (As both of you, God pardon it, have done)

176.play: To put down Richard, that sweet lovely Rose,

177.play: And plant this thorne, this canker Bullingbrooke?
notes: canker] STEEVENS (Var. ed 1773): The dog-rose So, in Much Ado, I, iii, 22: “I had rather be a canker in a hedge than a rose in his grace.”

178.play: And shal it in more shame be further spoken,

179.play: That you are foold, discarded, and shooke off

180.play: By him, for whom these shames ye underwent?

181.play: No, yet time serves, wherein you may redeeme

182.play: Your banisht honors, and restore your selves
notes: banisht] SINGER (Text of Sh. Vindicated, 1853): To redeem tarnish’d honours [see Textual Notes] would be a singular mode of expression.

183.play: Into the good thoughts of the world againe

184.play: Revenge the jeering and disdaind contempt
notes: disdaind] JOHNSON (ed 1765): Disdainful—ABBOTT (Sh Gram., 1870, p 273): In the present passage we can only say that, in the excessive use of this license, -ed is loosely employed for -ful, -ing, or some other suffix.

185.play: Of this proud king, who studies day and night

186.play: To answere all the debt he owes to you,

187.play: Even with the bloudie paiment of your deaths:

189.play: WORCESTER: Peace coosen, say no more.

190.play: And now I will unclaspe a secret booke,
notes: unclaspe a secret booke] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): This alludes to what he had said before of unclasping a secret book.

191.play: And to your quicke conceiving discontents

192.play: Ile reade you matter deepe and daungerous,

193.play: As full of perill and adventerous spirit,

194.play: As to orewalke a Current roring lowd,
notes: Current] THOMAS GRAY (MS note in his copy of Theobald’s 2nd ed of Shakespeare): This line seems to belong to Worcester, Hotspur being quite abstracted.

195.play: On the unstedfast footing of a speare
notes: footing] THEOBALD (Letter to Warburton, Jan 13, 1729/30): I presume the passage alludes to the custom of fording of currents, of keeping themselves up by spears or poles. WARBURTON (ed. 1747): That is, of a spear laid across.—DOUCE (Illust. of Sh., 1807, i, 420): Reflection of the “perilous bridge” (e. g., “sword-bridge”) of mediaeval romantic literature.

196.play: HOTSPUR: If he fall in, god-night, or sinke, or swim,

197.play: Send danger from the East unto the West.

198.play: So honor crosse it, from the North to South,

199.play: And let them grapple: O the bloud more stirs

200.play: To rouse a lyon than to start a hare.

201.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Imagination of some great exploit

202.play: Drives him beyond the bounds of patience.
notes: patience] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): A great number of words are dissolved in pronouncing, and made a syllable more than their common measure, a dozen or more in the present scene.

203.play: By heaven me thinkes it were an easie leape,
notes: JOHNSON (ed. 1765): This sally of Hotspur’s may be soberly and rationally vindicated as the violent eruption of a mind inflated with ambition and fired with resentment. WARTON (Var. ed. 1773, Appendix, vol. x): Probably a passage from some bombast play, afterwards used as a common burlesque phrase for attempting impossibilities.

204.play: To plucke bright honour from the palefac'd moone,

205.play: Or dive into the bottome of the deepe,

206.play: Where fadome line could never touch the ground,

207.play: And plucke up drowned honour by the locks,

notes: corrivall] SCHMIDT (1874) gives only two instances of the use of this word by Shakespeare, both in this play Here the word signifies rival, in IV, iv, 31, companion

209.play: But out upon this halfe fac’t fellowship.
notes: halfe fac’t] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): The image is taken from one who turns his face from another. MASON (Com. on the Last Edition, 1785). The image appears to be taken from medals struck to commemorate any signal event or great action... When two persons have had an equal share, they are both delineated, frequently in profile. This is the half-faced fellowship which Hotspur disdains.

210.play: WORCESTER: He apprehends a world of figures here,
notes: apprehend] WRIGHT (ed 1897): Conceives in his imagination Cf. Midsummer Night’s Dream, V, 1, 4: “Lovers and madmen have such seething brains.”

211.play: But not the forme of what he should attend,

212.play: Good coosen give me audience for a while. 212a: HOTSPUR: I crie you mercie. 213a: WORCESTER: Those same noble Scots that are your prisoners 213b: HOTSPUR: Ile keepe them all;

214.play: By God he shall not have a Scot of them,
notes: Scot] DAVIES (Dram. Misc., 1785): A proverbial expression meaning he shall not have the most worthless thing I have.—COWL (ed. 1914): Possibly a quibbling allusion to the saying cited in Fuller’s Worthies and Ray’s Proverbs: “We will not lose a Scot,” i. e. “anything which we can save or recover.”

215.play: No, if a Scot would save his soule he shall not 216a: Ile keepe them by this hand. 216b: WORCESTER: You, start away,

217.play: And lend no eare unto my purposes: 218a: Those prisoners you shall keepe. 218b: HOTSPUR: Nay I will. thats flat:

219.play: He said he would not ransome Mortimer,

220.play: Forbad my tongue to speake of Mortimer,

221.play: But I will find him when he lies asleepe,

222.play: And in his eear ile hollow Mortimer: 223/224: Nay, ile have a starling shalbe taught to speake

225.play: Nothing but Mortimer, and give it him

226.play: To keepe his anger still in motion.

227.play: WORCESTER: Heare you cosen a word.

228.play: HOTSPUR: All studies here I solemnly defie,
notes: defie] SCHMIDT (1874): Renounce.

229.play: Save how to gall and pinch this Bullenbrooke,

230.play: And that same sword and buckler Prince of Wales,
notes: sword and buckler] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): A Royster, or turbulent fellow that fought in the taverns, or raised disorders in the streets, was called a swash-buckler. HENLEY (ed Steevens, 1793): Stowe’s Survey of London: "The field called West-Smithfield was formerly called Ruffians’ Hall, by reason that it was the usual place of frays and common fighting during the time that swords and bucklers were in use."

231.play: But that I thinke his father loves him not,

232.play: And would be glad he met with some mischance

233.play: I would have him poisoned with a pot of ale
notes: poisoned with a pot of ale] STEEVENS (Var ed 1778): This has reference to the low company, drinkers of ale, with whom the prince spent so much time in the meanest taverns

234.play: WORCESTER: Farewel kinsman, ile talke to you
notes: Farewell] WALKER (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 136): Often metrically a trisyllabic word. Cf Folio readings of V, iv, 87 and IV, iii, 111.

235.play: When you are better temperd to attend

236.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Why what a waspe-stung and impatient foole
notes: waspe-stung] STEEVENS (Var ed 1778). That Shakespeare knew that the sting of a wasp is not situated in its mouth may be learned from Winter’s Tale, I, ii, 329. MASON (Com on Last Edition, 1785) Wasp-tongu’d would be good sense, for a peevish scold is frequently called a wasp.

237.play: Art thou? to breake into this womans moode,

238.play: Tying thine eare to no toung but thine owne?

239.play: HOTSPUR: Why looke you, I am whipt and scourg'd with rods,

240.play: Netled, and stung with pismires, when I heare
notes: Netled] Schmidt (1874). Stung as with nettles, fretted.
polititian] Wright (ed 1897): A political schemer, not the same as a statesman, in Shakespeare’s time, used in a deprecatory sense.

242.play: In Richards time, what do you call the place?

243.play: A plague upon it, it is in Glocestershire:

244.play: Twas where the mad-cap duke his uncle kept
notes: mad-cap] Wright (ed. 1897): It is not evident why Shakespeare applies this epithet to the Duke of York. Perhaps he intends it to be only part of Hotspur’s random language. Cowl (ed. 1914): The Duke of York is described by Holinshed as "a man rather coueting to live in pleasure than to deale with much businesse."

245.play: His uncle Yorke, where I first bowed my knee
notes: Yorke] Edmund Langley, fifth son of Edward III.

246.play: Unto this king of smiles, this Bullenbrooke. 247/248: Zbloud, when you and he came backe from Ravensipurgh. 249/250: NORTHUMBERLAND: At Barkly castle. HOTSPUR: You say true.

251.play: Why what a candy deale of curtesie,
notes: candy deale] SINGER (ed. 1826): Deal of candy. N. E. D. (1893) reads caudie in this passage and gives no definition or etymology. Nor does it cite similar use of candy.

252.play: This fawning greyhound then did profer me,
notes: greyhound] GREY (Crit. Notes, 1754): Spaniel would have been more proper; cf. Antony and Cleopatra, IV, xii, 27. The king gave his opinion on the part of the greyhound; because, said he, it hath the good nature of the spaniel without the fawning.

253.play: Looke when his infant fortune came to age,

254.play: And gentle Harry Percy, and kind coosen:

255.play: O the divill take such coosoners, god forgive me,

256.play: Good uncle tell your tale, I have done.
notes: WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The folio reading mends the verse, but is less in keeping with Hotspur’s abrupt manner.

257.play: WORCESTER: Nay, if you have not, to it againe, 258a: We wil stay your leisure 258b: HOTSPUR: I have done Isaith.

259.play: WORCESTER: Then once more to your Scottish prisoners,

260.play: Deliver them up without their ransome straight,

261.play: And make the Douglas sonne your only meane
notes: Douglas sonne] Worcester is here planning out a conspiracy to his nephew and brother.

262.play: For Powers in Scotland, which for divers reasons

263.play: Which I shall send you written, be assur'd

264.play: Wil easely be granted you my Lord.
notes: granted you my Lord] THEOBALD (ed. 1733): I have changed the pointing. Worcester is here planning out a conspiracy... At the end of the play, the king sends his son John with an army “to meet Northumberland and the prelate Scroop.”

265.play: Your sonne in Scotland being thus emploied,

266.play: Shal secretly into the bosome creepe

267.play: Of that same noble prelat welbelou'd,

269.play: HOTSPUR: Of Yorke, is it not?

271.play: His brothers death at Bristow the lord Scroop,

272.play: I speake not this in estimation,
notes: estimation] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): Estimation for conjecture. JOHNSON (ed. 1765): If the editor had... read ten lines forward, he would have seen that nothing is omitted. Worcester gives a dark hint of a conspiracy.

273.play: As what I thinke might be, but what I know

274.play: Is ruminated, plotted, and set downe,

275.play: And onely staies but to behold the face

276.play: Of that occasion that shal bring it on.

277.play: HOTSPUR: I smell it Upon my life it will do well.
notes: do well] SINGER (ed. 1856): This line has more of the spirit of Hotspur in it, and more nerve, without the word wondrous.

278.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Before the game is afoote thou still letst slip
notes: still] SCHMIDT (1874): Always, constantly.

279.play: HOTSPUR: Why, it cannot chuse but be a noble plot,

280.play: And then the power of Scotland, and of Yorke, 281a: To joine with Mortimer, ha. 281b: WORCESTER: And so they shall.

282.play: HOTSPUR: In faith it is exceedingly well aimd.

283.play: WORCESTER: And tis no little reason bids us speed,

284.play: To save our heads by raising of a head,
notes: head] JOHNSON (ed. 1765). A body of forces.

285.play: For beare our selves as even as we can,

286.play: The king will alwaies thinke him in our debt,

287.play: And thinke we thinke our selves unsatisfied,

288.play: Till he hath found a time to pay us home
notes: pay us home] N. E. D. (1909): Pay home: chastise, punish.

289.play: And fee already how he doth begin

290.play: To make us strangers to his lookes of love

291.play: HOTSPUR: He does, he does, weele be reveng'd on him.

292.play: WORCESTER: Coosen farewell. No further go in this,
notes: Coosen] MALONE (ed. 1790): A common address, in our author’s time, to nephews, nieces, and grandchildren.

293.play: Then I by letters shall direct your course

294.play: When time is ripe, which will be suddenly,

295.play: Ile steale to Glendower, and Lo: Mortimer,
notes: Lo:] COLLIER (ed. 1858): We find a line distinctly printed in the quarto of 1598 [Q1]: “I'll steale to Glendower and Lo: Mortimer”: Lo: being a common abbreviation for Lord. Drawing on the provided sources, I have performed a line-by-line audit of the Notes for 1 Henry IV Act 2, Scene 1. In the original Notes, many line numbers were incorrect (often off by one or two digits). I have identified each keyword, located it in the provided Play Text (lines 1–98), and corrected the line numbers accordingly. No words in the text or notes have been changed.

296.play: Where you and Douglas, and our powres at once,

297.play: As I will fashion it shall happily meete,

298.play: To beare our fortunes in our own strong armes,

299.play: Which now we hold at much uncertainty.

300.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Farewell good brother, we shall thrive I trust.

301.play: HOTSPUR: Uncle adieu: O let the houres be short,

302.play: Till fields, and blowes, and grones, applaud our sport. Exeunt

1.play: [Enter a Carrier with a lantern in his hand]
notes: GENTLEMAN (Introd. to Bell’s ed., 1773): We have no objection to Act One but that unnatural union of the contrast sisters Tragedy and Comedy; however, they agree in this piece better than in many of the others. . . If any solid apology can be offered for the unnatural Tragi-comic mode of writing, the richness of humour in many scenes may stand for justification; but this

2.play: First Carrier: Heigh-ho! an it be not four by the day, I'll be
notes: by the day] Cowl. (ed. 1914): Approaching day, towards morning, cf. Chaucer, "by the morwe" ... "By the day" is an archaic expression introduced, perhaps, to mark the rustic speech of the carriers. ... Mr. Craig suggested to me that it may be a parallel expression to "by the clock," meaning "to judge by the appearance of the day."

3.play: hanged: Charles' wain is over the new chimney, and
notes: Charles waine] Ritson (Remarks, 1783): The vulgar name given to the constellation called the Bear. A corruption of the Chorles or Churl’s (Saxon, ceorl or countryman) Wain [wagon].

4.play: yet our horse not packed. What, ostler!

6.play: First Carrier: I prithee, Tom, beat Cut's saddle, put a few flocks in the point; poor jade, is wrung in the withers out
notes: Cuts] Schmidt (1874): A docked or gelded horse—Cowl. (ed. 1914): A pet name frequently given to a "curtal" or docked horse. ... Greene, Life ... of Ned Browne (ed Grosart, xi, pp. 17, 18): "I could ride him one part of the day like a goodly gelding with a large tail ... and the other part of the day I could make him a Cut, for I had an artificial tail so cunningly counterfeited that the ostler when he dressed him could not perceive it."
flockes ... point] Hudson (ed. 1880): Flakes, or locks, of wool under the point of the saddle.

9.play: Second Carrier: Peas and beans are as dank here as a dog, and that
notes: dank] POPE (ed. 1723): Wet and rotten.

10.play: is the next way to give poor jades the bots: this
notes: next] SCHMIDT (1874): Nearest, quickest.

11.play: house is turned upside down since Robin Ostler died.

12.play: First Carrier: Poor fellow, never joyed since the price of oats

13.play: rose; it was the death of him.

14.play: Second Carrier: I think this be the most villanous house in all
notes: be] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 211): Be is used, as a rule, with some notion of doubt, question, or thought. [See also note on II, iv, 495.—Ed.]

15.play: London road for fleas: I am stung like a tench.
notes: stung . . . Tench] STEEVENS (MS. note by Steevens in his own copy of Var. '73, now in the Folger Shakespeare Library). A tench is spotted all over with red marks, like flea-bites.—IBID. (Var. ed 1803) It appears from the following passage in Holland's translation of Pliny's Natural History, IX, xlvi, that anciently fishes were supposed to be infested with fleas “What is ther not bred within the sea? Even the very fleas that skip so merrily in summer-time . . . are there engendered . . . and this vermin is thought to trouble the poor fishes in their sleep.”—CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 700): Shakespeare here uses, as in dank as a dog (line 8), the kind of meaningless comparison that has often been popular in colloquial English.—ELLACOMBE (Sh. as Angler, 1881): Probably refers to the popular notion that the tench, in sucking from each other the slimy substance secreted on their scales, were biting each other.

16.play: First Carrier: Like a tench! by the mass, there is ne'er a king

17.play: christen could be better bit than I have been since

19.play: Second Carrier: Why, they will allow us ne'er a jordan, and then we
