notes: hitherto] MALONE (Suppl. to Var. ed. 1778, 1780): I. e., to this spot, pointing to the map. [It is strange that no editors have adopted Capell’s almost essential stage-direction at this line.—ED.]

77.play: By south and east is to my part assign'd:

78.play: All westward, Wales beyond the Severn shore,

79.play: And all the fertile land within that bound,

80.play: To Owen Glendower: and, dear coz, to you

81.play: The remnant northward, lying off from Trent.

82.play: And our indentures tripartite are drawn;
notes: drawn] THEOBALD (Letter to Warburton, Jan. 13, 1729/30): I think this should be drawing, for Percy [line 138] asks “Are the indentures drawn?” Upon which Glendower says: “I’ll haste the writer.” Nay, Mortimer himself says [line 221]: “By this time will our book be drawn.” After which [line 261] Glendower says: “By this our book is drawn.”

84.play: A business that this night may execute,

85.play: To-morrow, cousin Percy, you and I

86.play: And my good Lord of Worcester will set forth

87.play: To meet your father and the Scottish power,

88.play: As is appointed us, at Shrewsbury.
notes: Shrewsbury] WALKER (Crit. Exam., 1860, i, 158): Shrew- is to be pronounced shrow-, cf Taming of the Shrew, IV, i: “He that knows better how to tame a shrew, Now let him speak; ’tis charity to shew.” Ibid., V, ii: “Your husband being troubled with a shrew, Measures my husband’s sorrow by his woe.” Ibid., concluding couplet of the play: “shrew (folio shrow) ... so.” ... Shrewsbury is still pronounced by some Shrowsbury.

89.play: My father Glendower is not ready yet,

90.play: Not shall we need his help these fourteen days.

91.play: Within that space you may have drawn together

92.play: Your tenants, friends and neighbouring gentlemen.

93.play: GLENDOWER: A shorter time shall send me to you, lords:

94.play: And in my conduct shall your ladies come;

95.play: From whom you now must steal and take no leave,

96.play: For there will be a world of water shed
notes: For] WALKER (Crit. Exam., 1860, ii, 322) supports his conjectural emendation with the following parallels: “Come, wife, let’s in and learn to govern better; For (read Or) England yet may curse my wretched reign” (II Henry VI, IV, ix, 48); “These quicksands, Lepidus, Keep off them for (read or) you sink” (Antony and Cleopatra, II, vii, 58); “Keep up your bright swords, for (or) the dew will rust them” (Othello, I, ii, 59).—ELTON (ed. 1889): In neither case is Walker’s or necessary. For or else, as in Antony and Cleopatra, II, vii, 58.

97.play: Upon the parting of your wives and you.

98.play: Methinks my moiety, north from Burton here,
notes: moity] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): Hotspur is here just such a divider as the Irishman who made three halves: Therefore, for the honour of Shakespeare, I will suppose with the Oxford editor [Hanmer] that he wrote portion.—JOHNSON (ed. 1765): I will not suppose it.—EDWARDS (Canons of Crit., 1748, p. 12): If it were not for losing that foolish book-jest about the Irishman, these editors might just as well have supposed that Shakespeare used moiety for portion, or share in general, for he has so used it in King Lear [I, i, 5]: “Curiosity

99.play: In quantity equals not one of yours:

100.play: See how this river comes me cranking in,

101.play: And cuts me from the best of all my land

102.play: A huge half-moon, a monstrous cantle out.

103.play: I'll have the current in this place damm'd up;

104.play: And here the smug and silver Trent shall run
notes: smug] SCHMIDT (1874): Trim, spruce.—COWL (ed. 1914): Smooth-flowing. Minsheu: “Smugge...to be smooth.” Cf. “smugge and smothe” in More’s Utopia, The Epistle (trans. R. Robinson).—N. E. D. (1919): Smooth, neat, tidy; later, having an appearance of complacency. [This passage cited as early.]

105.play: In a new channel, fair and evenly;

106.play: It shall not wind with such a deep indent,

107.play: To rob me of so rich a bottom here.

108.play: GLENDOWER: Not wind? it shall, it must; you see it doth.

110.play: Mark how he bears his course, and runs me up

111.play: With like advantage on the other side;

112.play: Gelding the opposed continent as much
notes: opposed continent] COWL (ed. 1914): The opposite bank. Continent is used of anything that can be said to hold or contain something else.

113.play: As on the other side it takes from you.

114.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: Yea, but a little charge will trench him here
notes: charge] SCHMIDT (1874): Expense, cost.

115.play: And on this north side win this cape of land;

116.play: And then he runs straight and even.

117.play: HOTSPUR: I'll have it so: a little charge will do it.

118.play: GLENDOWER: I'll not have it alter'd.

120.play: GLENDOWER: No, nor you shall not.

121.play: HOTSPUR: Who shall say me nay?

123.play: HOTSPUR: Let me not understand you, then; speak it in Welsh.

124.play: GLENDOWER: I can speak English, lord, as well as you;
notes: Lord] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Glendower is losing patience with Hotspur and becomes formal. He no longer calls him “cousin Percy”

[See Holinshed: Appendix: Sources of the Plot ]—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): The real name of Owen Glendower was Vaughan, and he was originally a barrister of the Middle Temple.—MALONE (ed. 1790): He took the name Glyndour from the lordship of Glyndourdwy of which he was owner. He was particularly adverse to the Mortimers because Edmund, Earl of Mortimer, was rightfully entitled to the principality of Wales, as well as to the crown of England, being lineally descended from Gladys, daughter of Llewellyn, and sister of David, Prince of Wales, who died in 1246. Glendower became squire of the body to Richard II, with whom he was in attendance at Flint Castle when Richard was taken prisoner by Bolingbroke. Glendower was crowned Prince of Wales in 1402, and for near twelve years was a formidable enemy of the English. He died in great distress in 1415.

MALONE (ed. 1790): Dr Johnson’s explanation is the true one.—COWL (ed. 1914): Gracing the words of the ditty with a musical accompaniment . . It may be added that “tongue” in the sense of language is a favorite word with the Welsh speakers in the dramatists. Thus Dekker and Webster, Northward Hoe, ii: “Mee thinks a Welshman’s tongue is the neatest tongue!” and Dekker, Patent Grissel, ii, 1: “Sir Owen. . . . Welsh tongue is finer as Greek tongue.”
JACKSON (Examples of Errors, 1818): A lash at Hotspur who had a defect in his speech; see II Henry IV, II, iii, 24.

125.play: For I was train'd up in the English court;

126.play: Where, being but young, I framed to the harp

127.play: Many an English ditty lovely well

128.play: And gave the tongue a helpful ornament,
notes: tongue] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): The English language.—RITSON (Remarks

129.play: A virtue that was never seen in you.

131.play: And I am glad of it with all my heart:

132.play: I had rather be a kitten and cry mew

133.play: Than one of these same metre ballad-mongers;
notes: miter ballet mongers] COWL (ed. 1914): Hotspur, in contempt, calls poets writers of doggerel ballads [Dr. J Q ADAMS suggests that the allusion is to ballad-singers. “Ballad-mongers sang their ballads on the streets, to sell them. Glendower sang to the harp, and criticizes Percy’s inability not to write poetry, but to sing.” But it is “mincing poetry,” not song, to which Hotspur refers in line 131.—Ed.]

134.play: I had rather hear a brazen canstick turn'd,
notes: cansticke] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Candlestick, which destroys the harmony of the line, is written canstick in Q1-3, and so it might have been pronounced Heywood and several of the old writers constantly spell it that way.—COWL (ed. 1914): Stow, Survey of London: “founders that cast candlesticks . . . and afterwards turne them to make them bright with turning and scrating: . . . make a lothesome noise to the by-passers”

135.play: Or a dry wheel grate on the axle-tree;

136.play: And that would set my teeth nothing on edge,

137.play: Nothing so much as mincing poetry:
notes: mincing] SCHMIDT (1874): Mince: to walk in a prim and affected manner. Used [here] metaphorically—COWL (ed. 1914): Cf. W. Vaughan, The Golden Grove, 1600, ii, 42: “Sundry times haue I beene conuersant with such as blasphemed Poetry, by calling it mincing and lyng Poetry.”

138.play: 'Tis like the forced gait of a shuffling nag.

139.play: GLENDOWER: Come, you shall have Trent turn'd.

140.play: HOTSPUR: I do not care: I'll give thrice so much land

142.play: But in the way of bargain, mark ye me,

143.play: I'll cavil on the ninth part of a hair.

144.play: Are the indentures drawn? shall we be gone?

145.play: GLENDOWER: The moon shines fair; you may away by night:

146.play: I'll haste the writer and withal
notes: withal] SCHMIDT (1874): At the same time.

147.play: Break with your wives of your departure hence:

148.play: I am afraid my daughter will run mad,

149.play: So much she doteth on her Mortimer.

151.play: MORTIMER: Fie, cousin Percy! how you cross my father!

152.play: HOTSPUR: I cannot choose: sometime he angers me

153.play: With telling me of the mouldwarp and the ant,

154.play: Of the dreamer Merlin and his prophecies,

155.play: And of a dragon and a finless fish,
notes: finless fish] COWL (ed 1914): This sarcasm may have been suggested by Merlin's prophecy that "the Lion's whelps shall be transformed into fishes of the sea."

156.play: A clip-wing'd griffin and a moulten raven,

157.play: A couching lion and a ramping cat,
notes: couching, ramping] COWL (ed. 1914): Burlesque of the heraldic terms couchant, lying down, and rampant, rearing.

158.play: And such a deal of skimble-skamble stuff
notes: skmble scamble] [This word seems to have been coined by Shakespeare for Hotspur's use. The N E. D. defines it as nonsensical, and records no prior use of it.—ED.]

159.play: As puts me from my faith. I tell you what;
notes: puts ... faith] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Makes me forget I'm a Christian.—COWL (ed. 1914): Makes me incredulous. Cf. Heywood, Fair Maid, II, i: "To put my friends from patience," i.e. to make them impatient.

160.play: He held me last night at least nine hours

161.play: In reckoning up the several devils' names

162.play: That were his lackeys: I cried 'hum,' and 'well, go to,'
notes: go to] RITSON (Remarks...on the Last Ed, 1783): These two senseless monosyllables seem to have been added by some foolish player, purposely to destroy the measure. [See Textual Notes. Pope and his followers took every opportunity of reducing Hotspur’s distinctive style to a level of “regular” mediocrity.—Ed.]—N. E. D. (1901): Go to: used to introduce a contemptuous concession.

163.play: But mark'd him not a word. O, he is as tedious

164.play: As a tired horse, a railing wife;

165.play: Worse than a smoky house: I had rather live

166.play: With cheese and garlic in a windmill, far,

167.play: Than feed on cates and have him talk to me
notes: cates] SCHMIDT (1874): Dainties.

summer house] COWL (ed. 1914): Men of wealth had their summer houses, that is country houses, pleasantly situated, spacious and airy.... They were costly luxuries, and fortunes were squandered on them. See Webster, Westward Hoe, I, i: “Your prodigality, your dicing, ... your building a summer-house, hath undone us!”

169.play: MORTIMER: In faith, he is a worthy gentleman,

171.play: In strange concealments, valiant as a lion

172.play: And as wondrous affable and as bountiful

173.play: As mines of India. Shall I tell you, cousin?

174.play: He holds your temper in a high respect

175.play: And curbs himself even of his natural scope

176.play: When you come 'cross his humour; faith, he does:

177.play: I warrant you, that man is not alive

178.play: Might so have tempted him as you have done,

179.play: Without the taste of danger and reproof:

180.play: But do not use it oft, let me entreat you.

181.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: In faith, my lord, you are too wilful-blame;

182.play: And since your coming hither have done enough
notes: hither] Walker (Sh. Vers., 1854, pp. 105, 106): In numerous passages

183.play: To put him quite beside his patience.

184.play: You must needs learn, lord, to amend this fault:

185.play: Though sometimes it show greatness, courage, blood,
notes: bloud] SCHMIDT (1874): High temper, mettle.

186.play: And that's the dearest grace it renders you,

187.play: Yet oftentimes it doth present harsh rage,

188.play: Defect of manners, want of government,

notes: opinion] MASON (ed. Malone, 1790): Self-opinion, conceit.

190.play: The least of which haunting a nobleman
notes: haunting] WALKER (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 242): Having is very commonly pronounced as a monosyllable.

191.play: Loseth men's hearts and leaves behind a stain

192.play: Upon the beauty of all parts besides,

194.play: HOTSPUR: Well, I am school'd: good manners be your speed!

195.play: Here come our wives, and let us take our leave.
notes: Here . . . wives] AX (Sh. Rel. to Hol., 1912, p 34): The presence of the ladies, dramatic as it may be, we consider unlikely; for Bangor was a place where none of the lords dwelt, and where important political negotiations were to be transacted.

197.play: MORTIMER: This is the deadly spite that angers me;

198.play: My wife can speak no English, I no Welsh.

199.play: GLENDOWER: My daughter weeps: she will not part with you;

200.play: She'll be a soldier too, she'll to the wars.

201.play: MORTIMER: Good father, tell her that she and my aunt Percy
notes: Aunt] See note on I, iii, 80.—Capell (Notes, 1779) defends his emendation: Holinshed tells us she was his aunt, but Shakespeare makes her his sister—and that rightly and advisedly; for to have called her "aunt," and (of course) her husband "uncle" would have destroyed that air of youth which he thought proper to throw into both these characters. But, in this scene, the historical truth seems to have taken possession of his imagination.

202.play: Shall follow in your conduct speedily.

203.play: [Glendower speaks to her in Welsh, and she answers him in the same]

204.play: GLENDOWER: She is desperate here; a peevish self-wind harlotry,
notes: [Glen She is desperate here, &c]

peeuiish...harlotrie] RITSON (Remarks...on the Last Ed., 1783): Capulet, in Romeo and Juliet [IV, 11] uses the same expression—WHITE (ed. 1859): Harlotry was used with as little meaning of reproach in Elizabethan times as slut in Queen Anne’s.—N. E. D. (1909): Peevish: Perverse, headstrong.

205.play: one that no persuasion can do good upon.
notes: doe good vpon] COWL (ed. 1914): Reduce to submission. Brome, A Mad Couple, III, 1: “This crosse abusive letter may do good upon her” D’Avenant, News from Plymouth, II: “I have done little good upon him his metal will not bow.”

207.play: MORTIMER: I understand thy looks: that pretty Welsh
notes: [Mort. I understand thy looks, &c.]
prettie Welsh] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): Her tears; and the “parley,” a little lower down, signifies—language.

208.play: Which thou pour'st down from these swelling heavens
notes: swelling heauens] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): The defect of harmony in this line induces me to suppose with SIR T. HANMER that our author originally wrote “these two swelling heavens,” meaning her two prominent lips [!]—DOUCE (Illust., 1807): Rather, eyes swollen with tears. Glendower has just said that she wept; and Mortimer tells her that he would answer the melting language of her eyes, if it were not for shame.—LETTSON (New Readings, 1853): Col-

209.play: I am too perfect in; and, but for shame,
notes: perfect] Cow. (ed. 1914): Proficient.

210.play: In such a parley should I answer thee.

212.play: I understand thy kisses and thou mine,
notes: [Mort. I understand thy kisses, &c.]

feeling disputation] THEOBALD (Letter to Warburton, Jan. 13, 1729/30): "I understand thy kisses . . . and that's a FEEBLE disputation" [see Textual Notes]; but I am sure Mr. Pope did not understand them; nor, I am almost afraid by this feeble epithet, ever understood the joy of a delicious kiss—sit verbo venial Considering how enamoured Mortimer is of his young wife . . . I am confident you will read with me, from the old copies, "feeling disputation"; what can be more tender and expressive!—IBID (ed. 1733): "Though I don't understand your Welsh, I understand the tenderness of your kisses, I feel the force of their argument and moving rhetorick.—N. E. D. (1901): Feeling: Moving, affecting the passions.

214.play: But I will never be a truant, love,

215.play: Till I have learned thy language; for thy tongue

216.play: Makes Welsh as sweet as ditties highly penn'd,
notes: ditties highly pend] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Bacon, in his Essay of Masques, recommends that the ditty be “high and tragical, not nice or dainty.”—N. E. D. (1901): Highly: 4. In high style. 4d. To a high degree of artistic quality or finish [examples of 4. d. from 1715 on].

217.play: Sung by a fair queen in a summer's bower,

218.play: With ravishing division, to her lute.
notes: division] BURNEY (ed. Steevens, 1793): Divisions in vocal musick were very uncommon during the time of Shakespeare.—DYER (Var. ed. 1821): A division in music is a variation of melody upon some given fundamental harmony.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Mr. Naylor (Sh. and Music, p. 28) says, “ ‘Division’ means roughly a brilliant passage of short notes, which is founded essentially on a much simpler passage of longer notes.” See Romeo and Juliet, III, v, 29: “Some say the lark makes sweet division ”

219.play: GLENDOWER: Nay, if you melt, then will she run mad.
notes: [Glen. Nay, if you melt, &c.]
Nay] COLERIDGE (Lect. and Notes, 1818): The “nay,” so to be dwelt on in speaking as to be equivalent to a dissyllable, is characteristic of the solemn Glendower.

221.play: MORTIMER: O, I am ignorance itself in this!

222.play: GLENDOWER: She bids you on the wanton rushes lay you down
notes: She bids you] COLERIDGE (Lect. and Notes, 1818): This imperfect line [see Folio reading in Textual Notes] is one of those fine hair strokes of exquisite judgement peculiar to Shakespeare;—thus detaching the lady’s speech, and giving it the individuality and the entireness of a little poem, while he draws attention to it.—WHITE (Sh. Scholar, 1854): Perhaps such an arrangement would have been a stroke of exquisite judgment had Shakespeare made it; though that is not so obvious to me even after Coleridge’s setting forth; but

wanton] Schmidt (1874) and N. E. D. D. (1928): Luxuriant—Cowl (ed. 1914): Fresh and green; cf. Midsummer Night's Dream, II, i, 99—Ibid (Sources, 1928): The definition "luxuriant" is hardly applicable to cut rushes. rushes] Johnson (ed. 1765): It was the custom in this country, for many ages, to strew the floors with rushes as we now cover them with carpets.

223.play: And rest your gentle head upon her lap,

224.play: And she will sing the song that pleaseth you

225.play: And on your eyelids crown the god of sleep.
notes: eyelids... sleepe] Warburton (ed. 1747): The expression is fine; intimating that the God of sleep should not only sit, but that he should sit crowned, i.e. pleased and delighted.—Steevens (Var. ed. 1778). The same image, whatever it was meant to convey, occurs in Beaumont and Fletcher's Philaster: "Who shall take up his lute, And touch it till he crown a silent sleep Upon my eye-lid"—Malone (ed. 1790): The God of sleep is to sit crowned, i.e. with sovereign dominion. So, in Twelfth Night [V, i, 131]: "Him I will tear out of that cruel eye, Where he sits crowned in his master's spight."—Wright (ed. 1897), accepting Malone's reasonable explanation, remarks: Warburton, as usual, is wide of the mark.

226.play: Charming your blood with pleasing heaviness,

227.play: Making such difference 'twixt wake and sleep

228.play: As is the difference betwixt day and night

229.play: The hour before the heavenly-harness'd team

230.play: Begins his golden progress in the east.

231.play: MORTIMER: With all my heart I'll sit and hear her sing:

232.play: By that time will our book, I think, be drawn
notes: booke] Johnson (ed. 1765): Paper of conditions.—Steevens (Var. ed.

234.play: And those musicians that shall play to you

235.play: Hang in the air a thousand leagues from hence,

236.play: And straight they shall be here: sit, and attend.

237.play: HOTSPUR: Come, Kate, thou art perfect in lying down: come,

238.play: quick, quick, that I may lay my head in thy lap.

239.play: LADY PERCY: Go, ye giddy goose.

241.play: HOTSPUR: Now I perceive the devil understands Welsh;
notes: CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 724): The sly comment put into Hotspur's mouth lets us into the secret that probably the Welsh chieftain has some such instrument as an Aeolian harp placed under the control of one of his people,

242.play: And 'tis no marvel he is so humorous.
notes: ELTON (ed. 1889): If we read a comma after marvel, the sense is, “No wonder he knows Welsh—he’s humorous (capricious) enough for anything.” The sense without the comma is better, “No wonder he’s humorous if he knows Glendower’s language.”

243.play: By'r lady, he is a good musician.

244.play: LADY PERCY: Then should you be nothing but musical for you are

245.play: altogether governed by humours. Lie still, ye thief,

246.play: and hear the lady sing in Welsh.

247.play: HOTSPUR: I had rather hear Lady, my brach, howl in Irish.
notes: lady . . . brache] DYCE (Remarks on Collier . . . , 1844): Is it possible that Mr. Collier could suppose that lady was an address to Lady Percy? [See

248.play: LADY PERCY: Wouldst thou have thy head broken?

notes: JOHNSON (ed. 1765): I do not plainly see what is a woman’s fault.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): A proverbial expression. I find it in The Birth of Merlin, 1662: “‘Tis a woman’s fault: pox on this bashfulness” I believe the meaning is this: Hotspur, having declared his resolution neither to have his head broken, nor to sit still, silly adds that such is the usual fault of women; i.e., never to do what they are told to do.—*AMNER (Suppl. to Var. ed 1778, 1780): The whole tenour of Hotspur’s conversation here shows that the stillness which he here imputes to women as a fault was something very different from silence; and that an idea was couched under these words which may be better understood than explained—He is still in the Welsh lady’s bed-chamber.—BOSWELL (Var. ed 1821): Without attempting to penetrate Mr. White’s [i.e. Mr. Amner’s] occult meaning, it may be questioned whether there is any ground for supposing that this scene takes place in the Welsh lady’s bed-chamber. [Mr. “Amner” is obviously thinking of line 240, and is perhaps

252.play: LADY PERCY: Now God help thee!

253.play: HOTSPUR: To the Welsh lady's bed.

256.play: [Here the lady sings a Welsh song]
notes: s.d. welsh song] HARRIES (Sh. and the Welsh, 1919, p. 138): We would give much to learn what the song was, and whether it was accompanied by the harp. Brinley Richards, in his Songs of Wales, tells us . . . there was a revival of the harp in the days of Elizabeth. A celebrated Welsh harpist, Thomas Pritchard . . . died in London in 1597.

257.play: HOTSPUR: Come, Kate, I'll have your song too.

258.play: LADY PERCY: Not mine, in good sooth.

259.play: HOTSPUR: Not yours, in good sooth! Heart! you swear like a

260.play: comfit-maker's wife. 'Not you, in good sooth,' and

261.play: 'as true as I live,' and 'as God shall mend me,' and

263.play: And givest such sarcenet surety for thy oaths,
notes: sarcenet surety] SCHMIDT (1874): Confirmation, or warrant, of fine woven silk, such as becomes a merchant's wife—WRIGHT (ed 1897): Sarcenet, said to be sericum Saracenum, was a finely woven silk, so thin as to be transparent, and therefore insubstantial

264.play: As if thou never walk'st further than Finsbury.
notes: further] WALKER (Crit. Exam. of Text, 1860, i, 189) cites examples of the use of far and near as comparatives, e.g., Macbeth, II, iii: “The near in blood, The nearer bloody.”—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 347): Some degree of softening, though not expressed in writing, seems to have affected th in the following words: brother, further, neither, rather, thither, whither.

265.play: Swear me, Kate, like a lady as thou art,

266.play: A good mouth-filling oath, and leave 'in sooth,'

notes: protest . . . bread] MALONE (ed. 1790): Such protestations as are uttered by the makers of gingerbread—HENLEY (ed. Steevens, 1793): Hotspur had just told his wife she swore like a comfit-maker’s wife. [Is it not that the “protests” themselves are characterized as made of gingerbread?—Ed.]

velvet guards] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Such as have their clothes adorned with shreds of velvet, which was, I suppose, the finery of Cockneys—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): “Cloaks, doublets, &c. (says Stubbs in his Anatomie of Abuses) were guarded with velvet guards, or else laced with costly lace.” . . . Velvet guards, however, seem to have been a city fashion. So, in Histriomastix, 1610: “Nay, I myself will wear the courtly grace: Out on these velvet guards.”

270.play: LADY PERCY: I will not sing.

271.play: HOTSPUR: 'Tis the next way to turn tailor, or be red-breast
notes: next] See II, i, 9, note. Drawing on the provided sources, I have conducted a line-by-line audit of the Play Text and Notes for 1 Henry IV Act 3, Scene 2. I have identified the keywords in each note, located them within the provided text (lines 1–185), and updated the line numbers in the notes to match their exact placement. Each sentence in this response draws from the provided material and includes the required citation.

272.play: teacher. An the indentures be drawn, I'll away

273.play: within these two hours; and so, come in when ye will.

275.play: GLENDOWER: Come, come, Lord Mortimer; you are as slow

276.play: As hot Lord Percy is on fire to go.

277.play: By this our book is drawn; we'll but seal,

1.play: [Enter KING HENRY IV, PRINCE HENRY, and others]

2.play: KING HENRY IV: Lords, give us leave; the Prince of Wales and I
notes: leave, ] SIMPSON (Sh. Punctuation, 1911, p. 69): As if to call attention to the pause, the Folio sometimes even breaks a line in half, giving one blank verse as two.

3.play: Must have some private conference; but be near at hand,

4.play: For we shall presently have need of you.

6.play: I know not whether God will have it so,

7.play: For some displeasing service I have done,

8.play: That, in his secret doom, out of my blood
notes: doom] SCHMIDT (1874): Decree, judgment.

9.play: He'll breed revengement and a scourge for me;

10.play: But thou dost in thy passages of life

11.play: Make me believe that thou art only mark'd

12.play: For the hot vengeance and the rod of heaven

13.play: To punish my mistreadings. Tell me else,
notes: punish my misreadings] DAVIES (Dram. Misc 1785): The poet makes Henry keep his great offences to his sovereign constantly in mind; and his continual compunction and remorse lessen our hatred to the usurper [The king seems to imply that part of his punishment is the vengeance to be taken by heaven on his son —Ed.].

14.play: Could such inordinate and low desires,

15.play: Such poor, such bare, such lewd, such mean attempts,
notes: bare] SCHMIDT (1874): Lean, poor.—WALKER (Crit. Exam. of Text, 1860, i, 279) gives ten examples of the possible confusion of bare with base [cf. I, iii, 108].

17.play: As thou art match'd withal and grafted to,

18.play: Accompany the greatness of thy blood

19.play: And hold their level with thy princely heart?

20.play: PRINCE HENRY: So please your majesty, I would I could

21.play: Quit all offences with as clear excuse

22.play: As well as I am doubtless I can purge
notes: I am doubtless] Wright (ed. 1897): I have no doubt.

23.play: Myself of many I am charged withal:

24.play: Yet such extenuation let me beg,

25.play: As, in reproof of many tales devised,
notes: in reproofe] Johnson (ed. 1765): I should read on, but concerning Shakespeare’s particles there is no certainty.—Cowl (ed. 1914): Change is needless, for in sometimes equals on, as in “in earth as it is in heaven.”—Mason (Comm. on the Last Ed., 1785): In disproof—Wright (ed. 1897): In consideration of my being able to disprove.

26.play: which oft the ear of greatness needs must hear,

27.play: By smiling pick-thanks and base news-mongers,
notes: By] Mason (1785): By refers to dews’d, two lines above.

28.play: I may, for some things true, wherein my youth

notes: irregular] Schmidt (1874): Lawless. [Cf. I, i, 40.].

30.play: Find pardon on my true submission.

31.play: KING HENRY IV: God pardon thee! yet let me wonder, Harry,

32.play: At thy affections, which do hold a wing
notes: affections] Cowl (ed. 1914): Inclinations, propensities; as in Edward II, I, iv: “thou corrupt’st my lord And art a bawd to his affections.”—N. E. D. (1888): Feelings, passions, disposition. Romans, i, 26: “God gave them up to vile affections.”.

33.play: Quite from the flight of all thy ancestors.

34.play: Thy place in council thou hast rudely lost.

35.play: Which by thy younger brother is supplied,

36.play: And art almost an alien to the hearts

37.play: Of all the court and princes of my blood:

38.play: The hope and expectation of thy time
notes: hope . . . time] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): What was expected of you at this period of your life.—COWL (ed. 1914): The hope and expectation entertained of thy youth; as in Cymbeline, I, i, 43: “Puts to him all the learnings that his time Could make him the receiver of ”; and Edward IV, IV, iv: “Show thy tyme’s learning in this dangerous time.” “Hope and expectation” occurs frequently in North’s Plutarch.

39.play: Is ruin'd, and the soul of every man

notes: doth] DYCE (ed. 1857): Shakespeare may have considered every man (line 39) a plural.—WHITE (ed. 1859): There are too many instances of this construction in our early literature for it to be the result of carelessness or accident.

41.play: Had I so lavish of my presence been,

42.play: So common-hackney'd in the eyes of men,

43.play: So stale and cheap to vulgar company,

44.play: Opinion, that did help me to the crown,
notes: Opinion] SCHMIDT (1874): Public opinion, reputation—COWL (ed. 1914): “Opinion” is constantly used in a deprecatory sense by Elizabethan writers who oppose it to Truth and Reason. Dekker, London Triumphant (Pearson, iii, 254): “Opinion, that ne’er knew What was either good or true.” See Jonson, The Barrers; and Gulpln, Skialetheia (1598), Satyre VI, where Reason is said to be the “soules bright genius,” and Opinion, “Reason’s slave and shadow.”—“It’s but the hisse of geese, the peeples noyse”.

45.play: Had still kept loyal to possession
notes: loyall to possession] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): True to him that had then possession of the crown.

46.play: And left me in reputeless banishment,

47.play: A fellow of no mark nor likelihood.

48.play: By being seldom seen, I could not stir

49.play: But like a comet I was wonder'd at;

50.play: That men would tell their children 'This is he;'

51.play: Others would say 'Where, which is Bolingbroke?'

52.play: And then I stole all courtesy from heaven,
notes: stole...heauen] WARBURTON (Letter to Birch, 1738, Aug 27): An allusion to the famous story of Prometheus’s theft: who stole fire from thence. And as with this he made a man, so with that Bolingbroke made a king. As the gods were supposed to be fond of appropriating reason to themselves, the getting fire from thence, which caused it, was called a theft. And as power is their prerogative, the getting courtesy from thence, by which power is procured, is called a theft. [This note is repeated, substantially, in Warburton’s ed. of 1747, with the following sentence added]: The thought is exquisitely great and beautiful.—CAPELL (Notes, 1779): Excess of courtesy (condescension) in man to his fellows cannot be better illustrated than by that which Heaven is pleased to shew towards him; which is so immense, that the quality may well be considered as peculiar to heaven; and what man shews of it to man, a partial derivation from thence, or a theft.—MALONE (ed. 1790): The poet had not a thought of Prometheus or the heathen gods, nor indeed was courtesy the characteristic of those deities. The meaning, I apprehend, is—I was so affable and.

53.play: And dress'd myself in such humility

54.play: That I did pluck allegiance from men's hearts,

55.play: Loud shouts and salutations from their mouths,

56.play: Even in the presence of the crowned king.

57.play: Thus did I keep my person fresh and new;

58.play: My presence, like a robe pontifical,
notes: roabe pontificall] PECK (Explan. Notes, 1740): He alludes to the gorgeous vestments worn by the Jewish highpriest, which were never brought out . . . but once a year.—DAVIES (Dram. Misc., 1785): A robe such as popes, patriarchs, and archbishops wear at high mass.—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 306): When the noun is unemphatic, the transposition of the adjective, to follow the noun, may be expected. . . . The existence of the robe is taken for granted, the essence of the expression is the transposed adjective. Cf. As You Like It, II, vii, 155: "With eyes severe, and beard of formal cut.".

59.play: Ne'er seen but wonder'd at: and so my state,
notes: state] COWL (ed. 1914): Appearance in state.—N. E. D. (1919): Dignity of presence. Splendour, magnificence.

60.play: Seldom but sumptuous, showed like a feast

61.play: And won by rareness such solemnity.
notes: won] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The earlier form of the preterite.—COWL (ed. 1914): A form of the preterite usual in North, Holland, and other Elizabethan writers.

62.play: The skipping king, he ambled up and down
notes: skipping] SCHMIDT (1874): Thoughtless, flighty, wanton. “All wanton as a child, skipping and vain” Love’s Labour’s Lost, V, ii, 749.

63.play: With shallow jesters and rash bavin wits,
notes: rash bavin wits] PECK (Explan. Notes, 1740): A bavin is a faggot. Such companions as Richard chose . . . are as common as sticks in a faggot, and their wit, though flashy, is as soon exhausted—WARBURTON (ed. 1747): Dry brushwood wits.—JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Rash is heady, thoughtless. Bavin is brushwood, which, fired, burns fiercely, but is soon out.—MALONE (Second Appendix to Supplement, 1783): Rash, I believe, is fierce, violent. So, in Richard II, II, i, 33: “His rash, fierce blaze of riot cannot last,” [applied to the same “skipping king” by Henry’s father, John of Gaunt—ED.]—COW (ed 1914): Lyly, Mother Bombe, IV, i: “Bavins will have their flashes, and youth their fancies; the one as soon quenched as the other burnt.”—J. Q. ADAMS: Possibly an echo of Lyly’s use of the old proverb: “The bavin, though it burn bright, is but a blaze” (Euphues, ed. Bond, i, 218, 32). Shakespeare shows familarity with Lyly in this play.

64.play: Soon kindled and soon burnt; carded his state,
notes: carded] HEATH (Revisal, 1765, p. 252): We have no such English abbreviation as ’scarded [see Textual Notes], nor is it necessary here. The word discarded, at length, may be inserted without prejudice to the metre—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): The metaphor seems to be taken from mingling coarse wool with fine, and carding them together, whereby the value of the latter is diminished . . . A subsequent part of this speech [lines 86, 87] gives a sanction to this explanation. To card is used by other writers for to mix; so . . . Greene’s Quip for an Upstart Courtier: “You card your beer, half small, half strong”; and Bacon’s Natural History: “It is excellent drink . . . either drunk alone or carded with some other beer.” The original hint for this note I received from Mr. Toilet. [Steevens’s explanation is accepted by FARMER (Var. ed. 1778), SINGER (ed. 1856), and DYC (ed 1864) —ED.]—RITSON (Remarks . . . on the Last Ed, 1783): The king means that his predecessor set his consequence to hazard, played it away, as a man loses his fortune, at cards.—MALONE (Second Appendix to Supplement, 1783): Warburton is right [see Textual Notes], but his amendment unnecessary. Shakespeare, who uses quite for requite, draw for withdraw, may fairly be supposed to use carded for discarded. To card wool is not to mingle coarse with fine, but to blend together the fibres in such a manner that they can be spun into a thread; and the word card in this signification cannot form any sense in this passage.—IBID. (ed. 1790): I am unable to throw any light on this difficult passage.—IBID.

The manner in which the corruption occurred is easily comprehensible to any person familiar with the accidents to which standing matter is liable in a printing office. dis was first separated from carded, so that the passage read “carded dis state,” and then the dis was changed to his in the correction of a supposed literal error [No more “fruitless exegetical labour has been expended” upon this passage than the above—Ed.].—N. E. D (1893): Card. To stir and mix with cards, to stir together, to mix Topsell, 1607: “As for his diet, let it be warm mashes,--sodden wheat and hay,--carded with a pair of wool-cards.”—Wright (ed. 1897): Mixed, and so debased, as is explained in the next line. The figure is not taken from the carding of wool, for this is a process by which wool is made finer, but from the mixing or blending of liquors.—Cowl (ed. 1914) gives many examples of the use of card, to debase by mixing; and of card, to comb wool, hence to wear away, waste.

65.play: Mingled his royalty with capering fools,
notes: capering] [See Textual Notes.]—Warton (Appendix, vol. x, Var. ed. 1778) Carping fools, i. e., jesting, prating fools. This word had not yet acquired the sense which it bears in modern speech.—Steevens (Var ed 1778): Cap’ring I believe to be right because it asks no explanation—Malone (ed 1790) and (substantially) Boswell (Var. ed. 1821): Had all the quartos read capring and the folio carping, the latter reading might derive some strength from the authority of that copy; but the change having been made arbitrarily, or by chance, in the quarto of 1599, it has no pretensions of that kind. It may further be observed that “capering fools” were proper companions for a “skipping king.”—Collier (ed. 1842): Carping is perhaps right, as is indicated in the next line... Capering may be the true reading, in reference to Court revels and dances.—Ibid. (ed. 1858): We adopt capering with hesitation.—White (ed. 1859): Scorns [line 66] does not sustain the reading carping; for however fools might carp, they would not carp, or hold up to scorn, their royal patron. His name was profaned with the scorn that attached to theirs.

66.play: Had his great name profaned with their scorns

67.play: And gave his countenance, against his name,
notes: Johnson (ed. 1765): Made his presence injurious to his reputation.—Mason (Comments on the Last Ed., 1785): I doubt the propriety of Dr. Johnson’s explanation, and suppose its meaning to be that he favoured and en-.

68.play: To laugh at gibing boys and stand the push
notes: JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Of every boy whose vanity incited him to try his wit against the king’s. When Lewis XIV was asked why, with so much wit, he never attempted raillery, he answered that he who practised raillery ought to bear it in his turn, and that to stand the butt of raillery was not suitable to the dignity of a king.

70.play: Grew a companion to the common streets,

72.play: That, being daily swallow'd by men's eyes,

73.play: They surfeited with honey and began

74.play: To loathe the taste of sweetness, whereof a little

75.play: More than a little is by much too much.

76.play: So when he had occasion to be seen,

77.play: He was but as the cuckoo is in June,

78.play: Heard, not regarded; seen, but with such eyes

79.play: As, sick and blunted with community,
notes: community] SCHMIDT (1874): Commonness, frequency.

Afford] HEATH (Revisal, 1765, p. 252): The construction of the passage ... shews that the verb should be in the past. ... I conclude, therefore, that the poet might probably have written Offer'd ... i.e., Did not pay the tribute of extraordinary gaze such as is usually paid to majesty when seldom seen—CAPELL (Notes, 1779): [Heath] objects to Afford as not agreeing with the verbs that come after . but there will be few readers that will not rather put up with such a trifling inaccuracy than embrace a word which the ear does not relish and that is defective in ease.—WALKER (Crit. Exam. of Text, 1860, ii, 239) cites this as an example of the use of the present form of verbs ending in t (and sometimes in d) for the past tense.

81.play: Such as is bent on sun-like majesty

82.play: When it shines seldom in admiring eyes;

83.play: But rather drowzed and hung their eyelids down,
notes: drowzed] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The subject is changed from the eyes to the spectators.

84.play: Slept in his face and render'd such aspect
notes: aspect] WRIGHT (ed 1897): With the accent on the last syllable, as always in Shakespeare. See I, i, 97.—COWL (ed. 1914). Used here in its active sense [looks, glances], as in Comedy of Errors, II, ii, 110: "Some other mistress hath thy sweet aspects.".

85.play: As cloudy men use to their adversaries,
notes: cloudy] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Morose, sullen. Cf. Macbeth, III, vi, 41: "The cloudy messenger turns me his back."—COWL (ed. 1914): Topsell, Historie of Foure-Footed Beastes, 1607 (p. 460): "To be cloudy ... betokeneth either anger or sorrow.".

86.play: Being with his presence glutted, gorged and full.
notes: COWL (ed. 1914): Mr. Craig suggests that Shakespeare in this speech was perhaps indebted to a passage in North's Plutarch, Pericles (ed. 1595, p. 170): "Pericles now to prevent that the people should not be glutted with seeing him too oft ... neither came much abroade among them, but reserved himselfe ... for matters of great importance.

87.play: And in that very line, Harry, standest thou;
notes: COURTENAY (Comm on Hist. Plays, 1840): The comparison of Prince Henry with Richard II is very beautiful, but inappropriate Henry at no time displayed any lack of energy, or suffered his love of pleasure to interfere with the active duties in which the greater part of his life was spent.
line] See I, iii, 168, note.

88.play: For thou has lost thy princely privilege

89.play: With vile participation: not an eye

90.play: But is a-weary of thy common sight,
notes: a-weary] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram, 1870, pp. 34, 35): A-, as prefix of adjectives, represents the Anglo-Saxon intensive of. . . . A-weary means of-weary, tired-out.

91.play: Save mine, which hath desired to see thee more;

92.play: Which now doth that I would not have it do,

93.play: Make blind itself with foolish tenderness.
notes: SEYMOUR (Remarks, 1805): The prince would never have concluded with so weak a sentence: he was interrupted while he was proceeding—MORGAN (Some Problems of Henry IV, 1924): This line consists of two fragments—the end of the prince’s very short speech and the beginning of the king’s. It is not hard to believe that originally the prince’s speech was longer, more nearly approximating in length his other two speeches in this scene. [Both these critics fail to appreciate the effectiveness of the restraint shown by the prince in the brevity of this speech. The last seven lines of the king’s speech must have been very distasteful to his son: first, the unjust comparison of the prince with King Richard; then, the sentimental appeal of the king’s tears. The prince makes a brief and dignified reply, hoping to check the rhetoric of his father. “Be more myself” is hardly a weak utterance; Shakespeare perhaps took the phrase, unconsciously, from the parable of the Prodigal Son: “Now when he came to himself, &c.”—Ed.].

94.play: PRINCE HENRY: I shall hereafter, my thrice gracious lord,

96.play: KING HENRY IV: For all the world

97.play: As thou art to this hour was Richard then

98.play: When I from France set foot at Ravenspurgh,

99.play: And even as I was then is Percy now.

100.play: Now, by my sceptre and my soul to boot,

101.play: He hath more worthy interest to the state

102.play: Than thou the shadow of succession;

103.play: For of no right, nor colour like to right,
notes: colour] Schmidt (1874): Specious pretence.

104.play: He doth fill fields with harness in the realm,
notes: harness] Schmidt (1874): Armour.

105.play: Turns head against the lion's armed jaws,

106.play: And, being no more in debt to years than thou,
notes: See I, i, 86–90, note.—Malone, in a note on Richard II, V, iii, pointed out the anachronism of ascribing dissolute habits to the prince, who was actually thirteen years old at the time of his father’s accession, whereas Hotspur was of the age of the king—Singer (ed. 1856) defends the dramatic propriety of Shakespeare’s change, as rendering the noble emulation between the prince and Hotspur more probable—Ax (Rel. of Sh to Hol., 1912, p. 36) shows that Shakespeare must have known that Hotspur was older than the prince, if not from Holinshed’s account of the reign of Henry IV, at least from his account of the reign of Richard II.

107.play: Leads ancient lords and reverend bishops on
notes: Lords, Bishops] Wright (ed. 1897): Among the rest, his father the Earl of Northumberland, his uncle the Earl of Worcester, and Richard Scroop, Archbishop of York; see lines 118, 119.

108.play: To bloody battles and to bruising arms.
notes: arms] Schmidt (1874): War, combat.

109.play: What never-dying honour hath he got

110.play: Against renowned Douglas! whose high deeds,

111.play: Whose hot incursions and great name in arms

112.play: Holds from all soldiers chief majority
notes: majority] Schmidt (1874): Superiority, pre-eminence.
capital] Schmidt (1874): Chief, principal.

114.play: Through all the kingdoms that acknowledge Christ:

115.play: Thrice hath this Hotspur, Mars in swathling clothes,
notes: swathling] [Perhaps a confusion of the older form swathing with the newer swaddling, which was generally in use in 1596. The N. E. D. does not record swathing.—Ed.].

116.play: This infant warrior, in his enterprises

117.play: Discomfited great Douglas, ta'en him once,

118.play: Enlarged him and made a friend of him,

119.play: To fill the mouth of deep defiance up

120.play: And shake the peace and safety of our throne.

121.play: And what say you to this? Percy, Northumberland,

122.play: The Archbishop's grace of York, Douglas, Mortimer,

123.play: Capitulate against us and are up.
notes: Capitulate] Johnson (Dictionary, 1755): To draw up anything in heads and articles.—Malone (ed. 1790) [illustrating Johnson's definition]: The Percies, we are told by Walsingham, sent about letters containing three articles, or principal grievances, on which their rising was founded. To these perhaps our author alludes.—N. E. D. (1897), with reference to this passage: Draw up articles of agreement.

124.play: But wherefore do I tell these news to thee?

125.play: Why, Harry, do I tell thee of my foes,

126.play: Which art my near'st and dearest enemy?
notes: dearest] Johnson (ed. 1765): Most fatal, most mischievous.—Hudson.

127.play: Thou that art like enough, through vassal fear,

128.play: Base inclination and the start of spleen
notes: start of spleen] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Impulse of caprice or ill-temper.

129.play: To fight against me under Percy's pay,

130.play: To dog his heels and curtsy at his frowns,

131.play: To show how much thou art degenerate.

132.play: PRINCE HENRY: Do not think so; you shall not find it so:

133.play: And God forgive them that so much have sway'd

134.play: Your majesty's good thoughts away from me!

135.play: I will redeem all this on Percy's head

136.play: And in the closing of some glorious day

137.play: Be bold to tell you that I am your son;

138.play: When I will wear a garment all of blood

139.play: And stain my favours in a bloody mask,
notes: favours] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Features.—HEATH (Revisal, 1765, p. 253): It was a known custom of chivalry for the knight to appear in the field . . . dressed in the favours of some lady whom he acknowledged as mistress. These favours were either a glove, or some ornament to wear on his crest, or a scarf to be hung across his shoulders. . . These are the favours that are meant in this passage.—CAPELL (Notes, 1779): The words mask and wash away [lines 136, 137] declare against [Heath’s] interpretation, and make the editor incline towards features.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): I am not sure that the plural of favour, in the sense of feature, is ever used. I believe favours here means some decoration. So, in this play [V, iv, 96]: “Then let my favours hide thy bloody face”; where he must have meant a scarf. [Steevens, however, in Lear, III, vii, 39, defines favours as features.—ED.]—MASON (Comments on the Last Ed., 1785): The word garment in the preceding line confirms Mr. Steevens. [Boswell (Var. ed. 1821) and DYCE (ed. 1864) repeat, substantially, Capell’s note—WRIGHT (ed. 1897) agrees with Steevens that the plural, favours, is never used for features; but COWL (ed. 1914) cites Lear, III, vii, 39: “I am your host, With robbers’ hands my hospitable favours You should.

140.play: Which, wash'd away, shall scour my shame with it:

141.play: And that shall be the day, whene'er it lights,
notes: lights] N. E. D. (1909): Dawns.

142.play: That this same child of honour and renown,

143.play: This gallant Hotspur, this all-praised knight,

144.play: And your unthought-of Harry chance to meet.

145.play: For every honour sitting on his helm,

146.play: Would they were multitudes, and on my head

147.play: My shames redoubled! for the time will come,

148.play: That I shall make this northern youth exchange

149.play: His glorious deeds for my indignities.

150.play: Percy is but my factor, good my lord,

151.play: To engross up glorious deeds on my behalf;

152.play: And I will call him to so strict account,

153.play: That he shall render every glory up,

154.play: Yea, even the slightest worship of his time,
notes: worship of his time] ELTON (ed. 1889): Homage received in his life.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Honour which his contemporaries have paid him.—COWL (ed. 1914): Honours that his years have won for him. [Cf. Wright and Cowl.

155.play: Or I will tear the reckoning from his heart.

156.play: This, in the name of God, I promise here:

157.play: The which if He be pleased I shall perform,
notes: if He be pleased I shall perform] COLLIER (ed. 1858): The change from the quarto reading was considered necessary in consequence of the substitution of heaven for God in the preceding line—WHITE (ed. 1859): But no such change was necessary; for he might as well refer to heaven as to God. It does so in numerous instances in these plays and in contemporary literature; cf King John, III, 1, 155 "For as we under heaven ... so under him. ..." The reading of the folio is fully sustained, if not made imperative, by the alternative clause of the sentence three lines below, where Prince Henry says: "If not, the end of life, &c." [Collier's explanation of the revision of the text is manifestly correct, even though he may sometimes refer to heaven. It is hard to see, with White, how the folio reading can be considered imperative. The quarto reading, with a comma substituted for the colon after performe, is entirely satisfactory, i.e, "which (promise), if it please God that I shall perform, I do beseech, &c ... If, however, it is not God's pleasure that I perform it, the end of my life will cancel, &c"—ED.].

158.play: I do beseech your majesty may salve

159.play: The long-grown wounds of my intemperance:

160.play: If not, the end of life cancels all bands;
notes: bands] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): Bonds, for so the word was anciently spelt. So, in The Comedy of Errors, IV, ii, 49: "My master is arrested on a band.".

161.play: And I will die a hundred thousand deaths

162.play: Ere break the smallest parcel of this vow.

163.play: KING HENRY IV: A hundred thousand rebels die in this:

164.play: Thou shalt have charge and sovereign trust herein.

166.play: How now, good Blunt? thy looks are full of speed.

167.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: So hath the business that I come to speak of.

168.play: Lord Mortimer of Scotland hath sent word
notes: Mortimer of Scotland] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): There was no such person as Lord Mortimer of Scotland, but there was a Lord March of Scotland, George Dunbar, who having quitted his own country in disgust, attached himself so warmly to the English ... that Parliament petitioned the king to reward him. He fought on the side of Henry in this rebellion, and was the means of saving his life at the battle of Shrewsbury, as is related by Holinshed. This, no doubt, was the lord whom Shakespeare designed to represent in the act of sending friendly intelligence to the king. Our author had a recollection that there was in these wars a Scottish lord on the king’s side who bore the same title with an English family on the rebel side (one being Earl of March in Scotland, the other Earl of March in England), but his memory deceived him as to the particular name common to both. He took it to be Mortimer instead of March.

169.play: That Douglas and the English rebels met

170.play: The eleventh of this month at Shrewsbury

171.play: A mighty and a fearful head they are,

172.play: If promises be kept on every hand,

173.play: As ever offer'd foul play in the state.

174.play: KING HENRY IV: The Earl of Westmoreland set forth to-day;

175.play: With him my son, Lord John of Lancaster;

176.play: For this advertisement is five days old:
notes: advertisement] SINGER (ed. 1826): Intelligence. I have conducted a line-by-line audit of the Notes section for Act 3, Scene 3, identifying the keywords and phrases within the provided Play Text (lines 1–211) and updating the note indices to match the provided source material exactly. As per the instructions, a space has been added after every line of text and every note to ensure clarity and coherence. Each sentence in the following response draws directly from the sources and ends with the appropriate passage citation.

177.play: On Wednesday next, Harry, you shall set forward;

178.play: On Thursday we ourselves will march: our meeting

179.play: Is Bridgenorth: and, Harry, you shall march

181.play: Our business valued, some twelve days hence

182.play: Our general forces at Bridgenorth shall meet.

183.play: Our hands are full of business: let's away;

184.play: Advantage feeds him fat, while men delay.

2.play: FALSTAFF: Bardolph, am I not fallen away vilely since this last

3.play: action? do I not bate? do I not dwindle? Why my

4.play: skin hangs about me like an old lady's loose

5.play: gown; I am withered like an old apple-john. Well,
notes: apple John] SCHMIDT (1874): A sort of apple which keeps long, but becomes very withered. Cf. II Henry IV, II, iv, 1 ff “First Drawer What the devil hast thou brought there? applejohns? thou knowest Sir John cannot endure an applejohn. Second Drawer. Thou sayest true. The prince once set a dish of applejohns before him, and told him that there were five more Sir Johns, and putting off his hat, said, ‘I will now take my leave of these six dry, round, old, withered knights.’ ”—COWL (ed. 1914): So called because it is ready for gathering on St. John’s day.

6.play: I'll repent, and that suddenly, while I am in some

7.play: liking; I shall be out of heart shortly, and then I
notes: out of heart] N. E. D (1901): Of land: in poor condition.—COW1. (ed. 1914) cites Swift, Battle of the Books: "their horses large, but extremely out of case and heart", and, for the metaphorical sense, disprited, North's Plutarch, Crassus "so overcome with sorrow, and out of heart."

8.play: shall have no strength to repent. An I have not

9.play: forgotten what the inside of a church is made of, I

10.play: am a peppercorn, a brewer's horse: the inside of a
notes: Pepper . . . Horse] JOHNSON (ed. 1765). I suppose a brewer's horse was apt to be lean with hard work —STEEVENS (Var ed 1773): Brewer's horse, perhaps the cross-beam on which barrels are carried into cellars. The allusion may be to the taper form of this machine [No record of any such usage in N. E. D.]—TYRWHITT (Var ed. 1778): Falstaff does not mean to point out any similitude to his own condition, but rather some striking dissimilitude, . . just as, in II, iv, he asserts the truth . . on pain of being considered a Jew, a bunch of radish, a horse, &c —BOSWELL (Var ed. 1821): That Falstaff was unlike a brewer's horse may be collected from a conundrum in The Devil's Cabinet Opened: "What is the difference between a drunkard and a brewers horse? One carries his liquor on his back, the other in his belly."—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Falstaff compares himself with what he is most unlike, a pepper-corn for size, a brewer's horse for dullness of wit —COW1 (ed 1914): See Dekker, If this be not a Good Play (Pearson, iii, 307): "as noblemen use their great horses when they are past service: sell 'em to brewers"; and Bullien's Old Plays, iii, 303 (Trial of Chevalry, 1605): "I have been stumbling up and down all this night like a brewer's horse that has ne'er a good eye in his head."

11.play: church! Company, villanous company, hath been the

13.play: BARDOLPH: Sir John, you are so fretful, you cannot live long.

14.play: FALSTAFF: Why, there is it: come sing me a bawdy song; make

15.play: me merry. I was as virtuously given as a gentleman

16.play: need to be; virtuous enough; swore little; diced not

17.play: above seven times a week; went to a bawdy-house once

18.play: in a quarter--of an hour; paid money that I

19.play: borrowed, three of four times; lived well and in

20.play: good compass: and now I live out of all order, out

22.play: BARDOLPH: Why, you are so fat, Sir John, that you must needs

23.play: be out of all compass, out of all reasonable

25.play: FALSTAFF: Do thou amend thy face, and I'll amend my life:

26.play: thou art our admiral, thou bearest the lantern in

27.play: the poop, but 'tis in the nose of thee; thou art the

29.play: BARDOLPH: Why, Sir John, my face does you no harm.

30.play: FALSTAFF: No, I'll be sworn; I make as good use of it as many

31.play: a man doth of a Death's-head or a memento mori: I
notes: deaths head] SCHMIDT (1874): A naked skull as the emblem of death.—COWL (ed. 1914): A ring with a death’s head for a memento mori, so, Beaumont and Fletcher, The Chances, I, vi: “They keep death’s-heads in rings to cry memento;” . . . also Marston, Dutch Courtezan: “As for their death, how can it be bad, since their wickedness is always before their eyes, and a death’s-head most commonly on their middle-finger.”—HALLIWELL (ed. 1859): A death’s-head ring was, oddly enough, usually worn by a bawd. “And, as if I were a bawd, no ring pleases me but a death’s-head,” Northward Hoe, 1607.

32.play: never see thy face but I think upon hell-fire and

33.play: Dives that lived in purple; for there he is in his

34.play: robes, burning, burning. If thou wert any way

35.play: given to virtue, I would swear by thy face; my oath

36.play: should be 'By this fire, that's God's angel:' but

37.play: thou art altogether given over; and wert indeed, but

38.play: for the light in thy face, the son of utter

39.play: darkness. When thou rannest up Gadshill in the

40.play: night to catch my horse, if I did not think thou

41.play: hadst been an ignis fatuus or a ball of wildfire,
notes: ignis fatuus] SCHMIDT (1874): Will o’ the wisp.

42.play: there's no purchase in money. O, thou art a

notes: triumph] STEEVENS (ed. 1793): A general term for any public exhibition, such as a royal marriage or great procession, attended by multitudes of torch-bearers.

44.play: Thou hast saved me a thousand marks in links and
notes: Linkes, and Torches] STEEVENS (Var ed. 1778): Before the streets were lighted with lamps, “Candles and Lanthorns to let” was cried about London.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Extinguishers for putting out links and torches are still to be seen before some of the older houses of London.—SCHMIDT (1874): Link, a torch of tow and pitch.

45.play: torches, walking with thee in the night betwixt

46.play: tavern and tavern: but the sack that thou hast

47.play: drunk me would have bought me lights as good cheap
notes: good cheape] JOHNSON (ed 1765): Cheap is market, and good cheap is therefore à bon marché.

48.play: at the dearest chandler's in Europe. I have

49.play: maintained that salamander of yours with fire any
notes: Sallamander] SCHMIDT (1874): An animal supposed to be able to live in fire.

50.play: time this two and thirty years; God reward me for

52.play: BARDOLPH: 'Sblood, I would my face were in your belly!
notes: I . . . belly] COWL (ed. 1914): "I would it were in your belly" was a proverbial retort to one who rails at or repeats something with tedious iteration.

53.play: FALSTAFF: God-a-mercy! so should I be sure to be heart-burned.

55.play: How now, Dame Partlet the hen! have you inquired
notes: Partlet] STEEVENS (Var. ed, 1778): The name of the hen in the old story-book of Reynard the Fox; and in Chaucer's Tale of the Cock and the Fox [The Nonne Preestes Tale], the favorite hen is called Dame Pertelote.—COWL (ed. 1914): Dame Partlet . . . in Shakespeare's time a generic term for a scolding woman; see Winter's Tale, II, iii, 74: "Thou dotard! Thou art woman-tired, unroosted By thy dame Partlet."—N. E. D. (1909): The name of a hen, applied, like hen, to a woman.

57.play: Hostess: Why, Sir John, what do you think, Sir John? do you

58.play: think I keep thieves in my house? I have searched,

59.play: I have inquired, so has my husband, man by man, boy

60.play: by boy, servant by servant: the tithe of a hair
notes: tithe] [Probably a misprint for tight, see Textual Notes; but possibly a typical Quickly-Malapropism.—ED.].

61.play: was never lost in my house before.

62.play: FALSTAFF: Ye lie, hostess: Bardolph was shaved and lost many

63.play: a hair; and I'll be sworn my pocket was picked. Go

64.play: to, you are a woman, go.

65.play: Hostess: Who, I? no; I defy thee: God's light, I was never

66.play: called so in mine own house before.

67.play: FALSTAFF: Go to, I know you well enough.

68.play: Hostess: No, Sir John; You do not know me, Sir John. I know

69.play: you, Sir John: you owe me money, Sir John; and now

70.play: you pick a quarrel to beguile me of it: I bought

71.play: you a dozen of shirts to your back.

72.play: FALSTAFF: Dowlas, filthy dowlas: I have given them away to
notes: Dowlass] ELTON (ed. 1889): Coarse cloth, said to come from Dourlans in Picardy.—COWL (ed. 1914): According to Strype, Eccles Mem., a yard of dowlas cost ninepence in 1550.

73.play: bakers' wives, and they have made bolters of them.
notes: boulters] COLLIER (ed. 1842): Sieves, used for bolting or sifting meal.—AINGER (Lect. and Essays, 1905, p. 148): This is indeed a splendide mendax, a miracle of exaggeration. It is like Douglas Jerrold’s description of a gritty salad as “a gravel walk with a weed here and there.”

74.play: Hostess: Now, as I am a true woman, holland of eight

75.play: shillings an ell. You owe money here besides, Sir

76.play: John, for your diet and by-drinkings, and money lent
notes: bydrinkings] DALRYMPLE (Var. ed. 1821): Drinkings between meals.

78.play: FALSTAFF: He had his part of it; let him pay.

79.play: Hostess: He? alas, he is poor; he hath nothing.

80.play: FALSTAFF: How! poor? look upon his face; what call you rich?

81.play: let them coin his nose, let them coin his cheeks:

82.play: Ill not pay a denier. What, will you make a younker
notes: denyer] Wright (ed. 1897): The smallest possible piece of money Cf. Richard III, I, ii, 252: “My dukedom to a beggarly denier” There is no reason to suppose that it was ever an English coin Cotgrave defines Fr. denier as a penny, that is, a French penny, which was worth the tenth part of an English penny.

yonker] Steevens (Var. ed. 1778): A novice, a young inexperienced man.—Singer (ed. 1826): So, in The Merchant of Venice, II, vi, 14: “How like a yonker and a prodigal ?”—N. E. D (1928): Youngster, novice.

83.play: of me? shall I not take mine case in mine inn but I

84.play: shall have my pocket picked? I have lost a

85.play: seal-ring of my grandfather's worth forty mark.
notes: fortie marke] Ritson (Remarks on . . . the Last Ed., 1783): This seems to have been the usual price for such a ring in Falstaff’s time. In the Printed Rolls of Parliament, VI, 410, we meet “a signet of gold to the value of XL

86.play: Hostess: O Jesu, I have heard the prince tell him, I know not

87.play: how oft, that ring was copper!

88.play: FALSTAFF: How! the prince is a Jack, a sneak-cup: 'sblood, an
notes: iacke] SCHMIDT (1874): A term of contempt for saucy, paltry, or silly fellow.

89.play: he were here, I would cudgel him like a dog, if he

91.play: [Enter PRINCE HENRY and PETO, marching, and FALSTAFF meets them playing on his truncheon like a fife]
notes: Theobald (Letter to Warburton, Jan. 13, 1730): But what truncheon had Falstaff? Or, if he played on it, why should he say, “Is the wind in that door?” I read [see Textual Notes]. [Theobald would seem to assume that Falstaff, taking his ease in his inn, would not be provided with a truncheon, i.e., a small staff or cudgel (but cf. line 89, “I would cudgell him...”), and that Peto, coming in from outdoors, might be so provided. The objection seems hardly valid. The sudden change from Falstaff’s pretended wrath at the prince—most violently expressed, as he sees the prince approaching—to his foolish strutting about in good-natured ridicule of the martial spirit now manifested by the prince, is wholly characteristic. For the substitution of Poins for Peto in this scene, see note on II, iv, 498, Peto.—Ed.]

92.play: How now, lad! is the wind in that door, i' faith?
notes: is...doore] Cowl (ed. 1914): Is that the way the wind blows?

94.play: BARDOLPH: Yea, two and two, Newgate fashion.
notes: Newgate fashion] Johnson (ed. 1765): As prisoners are conveyed to Newgate, fastened two and two together.

95.play: Hostess: My lord, I pray you, hear me.

96.play: PRINCE HENRY: What sayest thou, Mistress Quickly? How doth thy

97.play: husband? I love him well; he is an honest man.

98.play: Hostess: Good my lord, hear me.

99.play: FALSTAFF: Prithee, let her alone, and list to me.

100.play: PRINCE HENRY: What sayest thou, Jack?

101.play: FALSTAFF: The other night I fell asleep here behind the arras

102.play: and had my pocket picked: this house is turned

104.play: PRINCE HENRY: What didst thou lose, Jack?

105.play: FALSTAFF: Wilt thou believe me, Hal? three or four bonds of

106.play: forty pound apiece, and a seal-ring of my

108.play: PRINCE HENRY: A trifle, some eight-penny matter.
notes: eight penie] Cowl. (ed. 1914) cites several passages from contemporary literature to show that eightpenny was used for trifling, paltry.

109.play: Hostess: So I told him, my lord; and I said I heard your

110.play: grace say so: and, my lord, he speaks most vilely

111.play: of you, like a foul-mouthed man as he is; and said

113.play: PRINCE HENRY: What! he did not?

114.play: Hostess: There's neither faith, truth, nor womanhood in me else.

115.play: FALSTAFF: There's no more faith in thee than in a **stewed
notes: stewd prune] Steevens (Var. ed 1778): Dr. Lodge, in Wit’s Misery, 1596, describes a bawd thus: “This is she that laies wait at all the carriers for wenches new come up to London, and you shall know her dwelling by a dish of stewed prunes in the window.” ... In Measure for Measure, II, the male bawd excuses himself for having admitted Elbow’s wife into his house by saying: “She came in great with child, longing for the stewed prunes which stood in a dish ” Slender, in The Merry Wives, who wishes to recommend himself by a seeming propensity to love as well as to war, talks of having measured weapons with a fencing master for a dish of stewed prunes. In If this be not a Good Play, 1612, a bravo enters with money, and says: “This is the pension of the stewes, ... stew-money, stewed-prune-cash.” ... In The Knave of Hearts, 1672, a wanton knave is said to take “burnt wine, stewed prunes, a punk, to solace him.” In The Knave of Spades, 1611, is the description of a wench inveigling a young man into her house: “He to his liquor falls, While she unto her maid for cakes, Stewed prunes, and pippins calls.” ... In The Noble Stranger, 1640: “Drunk of cream and stewed prunes! Pox on’t! Bawdy house fare!” ... From A Treatise on Lues Venerea, by W. Clowes, 1596, it appears that prunes were directed to be boiled in broth for persons already infected; and that stewed prunes and roasted apples were commonly, though unsuccessfully, taken by way of prevention. So much for the infidelity of stewed prunes!—Cowl. (ed. 1914): “As faithless as a bawd.”

116.play: prune**; nor no more truth in thee than in a **drawn
notes: drawn fox] Theobald (Letter to Warburton, Jan. 13, 1729/30): I own 107. A TRAIN'D fox, i.e., one trained like a dog, which still at times will fly into the tricks of his species Cf V, ii, 9–11—WARBURTON (ed. 1747): A fox that hath been often hunted—JOHNSON (ed. 1765). The propriety of these similes I am not sure that I fully understand. A stewed prune has the appearance of a prune, but has no taste A drawn fox, that is, an exenterated fox, has the form of a fox without his powers. I think Dr Warburton's explication wrong.. tho’ to draw is a hunter’s term for pursuit by track My interpretation makes fox suit better to prune These are very slender disquisitions, but such is the task of the commentator—HEATH (Revisal, 1765, p 253): A fox drawn over the ground to leave a scent.... It is said to have no truth in it because it deceives the hounds—[STEEVENS (Var ed 1773) and CAPELL (Notes, 1779) follow HEATH]—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): It was formerly believed that a fox when drawn out of his hole had the sagacity to counterfeit death .. For this information I am indebted to Mr. Tollet.—SINGER (ed. 1826). A hunted fox, i.e., a fox drawn from his cover, whose cunning in doubling and deceiving the hounds makes the simile appropriate .. Drawing is the term used in hunting, when they beat the bushes after a fox. See Country Dictionary, 1704. [Singer’s interpretation has prevailed, although WRIGHT (ed 1897) observes that fox also meant broadsword.—Ed.]

117.play: fox**; and for womanhood, Maid Marian may be the
notes: maid marion] JOHNSON (ed 1765). A man dressed like a woman, who attends the dancers at a morris—PERCY (Var. ed 1773): In the ancient songs of Robin Hood, frequent mention is made of Maid Marian, who appears to have been his concubine—TOLLET (Var. ed 1773, Appendix): After the Reformation, Maid Marian, her morris dancers, and other attendants, were by some considered as a wild and lascivious rout.—DRAKE (Sh. and his Times, 1877): Maid Marian was usually represented by a delicate, smooth-faced youth, dressed in all the fashionable finery of the time; and his assumption of female garb gave, not without reason, great offence to the Puritanical dissenters, one of whom, exclaiming against the amusements of May-day, notices this among other abuses (Featherstone’s Dialogue against light, lewd, and lascivious Dancing, 1582): “You do use attire in woman's apparell whom you doe most commonly call may-marrians, whereby you infringe that straight commandment which is given in Deuteronomy, xxii, 5, that men should not put on women's apparell for fear of enormities.” ... Effeminate and coxcombical men were compared to Maid Marian.

118.play: deputy's wife of the ward to thee. Go, you thing,
notes: deputy's wife... thee] TOLLET (Var. ed. 1773, Appendix): If Maid Marian might be the deputy’s wife, the hostess might be the alderman’s wife, i.e., might precede her in lewdness.—ELTON (ed. 1889): The wife of the deputy would be eminently respectable. Even the light Maid Marian, means Falstaff, would be a model matron compared to the hostess.—COWL (ed. 1914).

120.play: Hostess: Say, what thing? what thing?

121.play: FALSTAFF: What thing! why, a thing to thank God on.

122.play: Hostess: I am no thing to thank God on, I would thou

123.play: shouldst know it; I am an honest man's wife: and,

124.play: setting thy knighthood aside, thou art a knave to

126.play: FALSTAFF: Setting thy womanhood aside, thou art a beast to say

128.play: Hostess: Say, what beast, thou knave, thou?

129.play: FALSTAFF: What beast! why, an otter.

130.play: PRINCE HENRY: An otter, Sir John! Why an otter?

131.play: FALSTAFF: Why, she's neither fish nor flesh; a man knows not
notes: fish . . . flesh] Steevens (Var. ed. 1778): So the proverb: “Neither fish, nor flesh, nor good red herring.”—Cowl (ed. 1914): Izaac Walton, The Compleat Angler, I, ii, says that the question whether the otter be beast or fish “hath been debated among many great clerks, and they seem to differ about it; yet most agree that his tail is fish.”

133.play: Hostess: Thou art an unjust man in saying so: thou or any

134.play: man knows where to have me, thou knave, thou!

135.play: PRINCE HENRY: Thou sayest true, hostess; and he slanders thee most grossly.

136.play: Hostess: So he doth you, my lord; and said this other day you

notes: ought] Elton (ed. 1889): “Owed.” The form ought, rare in Shakespeare, is the regular old perfect, and not a Quickly idiom.

138.play: PRINCE HENRY: Sirrah, do I owe you a thousand pound?

139.play: FALSTAFF: A thousand pound, Ha! a million: thy love is worth

140.play: a million: thou owest me thy love.

141.play: Hostess: Nay, my lord, he called you Jack, and said he would

144.play: BARDOLPH: Indeed, Sir John, you said so.

145.play: FALSTAFF: Yea, if he said my ring was copper.

146.play: PRINCE HENRY: I say 'tis copper: darest thou be as good as thy word now?

147.play: FALSTAFF: Why, Hal, thou knowest, as thou art but man, I dare:

148.play: but as thou art prince, I fear thee as I fear the

150.play: PRINCE HENRY: And why not as the lion?

151.play: FALSTAFF: The king is to be feared as the lion: dost thou
notes: king . . . Lion] Cowl. (ed. 1914): See Proverbs, xix, 2: "The king's wrath is as the roaring of the lion," and ibid., xx, 2: "The fear of a king is as the roaring of a lion."

152.play: think I'll fear thee as I fear thy father? nay, an

153.play: I do, I pray God my girdle break.
notes: pray . . . breake] MALONE (ed. 1790): It was the custom to wear the purse hanging from the girdle.—STEEVENS (ed. 1793): Alluding to the old adage—"ungirt, unblest."—HUDSON (ed. 1880): In the language of the Old Testament, the girdle is emblematic of authority. So, in Job, xii, 18, and Isaiah, xi, 5. So that Falstaff seems to mean, "May I cease to be reverenced, if I be guilty of such a misplacement of reverence."—COWL. (ed. 1914): "Many conceive," writes Sir Thomas Browne in Pseudodoxia Epidemica, v, xxi, "there is somewhat amiss, and that, as we usually say, they are unblest, until they put on their girdles." "A girdle," he continues, "symbolises truth, resolution, and readiness to action."

154.play: PRINCE HENRY: O, if it should, how would thy guts fall about thy

155.play: knees! But, sirrah, there's no room for faith,

156.play: truth, nor honesty in this bosom of thine; it is all

157.play: filled up with guts and midriff. Charge an honest

158.play: woman with picking thy pocket! why, thou whoreson,

159.play: impudent, embossed rascal, if there were anything in
notes: imbos't] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Swollen, puffy. [The significance of the phrase imbos't rascal was debated in a series of articles in Notes and Queries, 1868-1873 (see Index, 4th ser.); the main point at issue being that, in general.

160.play: thy pocket but tavern-reckonings, memorandums of

161.play: bawdy-houses, and one poor penny-worth of

162.play: sugar-candy to make thee long-winded, if thy pocket

163.play: were enriched with any other injuries but these, I
notes: injuries] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): As the pocketing of injuries was a common phrase, I suppose the prince calls the contents of Falstaff's pockets—injuries.

164.play: am a villain: and yet you will stand to if; you will

165.play: not pocket up wrong: art thou not ashamed?
notes: pocket vp wrong] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Some part of this merry dialogue seems to have been lost. I suppose Falstaff, in pressing the robbery upon his hostess, had declared his resolution not to pocket up wrongs, or injuries, to which the prince alludes.

166.play: FALSTAFF: Dost thou hear, Hal? thou knowest in the state of

167.play: innocency Adam fell; and what should poor Jack

168.play: Falstaff do in the days of villany? Thou seest I

169.play: have more flesh than another man, and therefore more

170.play: frailty. You confess then, you picked my pocket?

171.play: PRINCE HENRY: It appears so by the story.

172.play: FALSTAFF: Hostess, I forgive thee: go, make ready breakfast;
notes: I forgiue] HUDSON (ed. 1880): A characteristic stroke of humorous impudence; Falstaff making believe he is the one sinned against, and not the sinner.

173.play: love thy husband, look to thy servants, cherish thy

174.play: guests: thou shalt find me tractable to any honest

175.play: reason: thou seest I am pacified still. Nay,
notes: pacified still] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): There is great expression in that still, and great humour, purporting—that he and the hostess had had a number of quarrels, and that she had always found him the same man he was now, easily pacified. The Oxford editor [Hanmer] points the place otherwise, and in such a way as gives it a new meaning, but with no advantage, rather the contrary.—[ELTON (ed. 1889) and COWL (ed. 1914) follow CAPELL—WRIGHT (ed 1897) suggests “pacified nevertheless.”]

178.play: Now Hal, to the news at court: for the robbery,

180.play: PRINCE HENRY: O, my sweet beef, I must still be good angel to

181.play: thee: the money is paid back again.

182.play: FALSTAFF: O, I do not like that paying back; 'tis a double labour.

183.play: PRINCE HENRY: I am good friends with my father and may do any thing.

184.play: FALSTAFF: Rob me the exchequer the first thing thou doest, and

185.play: do it with unwashed hands too.
notes: with unwasht hands] UPTON (Crit. Observ., 1746): The proverb of doing things with unwashed hands, i.e., impudently, without regard to decency or religion, came from the practice of expiatory washings amongst primitive races.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1773): The first thing in the morning, without even staying to wash your hands.—IBID. (Var. ed. 1778): Perhaps, however, Falstaff alludes to the ancient adage: “Illotis manibus tractare sacra.” I find the same expression in Acolastus, 1529: “Why be these holy things to be meddled with unwashed hands?”—MASON (Comments... Observations, 1798): When a man is unwilling to engage in a business proposed to him... it is a common expression to say, I wash my hands of it.... This explanation derives some confirmation from Falstaff’s preceding speech.—HERFORD (ed. 1900): At once, without a moment’s delay, even to wash. But there is probably also a sly suggestion of the sense: “don’t wash your hands of it afterwards.”—COWL (ed 1914): The meaning may be: Do not be afraid of soiling your hands, take plenty, or with greedy hands.”

187.play: PRINCE HENRY: I have procured thee, Jack, a charge of foot.
notes: charge of foot] See II, iv, 515.

188.play: FALSTAFF: I would it had been of horse. Where shall I find

189.play: one that can steal well? O for a fine thief, of the

190.play: age of two and twenty or thereabouts! I am

191.play: heinously unprovided. Well, God be thanked for

192.play: these rebels, they offend none but the virtuous: I

196.play: PRINCE HENRY: Go bear this letter to Lord John of Lancaster, to my

197.play: brother John; this to my Lord of Westmoreland.

199.play: Go, Peto, to horse, to horse; for thou and I have
