notes: Peto] JOHNSON (ed 1765): I cannot but think that Peto is again put for Poins [cf. note on II, iv, 498]. I suppose the copy had only a P—. We have Peto, afterwards, not riding with the prince, but lieutenant to Falstaff.—MALONE (ed. 1790): In further support of Dr. Johnson's emendation it may be observed that Poins suits the metre of the line, which would be spoiled by Peto. [Po., which might be misread Peto, is the form used for Poins's speech-headings in the fragment of Q₀, now in the Folger Shakespeare Library.—ED.]

200.play: thirty miles to ride yet ere dinner time.

202.play: Jack, meet me to-morrow in the temple hall at two

204.play: There shalt thou know thy charge; and there receive

205.play: Money and order for their furniture.

206.play: The land is burning; Percy stands on high;

207.play: And either we or they must lower lie.

209.play: FALSTAFF: Rare words! brave world! Hostess, my breakfast, come!
notes: brave world] [Falstaff admires the spirit of the young prince, and the brave world that has such spirited young creatures in it, but—away with sentiment—“Hostess, my breakfast, come!”—ED.]

210.play: O, I could wish this tavern were my drum!
notes: drum] SEYMOUR (Remarks, 1805): The inn where the recruiting officer is quartered is called the Drum-head; perhaps, emphatically, the Drum. [This explanation is simple, and seems plausible; but unfortunately the N. E. D gives no evidence that Drum was ever used for Drumhead. Falstaff may refer to the drum, in its literal sense, as he pounds on the table for his breakfast; or he may refer to the Drum, or Drummer, of whose relation to his captain, T. Digges (Stratioticos, ed. 1579; quoted by COWL, ed. 1914) writes: [The captain] ought not to doe anye thing with his Souldiours, but to have his Ensign and his Drumm with him (p 94). Falstaff, comments Cowl, could wish to have the tavern ever with him.—ED.]

2.play: HOTSPUR: Well said, my noble Scot: if speaking truth
notes: Wel said] CLARKE (Sh Key, 1879, p. 4): Shakespeare often begins a scene with an abrupt commencement; with a conversation already begun and in course of progress as the speakers enter. It serves to give an effect of greater naturalness and ease to the dialogue.

3.play: In this fine age were not thought flattery,
notes: fine] SCHMIDT (1874): Refined.—N. E. D. (1901): Cunning, crafty, artful; or, used ironically, "egregious."

4.play: Such attribution should the Douglas have,
notes: attribution] SCHMIDT (1874): Praise.—N. E. D. (1888), with reference to this passage: Anything ascribed in one's estimation, e.g name, credit, character.

5.play: As not a soldier of this season's stamp

6.play: Should go so general current through the world.

7.play: By God, I cannot flatter; I do defy

8.play: The tongues of soothers; but a braver place

9.play: In my heart's love hath no man than yourself:

10.play: Nay, task me to my word; approve me, lord.
notes: taske . . . word] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Challenge me to be as good as my word.—N. E. D. (1919), with reference to this passage: Task to: To impose a task on.

11.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Thou art the king of honour:

12.play: No man so potent breathes upon the ground
notes: STAUNTON (ed. 1858): This hemistich is always allied to the preceding line; but it may refer to something supposed to have been said by Douglas before the beginning of the scene,—some threat of confronting the king, which has called forth Hotspur’s “Well said,” in line 1.

16.play: What letters hast thou there?--I can but thank you.

17.play: Messenger: These letters come from your father.

18.play: HOTSPUR: Letters from him! why comes he not himself?

19.play: Messenger: He cannot come, my lord; he is grievous sick.

20.play: HOTSPUR: 'Zounds! how has he the leisure to be sick

21.play: In such a rustling time? Who leads his power?
notes: iustling] N. E. D. (1901): Justle, usual form in 17th century, and main form in 18th, has now yielded largely to jostle.

22.play: Under whose government come they along?

23.play: Messenger: His letters bear his mind, not I, my lord.
notes: not . . . mind] WARBURTON (ed. 1747) interprets his reading [see Textual Notes] Hotspur asks. “Who leads his power?” The messenger answers “His letters bear his mind; not I.” The other replies “His mind!” as much as to say, I didn’t enquire about his mind, I want to know where his powers are—CAPELL (Notes, 1779) interprets his reading [see Textual Notes]. The eye of the first compositor [Q1] was caught by the [first] mind in this line, and he gave it to us again at the end of the line: the compositor next but one after him [Q3] went a step further, giving us a repetition of his also, as well as of mind; and both their mistakes are handed down faithfully to us in all other copies, old and new. In some of these [Hanmer’s and Warburton’s], these mistakes are made the groundwork of what are called emendations.—MALONE (ed. 1790): The correction made by Mr. Capell is certainly right. In two of the other speeches spoken by the messenger, he uses the same language [my lord, lines 17, 23], nor is it likely that he would address Hotspur without this mark of respect [Capell has inserted it in line 15, as well]. In his first speech the messenger is interrupted by the impetuosity of the person he addresses.

24.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: I prithee, tell me, doth he keep his bed?

25.play: Messenger: He did, my lord, four days ere I set forth;

26.play: And at the time of my departure thence

27.play: He was much fear'd by his physicians.
notes: feared] ABBOTT (Sh Gram., 1870, p. 133): The preposition [in this case, for] is omitted after some verbs which can easily be regarded as transitive “That do conspire (for) my death,” Richard III, III, iv, 62, “And his physicians fear (for) him mightily,” Richard III, I, i, 137.

28.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: I would the state of time had first been whole
notes: state of time] N. E. D. (1919): Juncture, or posture, of affairs.

29.play: Ere he by sickness had been visited:

30.play: His health was never better worth than now.

31.play: HOTSPUR: Sick now! droop now! this sickness doth infect
notes: sickness] MALONE (ed. 1790): A line has probably been lost [after 32]—STEEVENS (ed. 1793): I suspect no omission. Hotspur is abruptly enumerating the principal topicks of the letter before him.—CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 763): Who does not feel that this is Hotspur’s characteristic way of breaking off a phrase, and leaving its completion to be understood by the listener?—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Hotspur is only quoting the material portions of the letter, just as Kent (Lear, II, ii, 160) in reading a letter from Cordelia gives fragments of it.—WALKER (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 20): Words such as sickness, brightness, &c., are often pronounced as if a vowel were inserted before the suffix.

32.play: The very life-blood of our enterprise;

33.play: 'Tis catching hither, even to our camp.

34.play: He writes me here, that inward sickness--

35.play: And that his friends by deputation could not
notes: by deputation] COWL (ed. 1914): By means of a deputy.

36.play: So soon be drawn, nor did he think it meet

37.play: To lay so dangerous and dear a trust
notes: deare] Cf. I, i, 33.

38.play: On any soul removed but on his own.
notes: any . . . remouot’d] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): On any less near to himself; on any whose interest is remote.

39.play: Yet doth he give us bold advertisement,
notes: advertisement] SCHMIDT (1874): Instruction.

40.play: That with our small conjunction we should on,
notes: conjunction] ELTON (ed. 1889): Joint force.

41.play: To see how fortune is disposed to us;

42.play: For, as he writes, there is no quailing now.

43.play: Because the king is certainly possess'd
notes: posset] SINGER (ed. 1826): Informed.

44.play: Of all our purposes. What say you to it?

45.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: Your father's sickness is a maim to us.
notes: maim] COWL. (ed. 1914): Maim signified in law, "where ... any member is hurt or taken away, whereby the party so hurt is made ... the less able to defend himself." (Minsheu.)

46.play: HOTSPUR: A perilous gash, a very limb lopp'd off:

47.play: And yet, in faith, it is not; his present want
notes: his present want] SCHMIDT (1874): Our present want of him.

48.play: Seems more than we shall find it: were it good

49.play: To set the exact wealth of all our states
notes: set] SCHMIDT (1874): Stake.

50.play: All at one cast? to set so rich a main
notes: maine] JOHNSON (ed 1765): A hand at dice.—SCHMIDT (1874): A stake at gaming.

51.play: On the nice hazard of one doubtful hour?
notes: nice hazard] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Risk delicately balanced.

52.play: It were not good; for therein should we read
notes: read] See Textual Notes—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): I believe the old reading is the true one. So, in II Henry VI, V, ii, 78: "We then should see the bottom of all our fortunes."—MALONE (ed. 1790): I now think there is no need of alteration. To "read a list" is a harsh phrase, but not more so than many others. At the same time that the bottom of their fortunes should be displayed, its circumference [list] or boundary would be exposed to view. Sight becomes necessary to reading; to read is here used, in Shakespeare's licentious language, for to see. The passage quoted from Henry VI strongly confirms this interpretation. To it may be added this in Romeo and Juliet: "Is there no pity sitting in the clouds That sees into the bottom of my grief?"

53.play: The very bottom and the soul of hope,
notes: soul [of hope] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): That on which hope depends for its existence. It is to be feared that an equivoque was intended [soul and sole], as in list in the following line.

54.play: The very list, the very utmost bound
notes: list] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Selvage; figuratively, the utmost line of circumference, the utmost extent.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The word seems to have been chosen because its other meaning, “catalogue,” fits with “read”

56.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: 'Faith, and so we should;
notes: and] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 72): And is often found in an emphatic sense after statements implied by ejaculations.

57.play: Where now remains a sweet reversion:
notes: Where] MALONE (ed. 1790): Used for whereas.

58.play: We may boldly spend upon the hope of what

60.play: A comfort of retirement lives in this.
notes: comfort of retirement] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): A support to which we may have recourse.—COWL. (ed. 1914): A retreat to which we may retire for succour and support.—N. E. D. (1897): Comfort: Succour, support. [Comfort is used here in this, older, sense of support; cf. “aid and comfort to the enemy,” and “the comfort of the Holy Spirit.”—ED.]

61.play: HOTSPUR: A rendezvous, a home to fly unto.

62.play: If that the devil and mischance look big
notes: COWL (ed. 1914): A proverbial expression. See Heywood, Wise Woman, IV (Pearson, v, 337): “He goe, although the Deuill and mischance looke bigge”

63.play: Upon the maidenhead of our affairs.

64.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: But yet I would your father had been here.

65.play: The quality and hair of our attempt
notes: haire] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): The complexion or character. The metaphor appears harsh to us, but was perhaps familiar in our author’s time. We still say something is “against the hair,” i.e, against the natural tendency—BOSWELL (Var. ed. 1821): Perhaps, against the air, outward appearance.—COLLIER (ed. 1842): Worcester perhaps means there should be no splitting of their power.—SINGER (Text of Sh. Vindicated, 1853): Does Mr Collier think of splitting a hair?—COLLIER (ed. 1858): I offered it [not as explanation] but merely as conjecture.—DYCE (Structures on Collier, 1859) Whether as explanation or conjecture it is such a one as Mr. Collier ought never to have offered, after the notes of Johnson, Steevens, and Malone. [The latter editors had supported Johnson with citation of passages, e.g., The Family of Love: “They say I am of the right hair”; Sir Thomas More. “A fellow of your hair is very fit To be a secretary’s follower.”—Ed.]—N. E. D. (1901) defines the word, in this passage, as meaning sort, kind, nature; from the phrase “of one hair,” i.e. of one colour or external quality.—COWL (ed. 1914): Grain, texture; hence, character. . . . The metaphor is from the colour or texture of hair.

66.play: Brooks no division: it will be thought

67.play: By some, that know not why he is away,

68.play: That wisdom, loyalty and mere dislike

69.play: Of our proceedings kept the earl from hence:

70.play: And think how such an apprehension
notes: apprehension] SCHMIDT (1874): Conception, imagination.

71.play: May turn the tide of fearful faction
notes: fearefull faction] SCHMIDT (1874): Timid conspiracy.

72.play: And breed a kind of question in our cause;

73.play: For well you know we of the offering side
notes: offering] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Of this reading the sense is obscure, and therefore the change has been made [see Textual Notes]; but since neither offering nor offending are words likely to be mistaken, I cannot but suspect

74.play: Must keep aloof from strict arbitrement,
notes: arbitrament] Schmidt (1874): Judicial inquiry.—Wright (ed. 1897): Trial of our cause by argument.

75.play: And stop all sight-holes, every loop from whence
notes: loope] Wright (ed. 1897): Cf. Lear, III, iv, 31: “Your looped and windowed raggedness.”

76.play: The eye of reason may pry in upon us:

77.play: This absence of your father's draws a curtain,
notes: drawes] Malone (Suppl. to Var. ed. 1778, 1780): Draws it open. So, in a stage-direction in II Henry VI, Q 1600: “Then the curtaines being drawne, duke Humphrey is discovered in his bed.”

78.play: That shows the ignorant a kind of fear
notes: a kind of feare] Cowt. (ed. 1914): As it were, some apprehension on our part; ... cf. line 68, ante, a kind of question.
MORGAN (Some Problems of Henry IV, 1924) finds indication of curtailment here, in the fact that the two fragments which compose the line do not make a complete verse.

81.play: I rather of his absence make this use:

82.play: It lends a lustre and more great opinion,
notes: opinion] SCHMIDT (1874): Reputation. Cf V, iv, 48.

83.play: A larger dare to our great enterprise,
notes: dare] SCHMIDT (1874): Daring, boldness.

84.play: Than if the earl were here; for men must think,

85.play: If we without his help can make a head
notes: make a head] Cf. I, iii, 284, and III, i, 62.

86.play: To push against a kingdom, with his help

87.play: We shall o'erturn it topsy-turvy down.

88.play: Yet all goes well, yet all our joints are whole.
notes: Yet] SCHMIDT (1874): As yet.

89.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: As heart can think: there is not such a word

90.play: Spoke of in Scotland as this term of fear.
notes: tearme] STAUNTON (ed. 1858): We prefer dream, because Douglas appears to be scornfully referring to an expression in the previous speech of Worcester: "a kind of fear Before not dreamed of." [Staunton has not persuaded subsequent editors. The Textual Notes show how tearme became dreame.—Ed.]

92.play: HOTSPUR: My cousin Vernon, welcome, by my soul.

93.play: VERNON: Pray God my news be worth a welcome, lord.

94.play: The Earl of Westmoreland, seven thousand strong,

95.play: Is marching hitherwards; with him Prince John.

97.play: VERNON: And further, I have learn'd,

98.play: The king himself in person is set forth,

notes: intended] N. E. D. (1901), with reference to this passage: Intend, start on a journey, set out.

101.play: HOTSPUR: He shall be welcome too. Where is his son,

102.play: The nimble-footed madcap Prince of Wales,
notes: nimble footed] Steevens (Var. ed. 1773): Shakespeare rarely bestows his epithets at random. Stowe says of the prince, “he was passing swift in running, insomuch as he with two other of his lords, without hounds, bow or other engine, would take a wild duck, or doe, in a large park.” [Cf. II, iv, note on 128, 129.—Ed.]

103.play: And his comrades, that daff'd the world aside,

105.play: VERNON: All furnish'd, all in arms;

106.play: All plumed like estridges that with the wind

107.play: Baited like eagles having lately bathed;
notes: "Bated" is perhaps used for "bating," the passive for the active participle, a license which our author often takes.… To "bate," as appears from Minsheu’s Dictionary, 1617, was originally applied to birds of prey when they swoop upon their quarry.—DOUCE (Illust of Sh, 1807, i, 435) Although the ostrich was occasionally denominated "estridge" by our older writers, it is by no means certain that this bird is meant in the present instance. It may seem a very obvious comparison between the feathers of a crested helmet and those of the ostrich; and had the expression "plumed like estridges" stood alone, no doubt could have arisen. It is what follows that occasions the difficulty. If the ostrich had been alluded to, the conjectural substitution of "wing" for "with" would have been absolutely requisite; but the line that follows cannot be made to apply to the ostrich. It is related altogether to falconry, a sport to which Shakespeare is perpetually referring… It has been quite overlooked that "estridge" signifies "goshawk." In this sense the word is used in Antony and Cleopatra, III, xiii. "And in that mood (of fury) the dove will peck the estridge."… It would be absurd to talk of a dove pecking an ostrich, the allusion is to the practice of flying falcons at pigeons. "So doves do peck the falcons piercing talons" (III Henry VI). Thus Golding, trans. Ovid’s Metamorphoses (fol. 9). "With flittering feather siélie doves so from the goshawk flie." The manor of Radcelyve in Nottinghamshire is held by the service of "mewing a goshawk," "mutandi unum estricum."… Falconers are often called ostregers or ostringers in old books of falconry, and elsewhere. Estridge for ostrich is a corrupt spelling that crept into the language at the commencement of Queen Elizabeth’s reign, and it appears that after that period the two words were very often confounded.…—IBID. (p. 437): There is certainly a line lost, as Mr. Malone has justly and ingeniously conjectured; but the place should rather seem to be after the word "bath’d" than before. The sense… "plumed like falcons, which, with their feathers being ruffled with the wind, like eagles that have lately bathed, make a violent fluttering noise." The words in italics are mine, being here conjecturally offered as something like the sense of the omitted line.—BOSWELL (Var. ed. 1821): It is not uncommon in elliptical language to leave the verb to be understood.—SINGER (ed. 1826): The meaning is obviously this:—"The prince and his companions were all furnished, all in arms, all plumed: like estridges (ostriches) that bated (beat or fluttered) the wind with their wings; like eagles having lately bathed." Johnson’s reading is exceptionable, if not unwarrantable, because "to wing the wind" and "to bate" are the same; and the difficulties of an elliptical construction are not avoided by it.

108.play: Glittering in golden coats, like images;

109.play: As full of spirit as the month of May,

110.play: And gorgeous as the sun at midsummer;

111.play: Wanton as youthful goats, wild as young bulls.

112.play: I saw young Harry, with his beaver on,

113.play: His cuisses on his thighs, gallantly arm'd

114.play: Rise from the ground like feather'd Mercury,

115.play: And vaulted with such ease into his seat,

116.play: As if an angel dropp'd down from the clouds,

117.play: To turn and wind a fiery Pegasus

118.play: And witch the world with noble horsemanship.

119.play: HOTSPUR: No more, no more: worse than the sun in March,

120.play: This praise doth nourish agues. Let them come:

121.play: They come like sacrifices in their trim,

122.play: And to the fire-eyed maid of smoky war

123.play: All hot and bleeding will we offer them:

124.play: The mailed Mars shall on his altar sit

125.play: Up to the ears in blood. I am on fire

126.play: To hear this rich reprisal is so nigh

127.play: And yet not ours. Come, let me taste my horse,

128.play: Who is to bear me like a thunderbolt

129.play: Against the bosom of the Prince of Wales:

130.play: Harry to Harry shall, hot horse to horse,

131.play: Meet and ne'er part till one drop down a corse.

134.play: I learn'd in Worcester, as I rode along,

135.play: He cannot draw his power this fourteen days.

136.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: That's the worst tidings that I hear of yet.

137.play: WORCESTER: Ay, by my faith, that bears a frosty sound.

138.play: HOTSPUR: What may the king's whole battle reach unto?

141.play: My father and Glendower being both away,

142.play: The powers of us may serve so great a day

143.play: Come, let us take a muster speedily:

144.play: Doomsday is near; die all, die merrily.

145.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Talk not of dying: I am out of fear

146.play: Of death or death's hand for this one-half year.

2.play: FALSTAFF: Bardolph, get thee before to Coventry; fill me a
notes: Counentry] Ax (Rel of Sh to Hol, 1912, p. 43): The town of Coventry may have been chosen by Shakespeare for this scene, firstly, because it is on the road from London to Shrewsbury, secondly, because it is near his native town; ... and, thirdly, because it occurs in the Chronicle at about this date ... as the meeting place for “the laymen’s parliament” (Hol, III, 30).—ARTHUR GRAY (Chap. in Early Lyfe of Sh., 1926, p 47): There is evidence that when Shakespeare wrote Henry IV he was unfamiliar with the London-Stratford road. . . . If a straight line be drawn from London to Shrewsbury, it passes exactly through Stratford, and the direct line is continued thence through Bridgenorth. This is the route which Shakespeare, who does not take his authority from Holinshed, makes the king to follow, while the prince makes a detour south (cf. III, ii, 175). ... Falstaff makes a wide detour through northern Warwickshire, and is at some pains to tell us what places lay about his march from London (lines 43, 44). ... If only Shakespeare had willed that Falstaff’s ragged prodigals should take the Stratford road, what a chance for local scenes and characters.—CROFTS (Falstaff’s March, T.L.S., Jan. 8, 1931). The route from Buckingham to Bridgenorth in Ogilby’s Britannia (1675) follows pretty accurately Mr. Gray’s “straight line.” But there seems to have been something unsatisfactory about this road in the 16th and early 17th century. It is not given in any of the Elizabethan roadbooks catalogued by Sir Herbert Forman, nor in MS. Itinerary printed by Miss G. Thompson (Eng. Hist. Rev., 1918); and it seems clear from The Carriers’ Cosmography.

3.play: bottle of sack: our soldiers shall march through;

notes: Sutton cophill] KNIGHT (Wm Sh, a Biog, 1842, p. 164): There is no doubt that Sutton Coldfield, as it is now spelt, was meant by cop-hill; but the old printers improperly introduced the hyphen. [They did so only when the word cophill breaks at the end of a line (as it does in Q1), there is none, for example, in Q2, where the word is in the middle of the line. Q6, followed by Ff, introduces the hyphen between Sutton and cophill.—ED].

5.play: BARDOLPH: Will you give me money, captain?

notes: lay out] CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 346): “Spend freely!” an idiomatic expression.—COWL (ed. 1914): see Huloet, Dict., 1572: “Layer out of money, or Steward.”

7.play: BARDOLPH: This bottle makes an angel.
notes: makes an angel] WARNER (Letter to Garrick, 1768): An angel is a gold coin, with the figure of an angel stamped upon it, valued at ten shillings.—COWL (ed. 1914): “Makes an angel that I shall have laid out.”

8.play: FALSTAFF: An if it do, take it for thy labour; and if it make

9.play: twenty, take them all; I'll answer the coinage. Bid

10.play: my lieutenant Peto meet me at town's end.

13.play: FALSTAFF: If I be not ashamed of my soldiers, I am a soused
notes: 2 sout gurnet] JOHNSON (ed 1765): Pickled anchovy. Much of Falstaff’s humour consists in comparing himself to somewhat little.—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1773): An appellation of contempt frequently employed in our early comedies. So, in Dekker’s Honest Whore: “Punk! You souc’d gurnet!”—MALONE (ed. 1790): It should seem from Taylor’s A Bawd, 1635, that it was used in the same metaphorical sense in which we now use “gudgeon” [i.e., a credulous person]: “She hath baits for all kynde of frye: a great lord is her Greenland whale, a country gentleman her cod’s-head, a rich citizen’s son her sow’s’d gurnet.”—WRIGHT (ed. 1897) The instances of its use as a term of contempt are later than this passage, and possibly derive from it.—N. E. D. (1901): Gurnet: gurnard. . . . Used as a term of opprobrium.

14.play: gurnet. I have misused the king's press damnably.
notes: misused . . presse] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1803): Thus, in The Voyage to Cadiz (Hakluyt, I, 607): “About the 28 of said month, a certain Lieutenant was degraded for the taking of money by the way of corruption of certaine prest souldiers in the countrey, and for placing of others in their roomes, more unfit for service and of less sufficiency and abilitie.”—AX (Rel. of Sh to Hol., 1912, p. 43): Holinshed (II, 778) says that in 1387 “the lord admiral understanding that the Duke of Gloucester would see the muster of his men, used all diligence and spared no costs to have the most choicest and pickest fellows that might be gotten, not following the evil example of some in times past, which received tag and rag to fill up their numbers, whom they hired for small wages and reserved the residue to their purses.”—LINDSBURY (Study of Patriotism in Eliz. Drama, 1931, p. 148): Falstaff’s skill in using his office to line his pockets was no uncommon gift among Elizabethan recruiting officers. It provoked a severe rebuke in The Cobbler’s Prophecy (lines 1432-41), and in Histriomastix, V, 87. . . . The hero of The Fair Maid of the Exchange purchased the release of one recruit and won his gratitude forever. And Simon Eyre had the sympathy of the audience when he tried to save his young apprentice (Dekker’s Shoemakers’ Holiday, I, i, 132). [But Falstaff’s method is characteristically elaborate. He takes bribes for exempting the well-to-do; but he also fills his ranks with ragamuffins, who doubtless take less than the legal pay.—ED.].

15.play: I have got, in exchange of a hundred and fifty

16.play: soldiers, three hundred and odd pounds. I press me

17.play: none but good house-holders, yeoman's sons; inquire

18.play: me out contracted bachelors, such as had been asked
notes: contracted] SCHMIDT (1874): Betrothed.

19.play: twice on the banns; such a commodity of warm slaves,
notes: askt ... banes] HUDSON (ed 1880): The law required that parties intending marriage should have the banns asked three times before the ceremony could take place.
warme] COWL (ed. 1914): Bailey's Dictionary (Canting Words): "Warm, well-lined, or flush in the pocket."

20.play: as had as lieve hear the devil as a drum; such as

21.play: fear the report of a caliver worse than a struck
notes: Caliver] MEYRICK (Crit. Inquiry unto Antient Armour, 1824, iii, 42): As the musket was found unwieldy, a lighter piece was introduced (in the reign of Elizabeth), called a caliver. ... Edmund Yorke, an officer of Queen Elizabeth, says: "We had our peculiar calibre of harquebuse to our regiment. ... Before the battle of Moungunter the princes caused several harquebuses to be made all of one calibre, which was called Harquebuse de Calibre de M. le Prince; so, I think, some man not understanding French brought hither the name of the height of the bullet for the Piece."—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The accent was evidently on the second syllable: calecur (Cotgrave), caleuer (Shelton, Don Quixote, II, 432, ed. 1620).

22.play: fowl or a hurt wild-duck. I pressed me none but such

23.play: toasts-and-butter, with hearts in their bellies no
notes: tostes and butter] MALONE (ed 1790): Moryson’s Itinerary, 1617: “Londoners, and all within sound of Bow-bell, are called cocknies and eaters of butter’d tostes”—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Cockneys, self-indulgent fellows—COWL (ed. 1914): Milksops, soft, pampered cits.

24.play: bigger than pins' heads, and they have bought out
notes: bought . . services] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Paid money to be released from serving in the army.

25.play: their services; and now my whole charge consists of

notes: Ancients] SCHMIDT (1874): The next in command under a lieutenant.—COWL (ed 1914): Ancient-bearers, i.e., standard bearers, ensigns.

gentlemen of companies] COWL (ed. 1914): Private soldiers who received a little better pay than ordinary privates, and were exempt from sentinel duty. . . . Sir John Turner (Pallas Armata, 1683, p. 218) says that they were not of officers' rank, though by some allowed to be so.

27.play: companies, slaves as ragged as Lazarus in the
notes: Lazarus . . . cloth] NARES (Gloss., 1822): Painted cloth was cloth or canvas on which paintings in oil were depictured.—HALLIWELL (ed. 1859): Gayton’s Notes on Don Quixote, 1654 “The Acts of Dives and of Lazarus . . . Are seen in puppet plaies, or painted cloth.”—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): We have had Dives already, in III, iii, 30-32.—COWL (ed. 1914): Cloth painted in oil served as a cheap substitute for arras hangings. The subjects depicted were usually scriptural, classical, or allegorical. D. Lupton, London and the Country Carbonadoed, 1632, writes in a chapter on Ale-houses. “In these houses you shall see the History of Judith, Susanna, Daniel in the Lyons Den, or Dives and Lazarus, painted vpon the wall [See also Appendix: Characters: Falstaff—Simpson (1899).].

28.play: painted cloth, where the glutton's dogs licked his

29.play: sores; and such as indeed were never soldiers, but

30.play: discarded unjust serving-men, younger sons to
notes: vniuſt] STAUNTON (ed. 1858): Dishonest.

31.play: younger brothers, revolted tapsters and ostlers
notes: reuolted] COWL (ed. 1914): Who have run away from their indentures; faithless.

32.play: trade-fallen, the cankers of a calm world and a
notes: cankers] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): So, in Pierce Pennilesse, 1595: "All the canker-worms that breed on the rust of peace."

33.play: long peace, ten times more dishonourable ragged than

34.play: an old faced ancient: and such have I, to fill up
notes: olde fazd ancient] THEOBALD (ed. 1733): Shakespeare uses the word ancient so promiscuously, to signify an Ensign, or Standard-Bearer, and also the Colours, or Standard, borne, that I cannot be at a certainty for his allusion here. If the text be genuine, I think the meaning must be: "as dishonourably ragged as one that hath been an ensign all his days." ... Mr. Warburton, who understands it in the second construction [i.e., colours], has suspected the text, and given the following ingenious emendation: "How is an old-fac'd Ensign dishonourably ragged? On the contrary, nothing is esteemed more honourable than a ragged pair of colours. ... 'Ten times more ragged than an old FEAST Ancient,' i.e., the colours used by the city companies in their feasts and.

Olde fazd] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1773): An old standard mended with a different colour. "Olde fazd" should not be written as one word, as "old" and "faced" are two distinct epithets. To face a gown is to trim it. . . . In our author's time, the facings of a gown were always of a colour different from the stuff itself. So, in this play [V, i, 74]—COWL (ed 1914): [Steevens's] explanation is open to the objection that old ensigns are never patched. . . . Again, to face never means to patch, but to trim or edge a garment with another colour.—CHANDLER (Tudor ed. 1911): Feaz'd, or frayed.—N. E. D. (1901): Face, cf. feaze, unravel.

35.play: the rooms of them that have bought out their

36.play: services, that you would think that I had a hundred

37.play: and fifty tattered prodigals lately come from
notes: tottered] COWL (ed. 1914): Tattered. Lyly, Endimion, V, i: "Whose garment was so tottered that it was easier to number everie thread."—N. E. D. (1926): Variant of tattered.

38.play: swine-keeping, from eating draff and husks. A mad
notes: draff] SCHMIDT (1874): Refuse.

39.play: fellow met me on the way and told me I had unloaded

40.play: all the gibbets and pressed the dead bodies. No eye

41.play: hath seen such scarecrows. I'll not march through

42.play: Coventry with them, that's flat: nay, and the

43.play: villains march wide betwixt the legs, as if they had

44.play: gyves on; for indeed I had the most of them out of
notes: gyues] POPE (ed. 1723): Gyves, shackles.

45.play: prison. There's but a shirt and a half in all my
notes: not] COLLIER (ed. 1858): Consistently, with our usual course in such matters, we adhere to the early printed text, because Falstaff may have meant to say that there’s not so much as a shirt and a half in all his company—COWL (ed. 1914). Rowe is followed by many modern editors in substituting but for not, in the teeth of all the Qq and Ff. This unhappy change sacrifices one of Falstaff’s happiest sallies of humour to the spirit of literalism. Falstaff employs precisely the same device in II, iv, 115 [“There lives not three good men unhang’d… and one of them is fat”] and V, iii, 36 [“there’s not three of my 150 left alive, and they are for the town’s end”]. The description of the ragged recruits is in Falstaff’s richest vein of whimsical invention and daring improvisation, and the climax is reached when the shirt and a half, which the scarecrows did not possess among them, is described circumstantially.

46.play: company; and the half shirt is two napkins tacked

47.play: together and thrown over the shoulders like an

48.play: herald's coat without sleeves; and the shirt, to say
notes: Heralds coate] COWL (ed. 1914): A tabard, or sleeveless coat.

49.play: the truth, stolen from my host at Saint Alban's, or

50.play: the red-nose innkeeper of Daventry. But that's all

51.play: one; they'll find linen enough on every hedge.
notes: Hedge] COWL (ed. 1914): The light-fingered Autolycus in Winter’s Tale, IV, iii, 5-7, confesses that a “white sheet bleaching on the hedge… doth set his pugging tooth on edge.”

53.play: PRINCE HENRY: How now, blown Jack! how now, quilt!
notes: blowne] CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 533): Both “out of breath” and “swollen.”

54.play: FALSTAFF: What, Hal! how now, mad wag! what a devil dost thou

55.play: in Warwickshire? My good Lord of Westmoreland, I

56.play: cry you mercy: I thought your honour had already been

58.play: WESTMORELAND: Faith, Sir John,'tis more than time that I were

59.play: there, and you too; but my powers are there already.

60.play: The king, I can tell you, looks for us all: we must

notes: away] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Travel.—N. E. D. (1888): Elliptical use of away, with the verb suppressed, simulating an imperative or an infinitive.

62.play: FALSTAFF: Tut, never fear me: I am as vigilant as a cat to
notes: feare mee] SCHMIDT (1874): Fear for me.

64.play: PRINCE HENRY: I think, to steal cream indeed, for thy theft hath

65.play: already made thee butter. But tell me, Jack, whose

66.play: fellows are these that come after?

68.play: PRINCE HENRY: I did never see such pitiful rascals.

69.play: FALSTAFF: Tut, tut; good enough to toss; food for powder, food
notes: tosse] JOHNSON (ed 1765): Toss upon a pike.

70.play: for powder; they'll fill a pit as well as better:

71.play: tush, man, mortal men, mortal men.

72.play: WESTMORELAND: Ay, but, Sir John, methinks they are exceeding poor

74.play: FALSTAFF: 'Faith, for their poverty, I know not where they had

75.play: that; and for their bareness, I am sure they never

77.play: PRINCE HENRY: No I'll be sworn; unless you call three fingers on
notes: fingers] COWL (ed 1914): The finger is a measure of ¾ inch. Eden, Arte Nav., i, xviii, 19: “Foure graines of barley make a finger; foure fingers a hande; foure handes a foote.”

78.play: the ribs bare. But, sirrah, make haste: Percy is

80.play: FALSTAFF: What, is the king encamped?

81.play: WESTMORELAND: He is, Sir John: I fear we shall stay too long.

83.play: To the latter end of a fray and the beginning of a feast
notes: Feast] CAMDEN (Remains, 1605; ed. 1870, p. 320) cites, as a proverb of the former times: “Better coming to the latter end of a feast than the beginning of a fray.”

84.play: Fits a dull fighter and a keen guest.

2.play: HOTSPUR: We'll fight with him to-night.

3.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: It may not be.

4.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: You give him then the advantage.

6.play: HOTSPUR: Why say you so? looks he not for supply?
notes: supply] SCHMIDT (1874): Additional forces.

8.play: HOTSPUR: His is certain, ours is doubtful.

9.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: Good cousin, be advised; stir not tonight.

11.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: You do not counsel well:

12.play: You speak it out of fear and cold heart.

13.play: VERNON: Do me no slander, Douglas: by my life,

14.play: And I dare well maintain it with my life,

15.play: If well-respected honour bid me on,
notes: well respected] SCHMIDT (1874): Ruled by reasonable considerations.—ELTON (ed. 1889): Like our "well-considered."—COWL (ed. 1914): Perhaps, simply: "which I hold in dear respect." [The context seems to support Schmidt and Elton. Vernon is no reckless enthusiast like Hotspur; but he holds no counsel with fear if honour really calls him —Ed.]

16.play: I hold as little counsel with weak fear

17.play: As you, my lord, or any Scot that this day lives:
notes: this day] MASON (Comm. on the Last Ed., 1785): These words weaken the sense and destroy the metre.—STAUNTON (ed. 1858): It is not improbable that the line originally stood: "As you, or any Scot that this day lives," and was altered by the poet to "As you, my lord, or any Scot that lives"; but the compositor, while adding the words "my lord," neglected to omit "this day."

18.play: Let it be seen to-morrow in the battle

20.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Yea, or to-night.

23.play: VERNON: Come, come it nay not be. I wonder much,

24.play: Being men of such great leading as you are,
notes: leading] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Conduct, experience in martial business.

25.play: That you foresee not what impediments

26.play: Drag back our expedition: certain horse
notes: expedition] SCHMIDT (1874): Warlike enterprise. [But may not expedition, here, have its suggestion of haste, i.e., "drag back our expedition" in the sense of "keep us from hasty action"? Vernon is counselling delay.—Ed.]

27.play: Of my cousin Vernon's are not yet come up:

28.play: Your uncle Worcester's horse came but today;

29.play: And now their pride and mettle is asleep,

30.play: Their courage with hard labour tame and dull,

31.play: That not a horse is half the half of himself.

32.play: HOTSPUR: So are the horses of the enemy

33.play: In general, journey-bated and brought low:

34.play: The better part of ours are full of rest.

35.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: The number of the king exceedeth ours:

36.play: For God's sake. cousin, stay till all come in.

39.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: I come with gracious offers from the king,
notes: WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The offer of the king was really conveyed by Thomas Prestbury, Abbot of Shrewsbury, and by one of the clerks of the Privy Seal.

40.play: if you vouchsafe me hearing and respect.

41.play: HOTSPUR: Welcome, Sir Walter Blunt; and would to God

43.play: Some of us love you well; and even those some

44.play: Envy your great deservings and good name,

45.play: Because you are not of our quality,
notes: qualitie] MALONE (ed. 1790): Frequently used in the sense of fellowship or occupation. So, in The Tempest, I, ii, 192: “Task Ariel and all his quality,” i.e., all those employed with him in similar occupations.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Of our profession, on the same side as ourselves. Used especially of the theatrical profession; see Hamlet, II, ii, 333. [Here, of course, rather in the sense of “fellowship” than “profession.”—Ed.]—N. E. D. (1914): Party, side [with reference to this passage].

46.play: But stand against us like an enemy.

47.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: And God defend but still I should stand so,
notes: defend] SCHMIDT (1874): Forbid.

48.play: So long as out of limit and true rule
notes: limit] KELLNER (Restoring Sh, 1925, p 79) m is often misprinted for u (v). Level for limit would make a perfect metaphor here, taken from masonry: "you do not stand upright, your position toward the king is oblique." [But limit, in the sense of bounds, makes good sense —Ed.]

50.play: But to my charge. The king hath sent to know

51.play: The nature of your griefs, and whereupon
notes: grieves] MALONE (ed. 1790): Grievances.

52.play: You conjure from the breast of civil peace

53.play: Such bold hostility, teaching his duteous land
notes: hostility] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p 349): t, if unaccented, is very frequently dropped in pronunciation before ly and ty: "Such bold/hostil(l)/ty teach/ing his [’s] dut/eous land "

54.play: Audacious cruelty. If that the king

55.play: Have any way your good deserts forgot,

56.play: Which he confesseth to be manifold,
notes: MALONE (ed. 1790): The Percies were in the highest favour with King Henry IV for some time after his accession. Thomas Earl of Worcester was appointed governour to the Prince of Wales, and honoured with the custody of Isabel, widow of King Richard II, when she was sent back to France.

57.play: He bids you name your griefs; and with all speed

58.play: You shall have your desires with interest

59.play: And pardon absolute for yourself and these

notes: suggestion] WARNER (Letter to Garrick, 1768, p. 50): The word often occurs in its ordinary sense of hint, insinuation. But there are some passages where... it means more, and is used for pernicious counsel and advice.—SCHMIDT (1874) gives twelve examples of the use in the latter sense, and only one of its use in the former.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897) Secret prompting or enticement. The word is not used in a good sense, and was rather likely to provoke Hotspur; as it evidently did.

61.play: HOTSPUR: The king is kind; and well we know the king

62.play: Knows at what time to promise, when to pay.

63.play: My father and my uncle and myself

64.play: Did give him that same royalty he wears;

65.play: And when he was not six and twenty strong,
notes: sixe and twentie] COWL (ed. 1914): Holinshed says three score.

66.play: Sick in the world's regard, wretched and low,

67.play: A poor unminded outlaw sneaking home,
notes: unminnded] SCHMIDT (1874): Unnoticed, not cared for.

68.play: My father gave him welcome to the shore;

69.play: And when he heard him swear and vow to God

70.play: He came but to be Duke of Lancaster,

71.play: To sue his livery and beg his peace,
notes: sue his liuery] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): A law phrase belonging to feudal tenures, meaning to sue out the delivery or possession of his lands from the Court of Wards, which, on the death of any of the tenants of the crown, seized their lands, until the heir “sued out his livery.”

72.play: With tears of innocency and terms of zeal,

73.play: My father, in kind heart and pity moved,

74.play: Swore him assistance and perform'd it too.

75.play: Now when the lords and barons of the realm

76.play: Perceived Northumberland did lean to him,

77.play: The more and less came in with cap and knee;
notes: more and lesse] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): The greater and the less.—CAPELL (Notes, 1779): These words refer to those in line 66, the Lords and Barons, and mean all orders of them.—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 27): More is frequently used as the comparative of great.

78.play: Met him in boroughs, cities, villages,

79.play: Attended him on bridges, stood in lanes,

80.play: Laid gifts before him, proffer'd him their oaths,

81.play: Gave him their heirs, as pages follow'd him
notes: ELTON (ed. 1889) The punctuation of the old editions has hardly any sanctity compared with the words, and that which we adopt yields better sense [see Textual Notes].—WRIGHT (ed. 1897) objects to the punctuation introduced by Malone on the ground that the phrase “golden multitudes,” in the following line, “seems to imply that the words ‘as pages followed’ are closely connected . . . and this is still further shown by ‘Even at the heels,’ with which compare Timon of Athens, IV, iii, 221: ‘Will these moss’d trees, That have outliv’d the eagle, page thy heels?’ ”—COWL (ed. 1914): Against the pointing of Qq and F1–3, it may be said that pages in shows and pageants went before their masters; cf. The Three Lords and Three Ladies of London (Hazlitt’s Dodsley, vi, 472): “Canst thou declare to me the cause why all their pages follow them, when ours in show do ever go before? In war they follow, and the Spaniard is warring in mind.” [But “follow” may signify merely “follow his cause,” “become followers, or servants.”—Ed.]—IBID. (Notes on the Text, 1927, p. 9): Marmion, Holland’s Leaguer, I, ii: “Your rich men shall strive to put their sons to be his pages.” This “echo” seems to establish the correctness of the punctuation proposed by Malone. . . . The “more or less” gave Bolingbroke their heirs as pages. . . . The “heirs” would be received as hostages for their fathers; thus we read in The Fugger News-Letters (Brussels, Aug. 16, 1600): “As a surety of good faith the gentleman . . . is said to have sent one of his sons to Spain, to serve there as a page.”

82.play: Even at the heels in golden multitudes.

83.play: He presently, as greatness knows itself,
notes: as . . . selfe] ELTON (ed. 1889): As his greatness becomes conscious of itself.

84.play: Steps me a little higher than his vow

85.play: Made to my father, while his blood was poor,

86.play: Upon the naked shore at Ravenspurgh;

87.play: And now, forsooth, takes on him to reform

88.play: Some certain edicts and some strait decrees

89.play: That lie too heavy on the commonwealth,

90.play: Cries out upon abuses, seems to weep

91.play: Over his country's wrongs; and by this face,

92.play: This seeming brow of justice, did he win

93.play: The hearts of all that he did angle for;

94.play: Proceeded further; cut me off the heads

95.play: Of all the favourites that the absent king

96.play: In deputation left behind him here,
notes: in deputation] ELTON (ed. 1889): As his deputies; cf IV, i, 33.

97.play: When he was personal in the Irish war.
notes: personally] HUDSON (ed. 1880): Commanding in person.

98.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: Tut, I came not to hear this.

100.play: In short time after, he deposed the king;

101.play: Soon after that, deprived him of his life;

102.play: And in the neck of that, task'd the whole state:
notes: taskd] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Taskt is here used for tax’d; it was once common to employ these words indiscriminately. ... Holinshed, p 422: “There was a new and strange subsidy or task granted.”—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Technically “task” denoted the “fifteenth”, which by Magna Charta was given to the king in return for the liberties granted by him, and was levied originally upon every man’s possessions, but afterwards collectively upon every city, borough, or town. One of the complaints of the Percies was that Henry had sworn not to suffer “any dismes to be levied of the Clergie, nor fiftenes of the people.”—N. E. D. (1919): Task and tax were at first nearly synonymous; but in development tax follows the sense of the corresponding verb, as an assessed money payment.

103.play: To make that worse, suffer'd his kinsman March,

104.play: Who is, if every owner were well placed,
notes: ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 416) explains the metrical irregularity of this line as arising from Hotspur’s emotion and the intended solemnity of his utterance.

105.play: Indeed his king, to be engaged in Wales,

106.play: There without ransom to lie forfeited;

107.play: Disgraced me in my happy victories,

108.play: Sought to entrap me by intelligence;

109.play: Rated mine uncle from the council-board;

110.play: In rage dismiss'd my father from the court;

111.play: Broke oath on oath, committed wrong on wrong,

112.play: And in conclusion drove us to seek out

113.play: This head of safety; and withal to pry
notes: head of safetie] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Army from which I hope for protection.—COWL (ed. 1914): There is possible a play intended on “head,” a spring, source.

114.play: Into his title, the which we find

notes: indirect] ELTON (ed. 1889): “Obliquely won:” cf. II Henry IV, IV, v, 184: “God knows, my son, By what by-paths and indirect crook’d ways I met this crown”—COWL (ed 1914) Cf ., also, Henry V, II, iv, 94, and Edward III, I, i, where Robert of Artois argues that Edward is the rightful heir: “You are the lineal watchman of our peace, And John of Artois indirectly climbs.” —N E. D. (1901): Of succession not descending in a direct line.

116.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: Shall I return this answer to the king?

117.play: HOTSPUR: Not so, Sir Walter: we'll withdraw awhile.

118.play: Go to the king; and let there be impawn'd
notes: impawned] WRIGHT (ed 1897): Like “engaged” in line 105.

119.play: Some surety for a safe return again,

120.play: And in the morning early shall my uncle

121.play: Bring him our purposes: and so farewell.

122.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: I would you would accept of grace and love.

123.play: HOTSPUR: And may be so we shall.

124.play: SIR WALTER BLUNT: Pray God you do.

notes: GENTLEMAN (Dram. Censor, 1770). Our author has judiciously concluded this act [sic] with probable ideas of peace, which bring on more forcibly the operations of the next. [Nothing could emphasize more strongly the desperate state of the rebels than these unexpected words of peace from the defiant Hotspur. For a moment, even he seems to realize the impossibility of continuing the conflict. Observe that Shakespeare really “concludes this act” by the short scene which follows, in which the impending defeat of the rebels is again strongly stressed.—ED.] Based on the sources provided, I have completed the line-by-line audit for Act 4, Scene 4. I have identified the keywords in the notes, located them in the play text (lines 1–44), and updated the line numbers in the notes to reflect their exact position. As requested, no words have been changed, and I have included spacing after every line of text and every note. Key terms have been bolded for clarity.

1.play: [Enter the ARCHBISHOP OF YORK and SIR MICHAEL]

2.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Hie, good Sir Michael; bear this sealed brief

3.play: With winged haste to the lord marshal;
notes: Lord Marshall] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): Thomas Mowbray, his confederate in the other rebellion out of which is formed the whole serious part of the play that comes next [II Henry IV]: both plays appear to have been planned at the same time, and with great judgment, notwithstanding the inequality of their actions’ duration. ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 387): We may explain the apparent trisyllabic marshal by reference to the French mareschal. WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Cf. Lear, IV, iii, 8: “The Marshal of France, Monsieur La Far.”

4.play: This to my cousin Scroop, and all the rest
notes: coosen Scroope] AX (Rel. of Sh. to Hol., 1912, p. 45): This scene has been invented to prepare for the part played by the Archbishop in Part II We could not find in the Chronicle either Sir Michael or Cousin Scroop.

5.play: To whom they are directed. If you knew

6.play: How much they do to import, you would make haste.

9.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Like enough you do.

10.play: To-morrow, good Sir Michael, is a day

11.play: Wherein the fortune of ten thousand men

12.play: Must bide the touch; for, sir, at Shrewsbury,
notes: sir] WARNER (Letter to Garrick, 1768, p. 65): At Oxford, when an undergraduate has taken his degree as Bachelor of Arts, he is styled Dominus; in Cambridge, Sur. COWL (ed. 1914): Nothing is known of this Sir Michael, but he was no doubt a priest . . . “Sir” was a usual title of courtesy given to priests.

13.play: As I am truly given to understand,

14.play: The king with mighty and quick-raised power

15.play: Meets with Lord Harry: and, I fear, Sir Michael,

16.play: What with the sickness of Northumberland,

17.play: Whose power was in the first proportion,
notes: Theobald. (Letter to Warburton, Jan 15, 1729/30): I imagine a word is dropped out at the end of the line [see Textual Notes], i.e., when we first cast up the proportion of our numbers, we included his power in the account. The following line seems to warrant this conjecture “a rated sinew too,” i.e., accounted as part of their strength, rather than as Mr. Pope comments [see Pope’s note on line 17]. Johnson (ed. 1765). Whose quota was larger than that of any other.

18.play: And what with Owen Glendower's absence thence,

19.play: Who with them was a rated sinew too
notes: a rated sinew] Pope (ed. 1723): Accounted a strong aid. Johnson (ed. 1765): A strength on which we reckoned; a help of which we made account.

20.play: And comes not in, o'er-ruled by prophecies,

21.play: I fear the power of Percy is too weak

22.play: To wage an instant trial with the king.

23.play: SIR MICHAEL: Why, my good lord, you need not fear;

24.play: There is Douglas and Lord Mortimer.

25.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: No, Mortimer is not there.

26.play: SIR MICHAEL: But there is Mordake, Vernon, Lord Harry Percy,

27.play: And there is my Lord of Worcester and a head

29.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: And so there is: but yet the king hath drawn

30.play: The special head of all the land together:

31.play: The Prince of Wales, Lord John of Lancaster,

32.play: The noble Westmoreland and warlike Blunt;

33.play: And moe corrivals and dear men
notes: moe] N. E. D. (1901): More in number as distinguished from moe in quantity.

34.play: Of estimation and command in arms.
notes: estimation] SCHMIDT (1874): Reputation. Drawing on the provided sources, I have completed the line-by-line audit of the Notes for Act 5, Scene 1. I have identified each keyword or phrase in the notes, located its position in the Play Text (lines 1–148), and updated the line numbers in the notes to reflect their exact location in the text. As instructed, no words have been changed, and I have added spacing after every line of text and every note.

35.play: SIR MICHAEL: Doubt not, my lord, they shall be well opposed.

36.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: I hope no less, yet needful 'tis to fear;

37.play: And, to prevent the worst, Sir Michael, speed:

38.play: For if Lord Percy thrive not, ere the king

39.play: Dismiss his power, he means to visit us,

40.play: For he hath heard of our confederacy,

41.play: And 'tis but wisdom to make strong against him:

42.play: Therefore make haste. I must go write again

43.play: To other friends; and so farewell, Sir Michael.

1.play: [Enter KING HENRY, PRINCE HENRY, Lord John of LANCASTER, EARL OF WESTMORELAND, SIR WALTER BLUNT, and FALSTAFF]
notes: For the evidence that the Falstaff element in this scene was added by Shakespeare when he revised the play, see Appendix: Sources of the Plot: A. E. Morgan, section 21. See also M. Morgann on Falstaff, Appendix: Characters: Falstaff.

2.play: KING HENRY IV: How bloodily the sun begins to peer

3.play: Above yon busky hill! the day looks pale
notes: busky] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Busky is woody (Fr Bosquet) Milton writes the word, perhaps more properly, bosky—DYCE (ed. 1857): Steevens appears to have forgotten that in The Tempest, IV, i, 81, the folio has “my boskie acres.”—BLAKEWAY (Var. ed. 1821): I do not know whether Shakespeare ever surveyed the ground of the battlefield, but he has described the sun’s rising over Haughmond hill as accurately as if he had. It still merits the name of “busky hill”.
his] ELTON (ed. 1889): The sun’s.

6.play: Doth play the trumpet to his purposes,
notes: trumpet] SCHMIDT (1874): Trumpeter.

7.play: And by his hollow whistling in the leaves

8.play: Foretells a tempest and a blustering day.

9.play: KING HENRY IV: Then with the losers let it sympathize,

10.play: For nothing can seem foul to those that win.

13.play: How now, my Lord of Worcester! 'tis not well

14.play: That you and I should meet upon such terms

15.play: As now we meet. You have deceived our trust,

16.play: And made us doff our easy robes of peace,

17.play: To crush our old limbs in ungentle steel:
notes: old limbs] RITSON (Cursory Crit., 1792) Shakespeare must have known that the king was at this time not more than four years older than he was at the time of the deposition of Richard. And, indeed, in the next play, he makes him expressly tell us that it was then “but eight years since.”. But it is altogether fruitless to attempt the reconciliation of our author’s chronology.—SINGER (ed. 1856): The poet had little regard to dates if he could bring the substance of historic truth within the conditions of dramatic effect. It was necessary to anticipate the king’s age that Prince Henry should be represented as arrived at manhood.—CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 573): Henry was at.

18.play: This is not well, my lord, this is not well.

19.play: What say you to it? will you again unknit

20.play: This curlish knot of all-abhorred war?

21.play: And move in that obedient orb again
notes: obedient orb] Cowl. (ed. 1914). Move again obediently in your proper orb ... The allusion is to the orbs or spheres in which, in the Ptolemaic system of astronomy, the stars are fixed, and with which they move.

22.play: Where you did give a fair and natural light,

23.play: And be no more an exhaled meteor,
notes: exhale meteor] Anders (Sh. Books, 1904, p. 248): A meteor was supposed to be a kind of vapour drawn up by the sun and acted upon by the hot influences in the upper air. Hence, exhalation equals meteor [cf. II, iv, 296, 297]. Cf. Romeo and Juliet, III, v, 13: “It is some meteor that the sun exhales.”.

24.play: A prodigy of fear and a portent
notes: portent] [See Abbott on I, i, 97, aspects, and II, iii, 61, portents. If fear, in this line, is to be regarded as a monosyllable, portents must be accented on the final syllable.—Ed.].

25.play: Of broached mischief to the unborn times?

26.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: Hear me, my liege:

27.play: For mine own part, I could be well content

28.play: To entertain the lag-end of my life

29.play: With quiet hours; for I do protest,
notes: houres] Wright (ed. 1897): A dissyllable, as frequently. See II Henry IV, IV, v, 109: “To stab at half an ho-ur of my life.”.

30.play: I have not sought the day of this dislike.
notes: dislike] Schmidt (1874): Dissension, discord. Troilus, II, iii, 218: “You feed too much on this dislike.”.

31.play: KING HENRY IV: You have not sought it! how comes it, then?
notes: Lettsom (New Readings, 1853): The word “say,” introduced into this line by “the Old Corrector” [cf. Textual Notes, Coll. MS.] takes off the sharp edge of the king’s interrogation and converts him from a flesh and blood.

32.play: FALSTAFF: Rebellion lay in his way, and he found it.

notes: chewet] POPE (ed. 1723): Chevet, fr a Bolster—THEOBALD (ed 1733): This I take to be an arbitrary refinement of Mr. Pope's nor can I easily agree that chevet is Shakespeare's word here. Why should Prince Henry call Falstaff Bolster for interposing in the discourse betwixt the king and Worcester? With submission, he does not take him up here for his unreasonable size, but for his ill-timed unreasonable chattering. A Chewet is a chattering bird, a pie ... Besides, if the poet had intended that the prince should fleer at Falstaff on account of his corpulence, I doubt not but he would have called him Bolster in English, and not have wrapped up the abuse in a French word. In another passage the prince honestly calls him Quilt, ’tis pity Mr. Pope did not turn this into Loder or Mataes, if his French would extend so far. As to Prince Henry, his stock in this language was so small that when he comes to be king he hammers out one small sentence of it to the Princess Catherine and tells her, It is as easy for him to conquer the kingdom as to speak so much French. [See also LOUNSBURY on Pope, Appendix, p 342]—STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): In an old book of cookery, printed in 1596, I find a receipt to make chewuets, which from their ingredients seem to have been fat, greasy puddings ... So, in Bacon's Nat. Hist: "As for chuets, which are likewise minced meat, instead of butter and fat, it were good to moisten them with cream.".—MALONE (ed. 1790) See Florio's Italian Dictionary, 1598. "Frilingotti. A kinde of daintie chewet or minced pie"—COLLIER (ed. 1842): Chewet may be only a form of printing suet, a word very applicable to Falstaff.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897). A kind of pie made of minced meat. Supposed by Theobald to be the same as the French chouette, but this is an owl, though Cotgrave defines it as a chough or jackdaw. With either meaning it would have a certain appropriateness as applied to Falstaff.—COWL. (ed. 1914). A chough; a noisy, chattering bird. ... See Pierre Bellon, Hist. de la Nature des Oiseaux, 1555, vi, vi: "De la Chouette, ou Chouca rouge: L'on garde ce Chouca rouge apprivoise, & lui apprend-on a parler. ... Il et moult criard." ...—N. E. D. gives an earlier example of chewet from J. Heywood's Proverbs, 1562: "Chattering to chiding is not worth a chuet.". 34-35.] COWL (ed. 1914): See Richard II, II, iii, 26-28.

34.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: It pleased your majesty to turn your looks

35.play: Of favour from myself and all our house;

36.play: And yet I must remember you, my lord,

37.play: We were the first and dearest of your friends.

38.play: For you my staff of office did I break

39.play: In Richard's time; and posted day and night

40.play: to meet you on the way, and kiss your hand,

41.play: When yet you were in place and in account

42.play: Nothing so strong and fortunate as I.

43.play: It was myself, my brother and his son,

44.play: That brought you home and boldly did outdare

45.play: The dangers of the time. You swore to us,

46.play: And you did swear that oath at Doncaster,

47.play: That you did nothing purpose 'gainst the state;

48.play: Nor claim no further than your new-fall'n right,

49.play: The seat of Gaunt, dukedom of Lancaster:
notes: seat] SCHMIDT (1874): Estate.

50.play: To this we swore our aid. But in short space

51.play: It rain'd down fortune showering on your head;

52.play: And such a flood of greatness fell on you,

53.play: What with our help, what with the absent king,

54.play: What with the injuries of a wanton time,
notes: wanton] SCHMIDT (1874): Loose, frivolous.—ONIONS (1911): Capricious.—COWL (ed. 1914): Disordered.—N. E. D. (1928) does not cite this passage, but defines wanton, as applied to persons, as rebellious, unruly; and, as applied to times, as effeminate, luxurious.

55.play: The seeming sufferances that you had borne,
notes: sufferance] SCHMIDT (1874): Distresses, miseries, sufferings. [Ten other examples of Shakespeare’s use of the word in this sense are cited.]

56.play: And the contrarious winds that held the king

57.play: So long in his unlucky Irish wars

58.play: That all in England did repute him dead:

59.play: And from this swarm of fair advantages
notes: And] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): The necessity of this emendation [cf. Textual Notes] is so glaring that it is almost an impertinence to make any remarks on it: neither had there been any, if the And had not gone down.

60.play: You took occasion to be quickly woo'd

61.play: To gripe the general sway into your hand;

62.play: Forget your oath to us at Doncaster;

63.play: And being fed by us you used us so

64.play: As that ungentle hull, the cuckoo's bird,

65.play: Useth the sparrow; did oppress our nest;

66.play: Grew by our feeding to so great a bulk

67.play: That even our love durst not come near your sight

68.play: For fear of swallowing; but with nimble wing
notes: feare of swallowing] Schmidt (1874, p. 1418): The gerund may have a passive sense even when it is not in the predicate. "The unback'd breeder, jealous of catching (i.e. of being caught)," Venus and Adonis, 320; "like one that fears robbing (being robbed)," Two Gentlemen, II, i, 22.

69.play: We were enforced, for safety sake, to fly
notes: we] Capell (Notes, 1779): The inattention and haste of [the former editors] is more notorious still in this passage [cf note on V, i, 55] ... for they who run might have perceived its corruption and the change that was requisite, unless they have run in their sleep. [Cf. Textual Note. Rann is again the only subsequent editor who did not "run in his sleep."—Ed]

70.play: Out of sight and raise this present head;

71.play: Whereby we stand opposed by such means
notes: opposed] Johnson (ed. 1765): In opposition to you.—Cowl (ed. 1914): In opposition to one another. See I, i, 9.

by such meanes] Cowl (ed. 1914): By reason of such grounds (of offence)—an elliptical construction. "By means of" sometimes means "by reason of," "owing to," as in Bacon's Henry VII: "Their bodies were buried by him (the priest) in some place which, by means of the priest's death, soon after could not be known.".

72.play: As you yourself have forged against yourself

notes: daungerous countenance] Cowl (ed. 1914): Haughty, threatening looks. Ascham, Schoolemaster, says that a young gentleman learns at Court "to face, stand foremost, shove back: and to the meaner man ... to seeme somewhat solemne, coy, big, and daungerous of looke." ... For "dangerous," threatening, see King John, IV, ii, 212: "To know the meaning Of dangerous Majesty when perchance it frowns".

74.play: And violation of all faith and troth

75.play: Sworn to us in your younger enterprise.

76.play: KING HENRY IV: These things indeed you have articulate,
notes: articulate] Steevens (Var. ed. 1773): Articulated, i.e., drawn out. 78-79. face . . . colour] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): An allusion to our ancient fantastic habits, which were usually faced, or turned up, with a colour different from that of which they were made [Cf Steevens’s note on IV, ii, 29.]—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): To face is to patch outwardly, so as to give it a good appearance . . . Similarly, to line is used figuratively of inward strengthening and support in II, iii, 80.

77.play: Proclaim'd at market-crosses, read in churches,

78.play: To face the garment of rebellion

79.play: With some fine colour that may please the eye

80.play: Of fickle changelings and poor discontents,
notes: changlings] SCHMIDT (1874): Inconstant persons, waverers.—COWL (ed. 1914): Turncoats, renegades, as in Heywood, The Royal King, III: “He scornes to be a changeling or a shifter,” and North’s Plutarch, Demosthenes: “He lost his life, because he would be no changeling.”. . . . Daniel (Civil Wars, i) writes that Bolingbroke was welcomed “Of th’altering vulgar, apt for changes still.”

81.play: Which gape and rub the elbow at the news
notes: rub the elbow] DOUCE (Illust. of Sh., 1807, 1, 444): The itching of the elbow, according to popular belief, denoted approaching change.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Apparently a sign of approval or satisfaction. See Love’s Labour’s Lost, V, ii, 109: “One rubbed his elbow thus, and fleer’d and swore A better speech was never spoke before.”. Cf. Guilpin, Skialetheia (ed. Grosart, p. 25): “He’l’e cry, oh rare, and scratch the elbow too To see two butchers’ curres fight.”
hurly burly innovation] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Change which throws everything into confusion.

83.play: And never yet did insurrection want

84.play: Such water-colours to impaint his cause;
notes: water colors to impaint] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Water colors, because they.

85.play: Nor moody beggars, starving for a time
notes: CAPELL (Notes, 1779): The poet’s meaning is express’d with too much conciseness: want (line 83) is carry’d forward into this line; but after moody beggars we want a very different verb from impaint, such as uphold it: starving is used too licentiously for “watching with the eagerness of men who are starved.”. [The meaning of the passage is much clearer than the meaning of Capell’s note. “Never did insurrection want (lack) pretexts or, as supporters, moody beggars eager for riot and insurrection as a means of livelihood.”. This seems a better explanation than Elton’s (ed 1889). He makes beggars “co-subject with insurrection to want.”—Ed.]
moody] SCHMIDT (1874): Discontented, angry.—COWL (ed. 1914): Either sullen, as in I, iii, 19, and in Comedy of Errors, V, i, 79, or angry, savagely desperate, as in II Henry IV, IV, iv, 39.

87.play: PRINCE HENRY: In both your armies there is many a soul
notes: your] ELTON (ed. 1889) and WRIGHT (ed. 1897) Percy's and Douglas's.—COWL (ed. 1914): The king's army and that of the rebels. . . . The Folio reading our may be right.

88.play: Shall pay full dearly for this encounter,

89.play: If once they join in trial. Tell your nephew,

90.play: The Prince of Wales doth join with all the world

91.play: In praise of Henry Percy: by my hopes,

92.play: This present enterprise set off his head,
notes: set . . . head] MUSGRAVE (Var. ed. 1778): Taken from his account.

93.play: I do not think a braver gentleman,

notes: active . . . yong] THEOBALD (ed. 1733): Without [the hyphens; see Textual Notes] the sense seems feeble and cold. The prince means, in my opinion, he did not know a braver gentleman than Hotspur, one more sprightly and stirring in his valour, or more valiant for his youth.—UPTON (Crit. Observ., 1746): Shakespeare uses adjectives adverbially. So here, more actively valiant or more valiantly young ... So, in Hamlet: "I am myself indifferent honest.". He is plainly imitating Sir Philip Sidney, who in his Astrophel and Stella thus speaks of Edward IV: "Nor that he could young-wise, wise-valiant, frame His sire's revenge."—CAPELL (Notes, 1779): His compounds are beautiful, and of easy conception, importing that Hotspur's valour was of the most active kind, and the degree of it greater than is usually found in such young years.

95.play: More daring or more bold, is now alive

96.play: To grace this latter age with noble deeds.

97.play: For my part, I may speak it to my shame,

98.play: I have a truant been to chivalry;

99.play: And so I hear he doth account me too;

100.play: Yet this before my father's majesty--
notes: GENTLEMAN (Int. to Bell’s ed, 1773): It is common for the king to be here seated on a drum, to rise at this line, when Falstaff, who is strangely seated behind him, tumbles down, to create a very ill-timed Bartholomew-Fair laugh. Can any one suppose the Fat Knight would venture so near the king? So contemptible a stage-trick should be suppressed.

101.play: I am content that he shall take the odds

102.play: Of his great name and estimation,

103.play: And will, to save the blood on either side,

104.play: Try fortune with him in a single fight.

105.play: KING HENRY IV: And, Prince of Wales, so dare we venture thee,

107.play: Do make against it. No, good Worcester, no,

108.play: We love our people well; even those we love

109.play: That are misled upon your cousin's part;
notes: coosens part] SCHMIDT (1874): Kinsman’s party.

110.play: And, will they take the offer of our grace,
notes: will] THEOBALD (Letter to Warb., Jan. 15, 1730): The Editor [i.e., Pope,.

111.play: Both he and they and you, every man

112.play: Shall be my friend again and I'll be his:

113.play: So tell your cousin, and bring me word
notes: ABBOTT (Sh Gram., 1870, p. 411): Lines with four accents are very rare, unless there is a pause in the middle of the line. [Cf. III, i, 70, and V, i, 27, note.—Ed.]—ELTON (ed. 1889): Bring is, possibly, pronounced as a dissyllable (be-ring), as Henry is three (Hen-e-ry) in III Henry VI, III, ii, 90.

114.play: What he will do: but if he will not yield,

115.play: Rebuke and dread correction wait on us
notes: waight on vs] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Wait on us, are in our service.

116.play: And they shall do their office. So, be gone;

117.play: We will not now be troubled with reply:

118.play: We offer fair; take it advisedly.
notes: take it aduisedly] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Consider it with deliberation.

120.play: PRINCE HENRY: It will not be accepted, on my life:

121.play: The Douglas and the Hotspur both together
notes: The . . . the] ABBOTT (Sh Gram., 1870, p 66): It is not often that the is so used [i.e., to denote fame or notoriety] before English proper names. In this line the second the may be caused by the first, which, of course, is still used,—“the Bruce,” “the Douglas,” being frequent, and referring to the chief of the Bruces and Douglases.

122.play: Are confident against the world in arms.

123.play: KING HENRY IV: Hence, therefore, every leader to his charge;

124.play: For, on their answer, will we set on them:

125.play: And God befriend us, as our cause is just!

126.play: [Exeunt all but PRINCE HENRY and FALSTAFF]
notes: See Appendix: Sources of the Plot A E. Morgan, section 21.

127.play: FALSTAFF: Hal, if thou see me down in the battle and bestride
notes: [In The Dramatic Censor, 1770, Gentleman comments, at this juncture, on the “contemptible piece of buffoonery” which, in Bell’s ed., he assigns to line 200 of this scene (see note on that line). The “business” would be less inappropriate here than there, and would give additional meaning to Falstaff’s next speech.—Ed ] 127-128. bestride] Steevens (Var ed 1773): In the battle of Agincourt, Henry, when king, did this act of friendship for his brother, the duke of Gloucester.

128.play: me, so; 'tis a point of friendship.

129.play: PRINCE HENRY: Nothing but a colossus can do thee that friendship.

131.play: FALSTAFF: I would 'twere bed-time, Hal, and all well.
notes: bed time] Elton (ed. 1889): Suggested by the prince’s “Say thy prayers.”.

132.play: PRINCE HENRY: Why, thou owest God a death.

134.play: FALSTAFF: 'Tis not due yet; I would be loath to pay him before

135.play: his day. What need I be so forward with him that

136.play: calls not on me? Well, 'tis no matter; honour pricks

137.play: me on. Yea, but how if honour prick me off when I

138.play: come on? how then? Can honour set to a leg? no: or
notes: set to] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Put on a leg that is cut off.—COWL (ed' 1914): Huloet (Dictionnaire, 1572) has: "Set to. Appono, Mettre, Apposer' Adiustre.". The Folio too is a common misprint for to.

139.play: an arm? no: or take away the grief of a wound? no.
notes: griefe] SCHMIDT (1874): Pain. Cf. I, iii, 51. 141-142. what...aire] COWL (Sources of the Text, 1928): Daniel, A Pastorall, 1592. "that name, that Idle name of wind...that empty sound call'd Honour". Daniel's Pastorall is a translation of Tasso's Aminta, I, ii; and there we find: "quel vano nome senza soggetto.... Quel che da'l volgo insano Onor...fu detto...Vattene.".

140.play: Honour hath no skill in surgery, then? no. What is

141.play: honour? a word. What is in that word honour? what

142.play: is that honour? air. A trim reckoning! Who hath it?

143.play: he that died o' Wednesday. Doth he feel it? no.

144.play: Doth he hear it? no. 'Tis insensible, then. Yea,
notes: insensible] N. E. D. (1901): Not to be apprehended by the senses.

145.play: to the dead. But will it not live with the living?

146.play: no. Why? detraction will not suffer it. Therefore

147.play: I'll none of it. Honour is a mere scutcheon: and so
notes: meere skutchion] WARBU.

2.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: O, no, my nephew must not know, Sir Richard,
notes: and] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Qq-4 and Ff omit and, apparently for the sake of the metre, which is perfectly good with it.

3.play: The liberal and kind offer of the king.
notes: under one] COWL (ed. 1914), with all other editors, adopts the reading.

5.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: Then are we all undone.

6.play: It is not possible, it cannot be,

7.play: The king should keep his word in loving us;

8.play: He will suspect us still and find a time

9.play: To punish this offence in other faults:
notes: in other faults] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): When punishing other faults.

10.play: Suspicion all our lives shall be stuck full of eyes;
notes: ROWE’s emendation in his second edition (see Textual Notes) has been almost universally accepted, though many attempts have been made to regularize the metre of this line—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 401) cites the line as one of five pure Alexandrines in Shakespeare. Its purity, of course, depends upon the acceptance of Rowe’s emendation.—PERRING (Hard Knots in Sh., 1885) offers the strange suggestion that some one thought the whole line “supposititious,” and indicated his suspicion by a marginal note which the printers interpreted as part of the text. He would omit the line altogether.—COWL (ed. 1914) suggests the possibility of making the line decasyllabic by omitting all our lives and pronouncing Supposition as it is pronounced in Davenant’s Cruel Brother, 1630, V, i: “Supposition with arguments of strength.”—IBID. (Notes on Text, 1927, p. 18): In Jonson’s Masque of Queens, in the line “Thy fearful sister, wild Suspicion,” Suspicion is a word of four syllables.—JOHNSON (ed. 1765): The image of Suspicion is exhibited in a Latin tragedy called Roxana, written about the same time, by Dr. Alabaster.—COWL (ed. 1914): The imagery may be traced back to Vergil, Aeneid, iv, 180, and Ezekiel, x, 12.

11.play: For treason is but trusted like the fox,

12.play: Who, ne'er so tame, so cherish'd and lock'd up,

13.play: Will have a wild trick of his ancestors.

14.play: Look how we can, or sad or merrily,

notes: COWL (ed. 1914): Unfriendly critics will put an ill construction upon our looks.

16.play: And we shall feed like oxen at a stall,

17.play: The better cherish'd, still the nearer death.

18.play: My nephew's trespass may be well forgot;

19.play: it hath the excuse of youth and heat of blood,

20.play: And an adopted name of privilege,
notes: adopted...priueldge] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): The name of Hotspur will privilege him from censure.—COWL (ed. 1914): A “hotspur” was a common name for an impulsive person, but, according to the N. E. D., the word first occurs as surname to Sir Henry Percy.

21.play: A hair-brain'd Hotspur, govern'd by a spleen:
notes: hair-braind] COWL (ed. 1914): Hare-brain: rash, impulsive. . . . Wilkins, Essay towards a Real Character, 1668, distinguishes between “hare-brained,” rash, and “hair-brained,” conceited.—N. E. D (1902): Hare-brained: Having no more sense than a hare. The spelling hair-brained, suggesting another origin for the compound, is later, though occasional before 1600.

22.play: All his offences live upon my head

23.play: And on his father's; we did train him on,

24.play: And, his corruption being ta'en from us,
notes: corruption] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): Perhaps, a taint, an infection.—HEATH (Revisal, 1765, p. 254): But why should we take pains to pick holes in a sound place? The verb take signifies to catch by infection.

25.play: We, as the spring of all, shall pay for all.

26.play: Therefore, good cousin, let not Harry know,

27.play: In any case, the offer of the king.

28.play: VERNON: Deliver what you will; I'll say 'tis so.

32.play: Deliver up my Lord of Westmoreland.
notes: CAPELL (ed. 1768, and Notes, 1779), [again followed by the faithful RANN, assigns this line to Hotspur, for the following reasons]: Hotspur, who from his station, his temper, and every consideration imaginable, must have been the first to take fire at his uncle's intelligence, which moreover is directed to him, is made the second in copies [i.e., in former editions]; for this line is taken from him and given to Douglas. [Capell's implication is unfair and misleading.
