If this line had been assigned to Hotspur in Qq and Ff, we should indeed condemn those who have “taken it from him”; or if the line had been assigned to him in some of the Qq or Ff, we might be inclined to follow the reading of such copies; but as there is no authority for assigning it to Hotspur, and as it is appropriate to Douglas, we must rank this suggestion of Capell's among the foolish suggestions occasionally offered by him.—MALONE (ed. 1790) supports Capell in a note, but assigns the line to Douglas.—ED.]

34.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: The king will bid you battle presently.

35.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Defy him by the Lord of Westmoreland.
notes: Douglas] MALONE (ed. 1790): A trisyllable.—WALKER (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 7) lists this word and line under the following rule: “Words such as juggler, tickling, kindling, England, angry, children, &c., are often pronounced as though a vowel were interposed between the liquid and the preceding mute.

36.play: HOTSPUR: Lord Douglas, go you and tell him so.

37.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Marry, and shall, and very willingly.
notes: and shall] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): The and is made I by the four latter moderns [see Textual Notes], an unnecessary word, destructive of harmony, and of spirit and ease likewise.—COWL (ed. 1914): “Indeed, and I shall!”—an expression of eager assent; cf. Jonson, Volpone, V, i: “Sir, I must have a fairer answer. Mos. Madam! Marry, and shall!”

39.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: There is no seeming mercy in the king.
notes: seeming mercie] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Mercy, even in appearance.

40.play: HOTSPUR: Did you beg any? God forbid!

41.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: I told him gently of our grievances,

42.play: Of his oath-breaking; which he mended thus,

43.play: By now forswearing that he is forsworn:
notes: now] WALKER (Crit. Exam. of Text, 1860) cites seven passages in Shakespeare, including this line, in which he thinks the printers have mistaken new for now, e.g., Shrew, III, ii, 55 “now repaired with knots.”

44.play: He calls us rebels, traitors; and will scourge

45.play: With haughty arms this hateful name in us.

47.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Arm, gentlemen; to arms! for I have thrown

48.play: A brave defiance in King Henry's teeth,

49.play: And Westmoreland, that was engaged, did bear it;
notes: ingag’d] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Delivered as a hostage.

50.play: Which cannot choose but bring him quickly on.

51.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: The Prince of Wales stepp'd forth before the king,

52.play: And, nephew, challenged you to single fight.

53.play: HOTSPUR: O, would the quarrel lay upon our heads,

54.play: And that no man might draw short breath today

55.play: But I and Harry Monmouth! Tell me, tell me,
notes: Monmouth] HUDSON (ed. 1880): Henry was so named from the town in Wales where he was born.

56.play: How show'd his tasking? seem'd it in contempt?
notes: tasking] CAPELL (Notes, 1779) Challenge—MALONE (ed 1790): I know not whether “tasking” is not used here for “taxing,” or satirical representation. So, in As You Like It, II, vii, 86 “My taxing, like a wild goose, flies” “Tasking,” however, is sufficiently intelligible in its more usual acceptance: we still say “he took him to task”—COWL (ed. 1914). Task and tax (to accuse or censure) are metathetical forms of the same word.

57.play: VERNON: No, by my soul; I never in my life

58.play: Did hear a challenge urged more modestly,

59.play: Unless a brother should a brother dare

60.play: To gentle exercise and proof of arms.

61.play: He gave you all the duties of a man;
notes: duties of a man] CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 312): Duties, used elliptically for “qualities duly belonging to.”—COWL (ed. 1914). A manly observance and respect, cf. Hamlet, I, ii, 87: “Tis sweet and commendable in your nature, Hamlet, To give these mourning duties to your father,” i.e., dues and observances of mourning I think “of a man” refers to the prince, and not to Hotspur, the words are perhaps parallel to “with a princely tongue” in line 57, and “like a chronicle” in line 58. Minsheu defines “Dutie” as “that which by law of nature or civil respect is due to one” [Cowl’s interpretation is attractive, and possible; but it is not as simple and obvious as that given by Clarke and many others —Ed]

62.play: Trimm's up your praises with a princely tongue,

63.play: Spoke to your deservings like a chronicle,

64.play: Making you ever better than his praise

65.play: By still dispraising praise valued in you;

66.play: And, which became him like a prince indeed,

67.play: He made a blushing cital of himself;
notes: blushing citall] POPE (ed. 1723): Cital: taxation—COLLINS (Var. '73, Appendix, vol. x): I rather think cital means recital. The verb is used in that sense in The Two Gentlemen of Verona, IV, i, 53; Henry V, V, ii, 70; Titus Andronicus, V, iii, 117.—ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 339): Recital. [Abbott gives over fifty examples of the dropping of prefixes in similar words. Modern editors agree in defining the word as mention or recital, and find the idea of self-accusation sufficiently expressed in blushing.—ED.]

68.play: And chid his truant youth with such a grace

69.play: As if he master'd there a double spirit.
notes: mastred] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): Was master of.—ELTON (ed. 1889): Owned.

70.play: Of teaching and of learning instantly.
notes: instantly] COLLIER (ed. 1842): At the same instant. [N. E. D. gives no such definition.—COWL (ed. 1914) is probably correct in defining it as diligently, earnestly, urgently; cf. Luke, vii, 4: "They besought him instantly."]

71.play: There did he pause: but let me tell the world,

72.play: If he outlive the envy of this day,
notes: envie of this day] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The hostile malice to which he will be exposed today.

73.play: England did never owe so sweet a hope,
notes: owe] SINGER (ed. 1826): Own.

74.play: So much misconstrued in his wantonness.
notes: wantonnesse] Cowl (ed. 1914): In respect to, or as a consequence of, the levity of his behaviour.

75.play: HOTSPUR: Cousin, I think thou art enamoured

76.play: On his follies: never did I hear

77.play: Of any prince so wild a libertine.
notes: prince . . libertie] Theobald (ed 1733): a Liberty. Thus Mr. Pope has given it us in both his editions, as if a liberty could mean a libertine. Whether chance or purpose be the source of this reading is not easy to determine: for besides that this gentleman's indolence is so singular, his vein of criticism is so extravagant, that, like our author's fools, he is seldom or never called to account for his rhetoric! I have restored the reading of the old copies [But not of the oldest (see Textual Notes) which is here followed by the “indolent" Mr. Pope. Theobald did not know Q1, but that he did know Q2 is shown by his note on I, ii, 40, old lad of the castle He, however, chose to ignore that "old copy" in his attack, here, on Pope.—Ed.] The meaning is that a prince of so wild and licentious a behaviour should not be suffered at liberty for fear of doing mischief.—Johnson (ed 1765): "I never heard of any prince that played such tricks and was not confined as a madman."—Steevens (ed. 1793): The oldest reading may be the true one, for in The Comedy of Errors, I, ii, 101, the same phraseology occurs: "prating mountebanks, And many suchlike liberties of sin."—Malone (Var. ed. 1821): I doubt whether the passage quoted from The Comedy of Errors has the meaning assigned to it by Mr. Steevens.—Singer (ed. 1826): “So wild at liberty” may mean "so wild and licentious in his conduct." I much incline to "so wild a libertine."—Knight (ed. 1839): So wild of his own unrestrained will.—White (ed. 1859): Never did I hear so wild a liberty reported of any prince.—Hudson (ed. 1880): "So wild o'liberty" plainly means, using his freedom so wantonly. [That libertie was a misprint for libertine in Q1, seems to be the best opinion, although White's ingenious interpretation makes good sense out of the reading of Q1.—Ed.]

78.play: But be he as he will, yet once ere night

79.play: I will embrace him with a soldier's arm,

80.play: That he shall shrink under my courtesy.

81.play: Arm, arm with speed: and, fellows, soldiers, friends,

82.play: Better consider what you have to do

83.play: Than I, that have not well the gift of tongue,

84.play: Can lift your blood up with persuasion.

86.play: Messenger: My lord, here are letters for you.

87.play: HOTSPUR: I cannot read them now.

88.play: O gentlemen, the time of life is short!

89.play: To spend that shortness basely were too long,

90.play: If life did ride upon a dial's point,
notes: dials point] COWL (ed. 1914): The hand or needle of a clock or sundial.

91.play: Still ending at the arrival of an hour.
notes: Still] Always.

92.play: An if we live, we live to tread on kings;

93.play: If die, brave death, when princes die with us!

94.play: Now, for our consciences, the arms are fair,
notes: for our consciences] Wright (ed. 1897): As regards our consciences.

95.play: When the intent of bearing them is just.
notes: intent of bearing] Wright (ed. 1897): Object for which we bear.

97.play: Messenger: My lord, prepare; the king comes on apace.

98.play: HOTSPUR: I thank him, that he cuts me from my tale,

99.play: For I profess not talking; only this--

100.play: Let each man do his best: and here draw I

101.play: A sword, whose temper I intend to stain

102.play: With the best blood that I can meet withal

103.play: In the adventure of this perilous day.

104.play: Now, Esperance! Percy! and set on.
notes: Esperance] See Appendix: Sources of the Plot: Holinshed—Pope (ed. 1723): Esperance, the word of battle on Percy's side.—Percy (Var. ed. 1773): Esperance, or Esperanza, has always been the motto of the Percy family. . . "Esperance ma Conforte" is still legible over the great gate at Alnwick.—Capell (Notes, 1779): Esperance must be made of four syllables, more Gallico.

105.play: Sound all the lofty instruments of war,

106.play: And by that music let us all embrace;

107.play: For, heaven to earth, some of us never shall
notes: heaven to earth] Warburton (ed. 1747): "One might wager heaven to earth."—Singer (N. & Q., May 15, 1852): This could hardly be tortured to fit Warburton's paraphrase, a strange thought to enter Hotspur's mind at such a time. The context requires "here on earth." [Warburton's interpretation has for once, however, satisfied most of his successors, and is generally accepted].

108.play: A second time do such a courtesy.

109.play: [The trumpets sound. They embrace, and exeunt]

1.play: [Alarum. Excursions. Enter PRINCE HENRY, LORD JOHN OF LANCASTER, and EARL OF WESTMORELAND]

3.play: Harry, withdraw thyself; thou bleed'st too much.
notes: thou bleedest] STEEVENS (Var. ed. 1778): History says that the prince was wounded in the eye by an arrow—MALONE (ed 1790): All the chronicles that I have seen say that he was wounded in the face. It is not easy to conceive how he could continue fighting after being wounded in the eye.

4.play: Lord John of Lancaster, go you with him.

5.play: LANCASTER: Not I, my lord, unless I did bleed too.

6.play: PRINCE HENRY: I beseech your majesty, make up,
notes: make vp] CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, pp. 346, 381): Bring up your forces: an idiomatic expression. Cf. King John, III, ii, 5.

7.play: Lest your retirement do amaze your friends.
notes: retirement] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Dunbar, Earl of March, had withdrawn the king from that part of the field where Hotspur and Douglas made their attack on the royal standard.
dismay] COWL (ed. 1914): Fill with dismay. N. E. D. cites Drayton, Odes, xvii, 27: "Though they be one to ten, Be not amazed." [Cf. Holinshed: "stricken fear into their hearts."—Ed.]

8.play: KING HENRY IV: I will do so.

9.play: My Lord of Westmoreland, lead him to his tent.

10.play: WESTMORELAND: Come, my lord, I'll lead you to your tent.

11.play: PRINCE HENRY: Lead me, my lord? I do not need your help:

12.play: And God forbid a shallow scratch should drive

13.play: The Prince of Wales from such a field as this,

14.play: Where stain'd nobility lies trodden on,

15.play: and rebels' arms triumph in massacres!

16.play: LANCASTER: We breathe too long: come, cousin Westmoreland,

17.play: Our duty this way lies; for God's sake come.

19.play: PRINCE HENRY: By God, thou hast deceived me, Lancaster;

20.play: I did not think thee lord of such a spirit:

21.play: Before, I loved thee as a brother, John;

22.play: But now, I do respect thee as my soul.

23.play: KING HENRY IV: I saw him hold Lord Percy at the point

24.play: With lustier maintenance than I did look for
notes: lustier maintenance] SCHMIDT (1874): More gallant deportment, or carriage—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Stouter endurance or resistance.

30.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: Another king! they grow like Hydra's heads:
notes: like Hydraes heads] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Which grew as fast again as they were cut off. [Cf. Daniel’s Civil Wars (1595), iii, 86, in Appendix: Sources of the Plot.—Ed.]

31.play: I am the Douglas, fatal to all those

32.play: That wear those colours on them: what art thou,

33.play: That counterfeit'st the person of a king?

34.play: KING HENRY IV: The king himself; who, Douglas, grieves at heart

35.play: So many of his shadows thou hast met

36.play: And not the very king. I have two boys

37.play: Seek Percy and thyself about the field:

38.play: But, seeing thou fall'st on me so luckily,

39.play: I will assay thee: so, defend thyself.
notes: assay] COWL (ed. 1914): Attack. Cf. Henryson, The Paddock: “Death will thee assay, Thou wait not quhen.” [Or, perhaps, merely “make trial of your strength.”—Ed.]

40.play: EARL OF DOUGLAS: I fear thou art another counterfeit;

41.play: And yet, in faith, thou bear'st thee like a king:

42.play: But mine I am sure thou art, whoe'er thou be,

44.play: [They fight. KING HENRY being in danger, PRINCE HENRY enters]

45.play: PRINCE HENRY: Hold up thy head, vile Scot, or thou art like

46.play: Never to hold it up again! the spirits

47.play: Of valiant Shirley, Stafford, Blunt, are in my arms:
notes: Sherly] CAPELL (Notes, 1779): Holinshed spells the word Shorley, and calls the party Sir Hugh; putting him among the slain on the king’s side along with Sir John Clifton and Sir Nicholas Gausell [cf. lines 52, 53].—WALKER

48.play: It is the Prince of Wales that threatens thee;

49.play: Who never promiseth but he means to pay.
notes: MASON (Comments on Last Ed., 1785): This [cf. Folio reading, Textual Notes] agrees with what the prince says in the first act: "And pay the debt I never promised."

promisetli] WALKER (Sh. Vers., 1854, p. 66): There are certain classes of words, the greater part of them composed of two short syllables, punish, flourish, promise, &c. . . . which are frequently contracted into one syllable, or placed in monosyllabic places in the line. Cf. Comedy of Errors, V, i, 222: "Prom'sing to bring it to the Porcupine" with "Who never prom'seth but he means to pay."

51.play: Cheerly, my lord how fares your grace?

52.play: Sir Nicholas Gawsey hath for succor sent,
notes: Gawsey] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Sir Nicholas Goushill of Hoveringham, co. Notts, who, with his son Sir Robert, was killed at Shrewsbury. [See note on line 47.]

53.play: And so hath Clifton: I'll to Clifton straight.
notes: Clifton] WRIGHT (ed. 1897): Sir John Clifton, Knight of the Shire of Nottingham. [See note on line 47.]

54.play: KING HENRY IV: Stay, and breathe awhile:

55.play: Thou hast redeem'd thy lost opinion,
notes: opinion] Capell (Notes, 1779): Character, the opinion I had of you.—Reed (Var. ed. 1785): Reputation. So, ... in Shirley's Gamester: "You have the opinion of a valiant gentleman."—Wright (ed. 1897): See III, ii, 42, IV, i, 77

56.play: And show'd thou makest some tender of my life,
notes: makst some tender] Clarke (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 346, et cet.): Hast some loving regard for, cf. As You Like It, V, ii, 65: "By my life I do. which I tender dearly."

57.play: In this fair rescue thou hast brought to me.

58.play: PRINCE HENRY: O God! they did me too much injury

59.play: That ever said I hearken'd for your death.
notes: harkned for] Cowl (ed. 1914): Desired; cf. Taming of the Shrew, I, ii, 256.

60.play: If it were so, I might have let alone

61.play: The insulting hand of Douglas over you,
notes: insulting...ouer] Walker (Crit. Exam., 1860, i, 160-162) lists this phrase under the heading Peculiar Construction with the Adjective. Among other passages cited are Lear, III, ii: "Thou simular man of virtue" for "Thou man of simulated virtue"; and I Henry IV, V, i: "A portent of broached mischief" for "A portent of mischief broached to."

62.play: Which would have been as speedy in your end

63.play: As all the poisonous potions in the world

64.play: And saved the treacherous labour of your son.

65.play: KING HENRY IV: Make up to Clifton: I'll to Sir Nicholas Gawsey.

68.play: HOTSPUR: If I mistake not, thou art Harry Monmouth.

69.play: PRINCE HENRY: Thou speak'st as if I would deny my name.

70.play: HOTSPUR: My name is Harry Percy.

71.play: PRINCE HENRY: Why, then I see

72.play: A very valiant rebel of the name.

73.play: I am the Prince of Wales; and think not, Percy,

74.play: To share with me in glory any more:
notes: Seymour (Remarks, 1805): But heretofore the glory was all Percy’s.

75.play: Two stars keep not their motion in one sphere;
notes: Cowl (ed. 1914): A proverbial expression. . . . In the text there is an allusion to the opinion of Ptolemy that each planet has its own orb or sphere in which it moves. Cf. V, i, 21, note.

76.play: Nor can one England brook a double reign,

77.play: Of Harry Percy and the Prince of Wales.

78.play: HOTSPUR: Nor shall it, Harry; for the hour is come

79.play: To end the one of us; and would to God

80.play: Thy name in arms were now as great as mine!

81.play: PRINCE HENRY: I'll make it greater ere I part from thee;

82.play: And all the budding honours on thy crest
notes: honors . . . crest] See Hartman's note on line 110, favours.

83.play: I'll crop, to make a garland for my head.

84.play: HOTSPUR: I can no longer brook thy vanities.
notes: vanities] Schmidt (1874): Frivolities.

87.play: FALSTAFF: Well said, Hal! to it Hal! Nay, you shall find no
notes: said] Collier (ed. 1842): That is, done.—Cowden Clarke (ed. 1914): An expression of approval, used here as often elsewhere when nothing has been said.

88.play: boy's play here, I can tell you.

89.play: [Re-enter DOUGLAS; he fights with FALSTAFF, who falls down as if he were dead, and exit DOUGLAS. HOTSPUR is wounded, and falls]

90.play: HOTSPUR: O, Harry, thou hast robb'd me of my youth!
notes: youth] THEOBALD (Letter to Warburton, Dec 23, 1729) "Robbed me of my youth?" Why does Hotspur level his complaint here? He a little before wishes Henry's name were as great in arms as his own ... I have a strong suspicion that the poet wrote worth, i.e., thou hast cut off all the fame of my budding honours by this conquest—WARBURTON (ed 1747) I fancy Shakespeare wrote growth, i.e., honours in the bud. The two following lines seem to point to this reading, and not to agree with the former one—WRIGHT (ed 1897) My youth: not only my young, vigorous life, but all the honours it has brought me

91.play: I better brook the loss of brittle life

92.play: Than those proud titles thou hast won of me;

93.play: They wound my thoughts worse than sword my flesh:

94.play: But thought's the slave of life, and life time's fool;
notes: life times] [The comma after life in Ff₁ illustrates the principle of punctuation discussed by SIMPSON (Sh. Punct., 1911, p 53), under the heading "Comma marking an ellipse of copula." Cf. Richard II, I, i, 159: "We'll calm the Duke of Norfolk; you, your son"; and As You Like It, II, vii, 139. "All the world's a stage, And all the men and women, merely players "—ED.]

95.play: And time, that takes survey of all the world,

96.play: Must have a stop. O, I could prophesy,
notes: I...prophecy] STAUNTON (ed. 1858): The belief that the dying are endowed with a faculty of prevision is of high authority. Shakespeare has before illustrated the superstition in Richard II, II, i, 31, 32: "Methinks I am a prophet new inspired, And thus, expiring, do foretell of him"

97.play: But that the earthy and cold hand of death

98.play: Lies on my tongue: no, Percy, thou art dust

101.play: PRINCE HENRY: For worms, brave Percy: fare thee well, great heart!

102.play: Ill-weaved ambition, how much art thou shrunk!
notes: Ill...shrunk] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): A metaphor taken from cloth, which shrinks when it is ill-weaved, when its texture is loose.

103.play: When that this body did contain a spirit,

104.play: A kingdom for it was too small a bound;

105.play: But now two paces of the vilest earth

106.play: Is room enough: this earth that bears thee dead

107.play: Bears not alive so stout a gentleman.
notes: stout] SCHMIDT (1874): Bold and resolute.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): This is the only sense in which the word was originally used.

108.play: If thou wert sensible of courtesy,

109.play: I should not make so dear a show of zeal:
notes: deare] COWL (ed. 1914): Dear has, here, merely an intensive force.

110.play: But let my favours hide thy mangled face;
notes: favours] WARBURTON (ed. 1747): We should read favour, face or countenance. He is stooping down to kiss Hotspur.—JOHNSON (ed. 1765): He,

111.play: And, even in thy behalf, I'll thank myself

112.play: For doing these fair rites of tenderness.

113.play: Adieu, and take thy praise with thee to heaven!

114.play: Thy ignominy sleep with thee in the grave,
notes: ignominy] COWL (ed. 1914): A trisyllable, as in Paradise Lost, I, 115 ... The Folio is inconsistent in its use of the two forms [ignominy and ignomy; see Textual Notes] Here and in Troilus, V, x, 33 (Q ignominie) it reads ignomy; but in Titus, IV, ii, 115, it substitutes ignominy for (Q) ignomy; and in Measure for Measure, II, iv, 111, retains (Q) ignomy where the verse requires ignominy.

115.play: But not remember'd in thy epitaph!

117.play: What, old acquaintance! could not all this flesh

118.play: Keep in a little life? Poor Jack, farewell!

119.play: I could have better spared a better man:

120.play: O, I should have a heavy miss of thee,
notes: have . . . thee] COWL (ed. 1914): Miss thee grievously, with a quibbling reference to Falstaff's weight.

121.play: If I were much in love with vanity!

122.play: Death hath not struck so fat a deer to-day,
notes: fat] [See Textual Notes.] TOLLET (Var. ed. 1778): Fat seems the better reading, for Turberville, in The Terms . . . of Venerie and the Chace, observes: “You shall say by any deare, a great deare, and not a faire deare, unless it be a rowe.”

123.play: Though many dearer, in this bloody fray.
notes: dearer] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Of greater worth.

124.play: Embowell'd will I see thee by and by:
notes: Imbowelled] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Exenterated.—ANON (Desultory Comments, 1801): Of such brutality Prince Henry was incapable. The meaning is “put into the bowels of the earth.”—DOUCE (Illust. of Sh., 1807, i, 445): Surely the prince designs no more than that Falstaff's body shall be embalmed in 117-125.] MORRIS ARNOLD (Soh. of Sh., 1911, p 93): Shakespeare employs the overheard soliloquy for comic purposes. Unless the actor impersonating Falstaff should take the liberty of indulging in a surreptitious grimace, the fun is not apparent during the prince's speech, but explodes in the first word of the ensuing monologue by Falstaff.

125.play: Till then in blood by noble Percy lie.

127.play: FALSTAFF: [Rising up] Embowelled! if thou embowel me to-day,

128.play: I'll give you leave to powder me and eat me too
notes: powder] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): Salt.

130.play: that hot termagant Scot had paid me scot and lot too.
notes: paid] COWL (ed. 1914): Falstaff quibbles on the two meanings of paid, i.e., despatched (as in V, iii, 46), and paid, in the literal sense. 136-137. The... discretion] COWL (ed. 1914): “The wisdom and discretion of man,” writes Saviola (Of Honour, 1595), “is as great a virtue as his magnanimity and courage, which are so much the greater virtues by how much they are accompanied with wisdom: for without them a man is not to be accounted valiant, but rather furious.” See Beaumont and Fletcher, King and No King, IV, iii: “It shew’d discretion, the better part of valour;” and Humorous Lieutenant, I, i: “Valour’s best companion, staid Discretion.”

131.play: Counterfeit? I lie, I am no counterfeit: to die,

132.play: is to be a counterfeit; for he is but the

133.play: counterfeit of a man who hath not the life of a man:

134.play: but to counterfeit dying, when a man thereby

135.play: liveth, is to be no counterfeit, but the true and

136.play: perfect image of life indeed. The better part of

137.play: valour is discretion; in the which better part I

138.play: have saved my life.'Zounds, I am afraid of this

139.play: gunpowder Percy, though he be dead: how, if he

140.play: should counterfeit too and rise? by my faith, I am

141.play: afraid he would prove the better counterfeit.

142.play: Therefore I'll make him sure; yea, and I'll swear I

143.play: killed him. Why may not he rise as well as I?

144.play: Nothing confutes me but eyes, and nobody sees me.

147.play: with a new wound in your thigh, come you along with me.

notes: GENTLEMAN (Bell’s ed, 1773): The son of sack’s rolling and tumbling about the stage, to get Hotspur on his back, is too much in the style of pantomime mummery. . . . Such ludicrous attacks upon reason are beneath Shakespeare.—DAVIES (Dram. Misc., 1785): No joke ever raised such loud and repeated mirth in the galleries as Sir John’s labour in getting the body of Percy on his back. . . . How Booth and Harper managed this pantomimic scene is not easy to tell. Booth’s weight and roundness of figure would render the bulky Harper’s lifting him worse than walking a hundred yards on uneven ground! Quin had little or no difficulty in perching Garrick on his shoulders, who looked like a dwarf on the back of a giant. But, oh! how he toiled to raise Barry from the ground As they were rivals, and occasionally jarred, we may without breach of charity suppose that Hotspur sometimes enjoyed the sweat of Falstaff. . . . At length this upper-gallery merriment was done away with

149.play: [Re-enter PRINCE HENRY and LORD JOHN OF LANCASTER]

150.play: PRINCE HENRY: Come, brother John; full bravely hast thou flesh'd

152.play: LANCASTER: But, soft! whom have we here?

153.play: Did you not tell me this fat man was dead?

154.play: PRINCE HENRY: I did; I saw him dead,

155.play: Breathless and bleeding on the ground. Art

157.play: Or is it fantasy that plays upon our eyesight?

158.play: I prithee, speak; we will not trust our eyes

159.play: Without our ears: thou art not what thou seem'st.

160.play: FALSTAFF: No, that's certain; I am not a double man: but if I
notes: double man] JOHNSON (ed. 1765): I am not Percy and Falstaff together, though having Percy on my back I seem double.—N. E. D. (1897): Double (sb. ac): The apparition of a living person; a wraith, fetch.—COWL (ed. 1914): With a play perhaps on the other meaning of double. The word doubleman, in the sense of apparition, occurs in Robert Kirk’s Secret Commonwealth, 1691 (ed. Andrew Lang, p. 9). [Falstaff’s use of the word in this sense is earlier than any cited in the N. E. D.]

161.play: be not Jack Falstaff, then am I a Jack. There is Percy:
notes: Iacke] See III, iii, 81, note.

COLLIER (ed. 1858): This stage-direction is found nowhere but in my corrected folio of 1632 [but see Textual Notes], and Mr Singer has copied it without observation [the injustice of this accusation is apparent.—ED.] It shows what was the practise of our old stage, and what ought to have been the practise on the modern stage, viz, that Falstaff, instead of keeping the corpse of Hotspur on his back until the end of the scene, should relieve himself of the burden by throwing it down. When the dialogue with the two princes was at an end, he did not pick up the body, but dragged it off.—DYCE (Strictures on Collier, 1859): Was there ever an editor who made so many random assertions? Before accusing Mr. Singer, why did he not look at the passage in later editions? ... Besides, Mr. Collier is quite in the dark about "the practise of the modern stage." ... I never saw a Falstaff that did so, and I have seen several;—but as in all probability Mr. Collier will not take my word for it, I call in the unimpeachable testimony of Oxberry's acting edition of the play, 1822.

163.play: if your father will do me any honour, so; if not, let

164.play: him kill the next Percy himself. I look to be either

165.play: earl or duke, I can assure you.

166.play: PRINCE HENRY: Why, Percy I killed myself and saw thee dead.

167.play: FALSTAFF: Didst thou? Lord, Lord, how this world is given to

168.play: lying! I grant you I was down and out of breath;

169.play: and so was he: but we rose both at an instant and

170.play: fought a long hour by Shrewsbury clock. If I may be
notes: Shrewsburie clocke] CLARKE (Sh. Key, 1879, p. 97): This mention of the church-clock by its name gives humorous effect of pretended exactness and serves as reminder of the site of the scene.

171.play: believed, so; if not, let them that should reward

172.play: valour bear the sin upon their own heads. I'll take

173.play: it upon my death, I gave him this wound in the

174.play: thigh: if the man were alive and would deny it,

175.play: 'zounds, I would make him eat a piece of my sword.

176.play: LANCASTER: This is the strangest tale that ever I heard.

177.play: PRINCE HENRY: This is the strangest fellow, brother John.

178.play: Come, bring your luggage nobly on your back:

179.play: For my part, if a lie may do thee grace,

180.play: I'll gild it with the happiest terms I have.

182.play: The trumpet sounds retreat; the day is ours.

183.play: Come, brother, let us to the highest of the field,
notes: highest of the field] SCHMIDT (1874): The farthest visible part of the field.—WRIGHT (ed. 1897): The highest ground, from which the whole field could be seen.

184.play: To see what friends are living, who are dead.

186.play: FALSTAFF: I'll follow, as they say, for reward. He that

187.play: rewards me, God reward him! If I do grow great,
notes: great] WHITE (ed. 1859): The folio reading, great again, has been rejected by all modern editors, unwarrantably and injuriously; for such a word [as again] could not come into the text by accident; and it has value as one of several indications that Falstaff is a decayed man of family, one whose vices and follies, aided by his humour, have dragged him from the position to which he was born and bred.

188.play: I'll grow less; for I'll purge, and leave sack, and

189.play: live cleanly as a nobleman should do.

1.play: [The trumpets sound. Enter KING HENRY IV, PRINCE HENRY, LORD JOHN LANCASTER, EARL OF WESTMORELAND, with WORCESTER and VERNON prisoners]

2.play: KING HENRY IV: Thus ever did rebellion find rebuke.

3.play: Ill-spirited Worcester! did not we send grace,

4.play: Pardon and terms of love to all of you?

5.play: And wouldst thou turn our offers contrary?

6.play: Misuse the tenor of thy kinsman's trust?

7.play: Three knights upon our party slain to-day,

8.play: A noble earl and many a creature else

10.play: If like a Christian thou hadst truly borne

12.play: EARL OF WORCESTER: What I have done my safety urged me to;

13.play: And I embrace this fortune patiently,

14.play: Since not to be avoided it falls on me.
notes: avoided] ABBOTT (Sh. Gram., 1870, p. 358): Ed following d or t is often not written; and, when written, often not pronounced. [Cf. Abbott’s note on V, i, 72.]

15.play: KING HENRY IV: Bear Worcester to the death and Vernon too:
notes: AX (Rel. of Sh. to Hol., 1912): Holinshed (III, 26) says that “the Earl of Worcester, the setter forth of all this mischief, was taken prisoner,” and that “he, the Baron of Kinderton, and Sir Richard Vernon were condemned and beheaded the following Monday. The earl’s head was sent to London, there to be set on the bridge”

16.play: Other offenders we will pause upon.

19.play: PRINCE HENRY: The noble Scot, Lord Douglas, when he saw

20.play: The fortune of the day quite turn'd from him,

21.play: The noble Percy slain, and all his men

22.play: Upon the foot of fear, fled with the rest;

23.play: And falling from a hill, he was so bruised

24.play: That the pursuers took him. At my tent

25.play: The Douglas is; and I beseech your grace

27.play: KING HENRY IV: With all my heart.

28.play: PRINCE HENRY: Then, brother John of Lancaster, to you

notes: COWL (ed. 1914) quotes Moorman as referring to Daniel’s Civile Warres (1595), iv, 56, as the source of the phraseology of this line. [But the passage cited appears for the first time in the 1609 quarto of the Civil Wars.—ED.]

30.play: Go to the Douglas, and deliver him

31.play: Up to his pleasure, ransomless and free:

32.play: His valour shown upon our crests to-day

33.play: Hath taught us how to cherish such high deeds
notes: taught] MALONE (ed. 1790) reads shewn for taught, and comments: Thus the quarto of 1598. In that of 1599 shewn was arbitrarily changed to taught. . . . The repetition is much in our author’s manner.—CAMB EDD. (1864): Capell's copy of Q1 [in Trin. Coll., Camb.] has taught, and this is the reading of Malone’s own copy, now in the Bodleian Library. Malone’s error is due to his following Capell's note, in which shewn is said to be the reading of Qq and Ff in line 30, instead of in line 29.—COWL (ed. 1914): There is, I believe, no copy of Q1 (1598) in the Bodleian. In Malone’s copy of Q2 (1599), which is in the Bodleian, there is a marginal collation of variants from Q1, and there shown is not recorded.

34.play: Even in the bosom of our adversaries.

35.play: LANCASTER: I thank your grace for this high courtesy,

36.play: Which I shall give away immediately.

37.play: KING HENRY IV: Then this remains, that we divide our power.
notes: HARTLEY COLERIDGE (Essays, 1851, ii, 156): If we are to consider the "first part" as an entire play, King Henry's closing speech offends sadly against Aristotle, in a point where Aristotle's authority, sound in itself, has a jurisdiction general and not limited to the Greek stage It is a conclusion in which nothing is concluded. . . . Had the last speech been omitted, the fault would have been less palpable, for the tragic interest of the play rests solely upon Hotspur, and closes with his death.

38.play: You, son John, and my cousin Westmoreland

39.play: Towards York shall bend you with your dearest speed,

40.play: To meet Northumberland and the prelate Scroop,

41.play: Who, as we hear, are busily in arms:

42.play: Myself and you, son Harry, will towards Wales,

43.play: To fight with Glendower and the Earl of March.

44.play: Rebellion in this land shall lose his sway,

45.play: Meeting the cheque of such another day:

46.play: And since this business so fair is done,

47.play: Let us not leave till all our own be won.
